fiches.title: Dribble Tag Dribble Tag
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Set up cones in the corners of the room so the children know the boundaries.  Spread 
children out throughout the room and give each child a basketball. Designate one child to 
start as the chaser.
1.	
On ‘go’ the chaser will run around the room chasing the other children.
2.	 The only way the children can move about the room is to dribble a basketball.  They may stop 
and rest when needed, but must dribble to move.
3.	 If the chaser tags another child, they must stop where they get tagged and free themselves by 
dribbling in their spot 20 times.  Once they complete that task, they are free to join the game 
again.
4.	 Rotate the chaser every few minutes.
4 cones, 1 basketball 
for each child
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Younger children can dribble the ball, 
catch it, then dribble it again if they have 
difficulty with normal dribbling.
•	
Add multiple chasers. Decrease the size 
of the play area.
Make it harder
Dribble Tag
Dribble Tag
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Knock Out Knock Out
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Have the students make 1 line behind a foul line.  Give the first 2 children in line a ball.
1.	
On ‘go’ the first child in line shoots from the foul line with the objective of making a basket 
before the child in line behind them sinks a basket.
2.	 If the first child misses their foul shot, they can retrieve the ball and now shoot a layup as the 
next child must now shoot a foul shot, following the same pattern of shooting a layup if they 
miss their first shot.
3.	 If the initial child makes a basket first, they retrieve the ball and give it to the next child in line 
without a ball.  If they do not make the basket first, they are out.
4.	 The last child left wins.
 1 basketball hoop, 
1 basketball
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Shoot from a spot directly in front of the 
basket
•	
Move the first shot out past the foul line, 
to the corners, or behind the 3-point line
Make it harder
Knock Out
Knock Out
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Figure-8 Dribble Race Figure-8 Dribble Race
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Each 
team lines up in a relay race format, starting on the baseline.  The child at the start line holds 
a basketball.  Place 3 cones about 5 feet apart in front of each team.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid must dribble the ball between the cones in a figure-8 
format.
2.	 When they get to the last cone, they must go around it and come back to the start line in the 
same format using figure-8’s to dribble in and out of the cones.
3.	 When they get back to the start line, they must hand the ball off to the next child in line.
4.	 The next child must follow this same pattern.  The first team to finish wins the race.
 basketballs, cones
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Use 2 cones instead of 3 cones and allow 
them to use only one hand to dribble
•	
Use 4 or 5 cones and have them dribble 
with the right hand in one direction, but 
the left hand coming back
Make it harder

fiches.title: Over-Under Relay  Over-Under Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Set up the children in 2 relay race lines.  They should start at one end of the room with their 
backs facing the basket.  Each team will line up using half of the room (horizontally) as 
shown in the picture below.
1.	
On ‘go’ the first student in line must pass the basketball over their head to the next child in line, 
who takes the ball and now passes it between their legs to the student behind them.
2.	 Whenever they pass the ball to the next child, they must run to the end of their line as the ball 
will eventually be passed again to them.  This is the way their team travels towards the basket 
at the other end of the room.
3.	 If the ball drops while passing, the whole team must run back to start line and begin the 
process again.  Once the team gets close enough to the basket, one child will attempt to shoot 
the ball.  If they make the basket first, they win the race.  If they miss, the team must run back 
to the start line and begin again.  The first team to make a basket wins the race.
 2 basketballs
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Have the children perform this task 
going in a straight line from point A to 
point B.
•	
Create difficult shapes for the line to fol-
low going from point A to point B.  For 
example, their line must follow a pattern 
of a square instead of a simple straight 
line.
Make it harder
Over-Under Relay 
Over-Under Relay 
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Pass & Shoot Relay Pass & Shoot Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Split your kids up into 2 teams. Each team sets up in a line that stretches out from the center 
line to the baseline of the court. The kid at the center line holds a basketball.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid passes the ball to the next kid in line.  This pattern 
continues until the last kid in line gets the ball.
2.	 When the last kid gets the ball, they turn and shoot a layup.  When they make a shot or take 3 
shots (whichever happens first), the line rotates.
3.	 When they rotate, the child who just shot runs with the ball to the start line and all the other 
children move up one spot in line towards the basket.
4.	 The first team to have all children make a basket wins that race.
 basketballs, basketball 
hoop
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Lower the basket, or consider eliminat-
ing the shot and just make it a dribble 
relay
•	
Students must make it two baskets or 
take 6 shots, whichever happens first
Make it harder
Pass & Shoot Relay
Pass & Shoot Relay
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Musical Dribbling Musical Dribbling
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Spread out bowling pins all around your room.  Give each child in the class a basketball.  
Have music ready to play.
1.	
When the music begins, the children must start to dribble their basketball all around the room.
2.	 When the music stops, they must stop dribbling. Continue following this pattern.
3.	 If a child loses control of the ball while dribbling, they must stop and do 10 jumping jacks.
4.	 If a child accidentally knocks over a pin, they must pick it up but also do 10 jumping jacks.
 20 bowling pins, a 
basketball for each 
child, music

Equipment
Make it Easier
•

dominant hand.
•

the children can only dribble with their 
non-dominant hand.

fiches.title: Layup Race  Layup Race
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Divide your class into 2 even teams that form a line at each basket.  Give the first child in line 
a basketball.
1.	
On ‘go’ the first child in line shoots a layup.
2.	 Whether they make it or miss it, they retrieve the ball, hand it to the next child in line, then go 
to the end of the line waiting for their turn to come again.
3.	 After 5 minutes, see which team made the most baskets. 
4.	 Mix up the students and proceed to do the contest again with new teams.
 2 basketball hoops, 
2 basketballs
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
If your baskets allow for it, lower them.
•	
Have players shoot from the left side 
instead of the right side.
Make it harder

fiches.title: Ace of Spades Ace of Spades
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Divide the class into 2 teams and place them in a single file line on both ends of your room, 
facing a basket.  Give the first child in each line a basketball.  The teacher will be in the 
middle of the room holding a regular deck of playing cards.
1.	
When ready, the teacher will shuffle a deck of cards and then show the class the first card in 
the deck.  A picture card (jack, queen or king) counts as a 10. 
2.	 When the first card is shown, the children on each team must make that number of baskets 
before the other team.  For example, if the number 6 card is drawn, both teams must try to 
make 6 baskets before the other team.
3.	 If the ace of spades is drawn, it’s a sudden-death competition – first basket wins.
4.	 As soon as the number is revealed, the first child shoots the ball at their basket.  Whether the 
shot is made or missed, the shooter must retrieve the ball and give it to the next child in line 
who follows the same pattern.  This continues until one team makes the shown number of 
baskets before the other team.  
5.	 The teacher will now draw another card to start the process over again.
 2 basketballs, 
1 deck of cards
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Lower the basket to 8 or 9 feet instead 
of the regular 10-foot height and allow 
the students to shoot the ball from a 
close range.
•	
Keep the basket at 10 feet and have the 
children shoot from further away.
Make it harder
Ace of Spades
Ace of Spades
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
97
Group Size
Group Size
Instructions
Instructions
ENDURANCE

fiches.title: 10-Second Buzzer Beater  10-Second Buzzer Beater
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Lay out 
bean bags all over your room.  Place 2 basketballs under the hoop at one end of your room.
1.	
Line the teams up single file behind a starting line.
2.	 On ‘go’ the first child from each team must pick up 10 bean bags.  Each bean bag counts as 
a ‘clock’ second to simulate a 10-second countdown.  They can hold all 10 bean bags in their 
hand or the teacher can give them a bag to place them in.
3.	 Once they collect all 10 bags, they can drop the bags and run to the hoop.  They now have 10 
seconds to make a buzzer beater basket. 
4.	 If they score, they get a point. After the 10 seconds elapse for each kid, replace the bean bags 
and start again with the next 2 kids.
40 bean bags, 
2 basketballs, 1 hoop
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Use smaller or lighter balls to shoot at 
the basket and lower the height of the 
basket.
•	
Use a regulation basketball to shoot as 
well as keeping the basket at the 10-foot 
height.
Make it harder
10-Second Buzzer Beater 
10-Second Buzzer Beater 
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
98

Group Size
Instructions
Instructions
ENDURANCE
GROSS MOTOR

fiches.title: Touchdown Relay Touchdown Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Set up each team in a traditional relay race line.  Place 2 cones near the opposite end of the 
room, mark the start line with floor tape and give the first child in each line a football.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid in line must run with the football to the cone across from 
them.
2.	 Once they get to the cone, they must run around it and come back to the start line.
3.	 As they get back to the start line, they must hand off the football to the next child who follows 
the same process.
4.	 The first team to finish the race wins.
 2 footballs, 2 cones, 
floor tape
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Decrease the distance the children must 
run
•	
Increase the distance the children must 
run or ask the children to pass the ball 
back to the next child in line from a 
specific distance

fiches.title: Pigskin Pass Pigskin Pass
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Place the students in 2 traditional relay race lines with each line facing their hula hoop filled 
with 10 footballs along with a ‘throwing circle’ on floor.
 
1.	
On ‘go’ the first child in line must run to the pile of footballs, pick up 1 ball and toss it from the 
throwing circle to the next child in line.  If the ball is caught, the catcher must place the ball in 
the empty hoop and then run to the pile to start this process again as the first child goes back 
to the end of the line.
2.	 If the ball is missed, the child must pick up the missed ball and run with it to the throwing circle 
and attempt to throw it to the next child in line.
3.	 The first team to finish and empty all the footballs from their hula hoop wins.
10-20 footballs, 4 hula 
hoops, floor tape.
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Use fewer balls and shorten the throwing 
distance.
•	
Use more balls and lengthen the 
throwing distance.
Make it harder
Pigskin Pass
Pigskin Pass
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 101

Group Size
Instructions
Instructions

fiches.title: Hoop Football Hoop Football
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Place one empty milk crate or ball storage container behind each end line as shown in 
image.  Divide the class into 2 teams with a middle line separating the children.
1.	
On ‘go’ the children will toss footballs across the room towards the other team’s milk crate, 
being careful not to cross the middle line.
2.	 Start with no guards in front of the milk crates.
3.	 After a child throws their ball, they should look for a new ball somewhere on their half of the 
room, not going onto the other team’s side.
4.	 The first team to get 2 balls into the opposing team’s box wins.
2 milk crates, 
20 sponge footballs (or 
other sponge balls)
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Have no guards and play until the first 
ball goes into the box.
•	
Allow a guard for the milk crate and 
have them get 3 footballs into the milk 
crate to win.
Make it harder

Hoop Football
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 102

fiches.title: Throw & Go Relay Throw & Go Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Use floor tape to mark the start line.  Set up cones on the far end of the room and balance a 
soccer-sized ball on top of each cone. 
1.	
On ‘go’ the first child in line must throw their football across the room at the cone with the ball 
balanced on top.
2.	 Their objective is to knock the ball off the cone.
3.	 Once they throw the football, they must retrieve it and bring it back to the next child in line.  If 
they hit the ball or cone, it is their job to put it back to the correct position.  Each time a ball 
gets knocked off they receive a point.
4.	 The first team to 10 points wins the race.
2 cones, 2 sponge balls, 
2 footballs, floor tape
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Shorten the distance the children have 
to throw and decrease the points to win 
from 10 to 5
•	
Increase the distance the children have 
to throw the ball. Use a smaller target 
ball.
Make it harder
Throw & Go Relay
Throw & Go Relay
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 103

fiches.title: All-Pro Quarterback All-Pro Quarterback
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams as a relay race or have the kids compete against each other 
individually. Place 2 hula hoops against a wall opposite from the children.  Mark the floor 
with tape to designate where the children must throw from and give the first child in each 
line a football.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid in line must throw their football towards their hula hoop.  
Their objective is to throw the football into the hula hoop.
2.	 After they throw the ball, they must retrieve it and bring it back to the next child in line who 
will attempt the same process.
3.	 Award 2 points each time a child throws the ball into the hoop and only 1 point if they hit the 
hoop.
4.	 If a child hits a hoop, it may fall over, so the teacher may have to stand it up again.  It is 
recommended to securely tape the hoop to the wall.
2 footballs, hula hoops, 
floor tape

Equipment

•	
Shorten the distance the children have 
to throw the ball
•

to throw the ball

fiches.title: Punt Returner Punt Returner
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Place the 
punters in lines at one end of the play area and the returners at the other.  Give the first kid 
in each line a football.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid in line must punt the football to the returner at the 
opposite end of the play area. 
2.	 If the returner catches it, they run full speed back to the line and hand the ball off to the next 
kid. 
3.	 The punter runs to the opposite end and becomes the new returner.
4.	 Even if the returner does not catch the punt, they still return to the back of the line and the 
punter becomes the next returner.
5.	 Each catch is worth 1 point. Continue until a team has 5 points.
 2 footballs, cones
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Shorten the distance between the punter 
and returner
•	
Increase the distance between the 
punter and returner. Place kick instead of 
punt.
Make it harder

© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 105

sections.Setup:
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Place the
punters in lines at one end of the play area and the returners at the other.  Give the first kid
in each line a football.
1.
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid in line must punt the football to the returner at the
opposite end of the play area.
2.	 If the returner catches it, they run full speed back to the line and hand the ball off to the next
kid.
3.	 The punter runs to the opposite end and becomes the new returner.
4.	 Even if the returner does not catch the punt, they still return to the back of the line and the
punter becomes the next returner.
5.	 Each catch is worth 1 point. Continue until a team has 5 points.
2 footballs, cones

•
Shorten the distance between the punter
and returner
•
Increase the distance between the
punter and returner. Place kick instead of
punt.

fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Use floor 
tape to mark the start and finish lines.  Set up cones in your room in no particular order (as 
seen in image below).  Place the children in traditional relay race lines. 
1.	
On ‘go’ the first child in line must dribble their soccer ball to the opposite end line, weaving in 
and out of the cones.
2.	 Once they get to the opposite end line, they must turn around and come back to the start line.
3.	 Once back at start line, they give the ball to the next child who performs the same task  The 
first team to finish wins the race.
 25 cones, 2 soccer 
balls, floor tape

race

fiches.title: Soccer Shot Relay Soccer Shot Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Set up 
each team in a traditional relay race line.  Place 2 cones as a goal across the room on the 
other end line.  Give the first child in line a soccer ball and use floor tape to mark off a 
shooting line.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid in line must kick a soccer ball across the room at a goal 
which is 2 cones.
2.	 Whether they make it in or not, they must run after the ball, pick it up and bring it back to the 
next child in line.
3.	 If the ball goes directly into the goal, they will also receive a point.
4.	 Once the next child in line gets the ball, they follow the same process.
5.	 The first team to 15 points wins.
 2 soccer balls, 2 cones 
(or soccer goal), floor 
tape
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Keep the distance the children shoot 
closer so the shot is easier
•	
Move the goals further away and raise 
the point total to win to 21 instead of 15
Make it harder
Soccer Shot Relay
Soccer Shot Relay
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 108

fiches.title: Soccer Pass Relay Soccer Pass Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Mark off the floor with tape, giving each child a space in their line.  Give the first child in line 
a soccer ball.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid in line must kick their soccer ball to the next child in line.
2.	 That next child must trap the ball, then proceed to kick it to the next child in line.
3.	 The pattern continues until the last child in line gets the ball.
4.	 Once the last child gets the ball, they start the process going in the reverse direction.
5.	 The first team to get the ball back to the first child wins the race.
2 soccer balls, floor 
tape
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Decrease the distance between players
•	
Use your non-dominant foot only
Make it harder

fiches.title: Speed Dribble Relay Speed Dribble Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Each 
team lines up in a relay race format.  Set up 2 cones on the opposite end of the room and 
place tape on the floor to designate where the children start from.  Give the first child in 
each line a soccer ball.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid must speed dribble the soccer ball to their cone and go 
around it.
2.	 Once they go around it, they must speed dribble all the way back to the start line.
3.	 Once they get the ball back to the start line, the next child follows the same process.  The ball 
cannot be passed back to the next child.
4.	 The first team to have everyone complete the tasks wins the race.
 2 soccer balls, 2 cones, 
floor tape
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Make the distance shorter that the chil-
dren need to dribble
•	
Have each child go 2 times instead of 1 
time as well as increase the distance of 
the race
Make it harder
Speed Dribble Relay
Speed Dribble Relay

Instructions

fiches.title: Tornadoes and Volcanoes Tornadoes and Volcanoes
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Set up a 20x30m space and fill it with disc cones, half of them upside down (Tornadoes) 
and half right side up (Volcanoes). Split your group into 4 teams and line up each team in a 
corner. The first child in each line has a soccer ball. 
1.	
Choose two teams to be volcanoes and two teams to be tornadoes. The object of the game is 
for the kids to flip as many cones as they can to match their team name. 
2.	 On ‘go’ the first child in line dribbles into the playing area and finds the closest cone. They 
stop, trap the ball, then flip the cone over to a volcano or tornado.
3.	 They then dribble back, exchange the ball, and the next person in line repeats
4.	 Play for 2-3 minutes. The team with the most cones matching their team name wins.
 20-30 disc cones
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Use a smaller space with fewer disc 
cones to flip. Reduce the teams from 
four to two.
•	
Have players use their weak foot (usually 
left foot) only. Have a larger space with 
more disc cones to flip.
Make it harder
Tornadoes and Volcanoes
Tornadoes and Volcanoes
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
111
Group Size
Group Size
Instructions
Instructions
ENDURANCE
GROSS MOTOR
Fine Motor

fiches.title: Pin Soccer Pin Soccer
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Give each child a bowling pin and have them place it somewhere in the room.  Provide 
almost double the amount of sponge soccer balls for the game.  So if you have 10 kids 
playing, have about 20 balls.
1.	
Each child is a guard for their pin as well as a shooter hitting other kids’ pins.
2.	 On ‘go’ kids will kick a ball at other children’s pins.  At the same time, they are also protecting 
their pin.  They can use any ball they find not being used.
3.	 If a student’s pin gets knocked over, that child is out of the round.  The last child left is the 
winner.  It is a continuous game until the last child is left.
4.	 Kids may leave the pin they are guarding at their own risk, as their pin may get knocked over, 
forcing them out of the game.  No hands can be used.
 Enough pins for each 
child, 20 sponge soccer 
balls
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Play the game and allow the children to 
have partners. This way one child is the 
shooter while the other is the goalie.
•	
Children have to play solo with no part-
ners helping them.
Make it harder
Pin Soccer
Pin Soccer
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 112
Group Size
Group Size
Instructions
Instructions
ENDURANCE
GROSS MOTOR

fiches.title: Figure-8 Dribble Relay Figure-8 Dribble Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Each 
team lines up in a relay race format, starting on the baseline. Place 4 cones about 5 feet 
apart in front of each team.  The first child in line should have a soccer ball.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid must dribble their soccer ball, weaving in and out of the 
cones in front of them like a figure-8.
2.	 When they get to the last cone, they must go around it and come back to the start line the 
same way, weaving in and out of cones.
3.	 When they get back to start, the ball is given to the next child in line who now takes their turn.
4.	 The first team to finish wins the race.
 2 soccer balls, 8 cones
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Use fewer cones
•	
Use more cones and increase the dis-
tance
Make it harder
Figure-8 Dribble Relay
Figure-8 Dribble Relay
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 113

fiches.title: Volley Tag  Volley Tag
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Place a cone in each corner for the boundaries.  Spread the kids around the room, give each 
a balloon.  Pick one child to be the chaser with a different color balloon.
1.	
In this game, students can move anywhere within the boundaries.
2.	 On ‘go’ the children will travel around the room by tapping their balloon into the air, including 
the chaser.
3.	 The chaser has a different color balloon.  The chaser is making an attempt to travel around the 
room and tag other children.
4.	 When a child gets tagged, they need to do 20 jumping jacks to return to game.
 4 cones, a balloon for 
each child
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Only use one chaser in the game and use 
bigger balloons filled to the max with 
air to allow more time in the air when 
tapped.
•	
Use 2 chasers in the game to increase 
the level of difficulty.
Make it harder
Volley Tag 
Volley Tag 
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 115

Group Size

Instructions

fiches.title: Serve a Strike  Serve a Strike
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Place the 
children in 2 traditional relay race lines.  Give the first child in each line a volleyball.  Place a 
bowling pin on the opposite end of the room.  Use a line on the floor as the serving line.
1.	
On ‘go’ the first child in line will serve the volleyball overhand or underhand at their bowling 
pin on the opposite end of the room with the intent of knocking over their pin to receive a 
point.  Hitting an opponent’s pin by accident will give the other team a point.
2.	 After the ball is hit, the child must chase the ball, pick up their pin if needed, and return the ball 
to the next child in line who follows the same process.
3.	 After 10 minutes, whichever team knocked over their pin the most wins.
 2 bowling pins, 
2 volleyballs
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Move the serving line closer to the pin.
•	
Move the serving line further from the 
pin.
Make it harder
Serve a Strike 
Serve a Strike 
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 116

fiches.title: Catch & Throw  Catch & Throw
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
Set up an even number of children on each side of the net. If you do not have a net, you can 
improvise using tall cones.  One team will start off with a volleyball.
1.	
On ‘go’ the team with the ball must pass the ball 3 times to each other on their half of the 
room.  On the last catch, that child must now throw the ball over the net onto the other side.
2.	 The other side now follows the same pattern.
3.	 The ball must always stay within the boundary lines.
4.	 If a team misses a ball, the other team is awarded a point.  If a team fails to get the ball over 
the next, again, the other team receives a point.
 Volleyball net, 
volleyball
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Allow the children to make as many 
throws on their side as they would like 
before throwing it over the net.
•	
Put a 5 to 7 second time limit once a 
team receives a ball to get the ball back 
on the other side of the net.
Make it harder
Catch & Throw 
Catch & Throw 
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 117

Group Size
Instructions
Instructions

fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Each 
team lines up in a relay race format, starting on the baseline. Place a milk crate or ball 
storage container opposite each team and give the first child in each line a volleyball.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid in line must serve the ball at their milk crate.
2.	 Whether they make it or miss it, they must retrieve the ball and run it back to the next child in 
line who will follow the same pattern.
3.	 Each child gets one attempt each time their turn occurs.
4.	 If they make it in their milk crate, they also receive a point.
5.	 The first team to 15 points wins the race.

fiches.title: Volley Pass Relay Volley Pass Relay
fiches.body:
focus
focus
Setup
Setup
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Each 
team sets up in a line that stretches out from one end line to the next end line.  The child at 
the start line holds a volleyball.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid must gently serve the ball to the next kid in line who 
catches it with their hands.
2.	 Once the next kid catches it, they turn and face the child in line behind them and now serve it 
to them, who catches the ball.
3.	 This process continues until the last child in line catches it.  When the last child catches it, the 
process continues in the reverse direction until it gets back to the first child in line.
4.	 Once the first child in line catches it again, the race is over and there is a winner.
 2 volleyballs, floor tape
Equipment
Equipment
Make it Easier
•	
Shorten the distance between kid
•	
Increase the distance between kids

sections.Setup:
You can do this in teams or have the kids compete against each other individually. Each
team sets up in a line that stretches out from one end line to the next end line.  The child at
the start line holds a volleyball.
1.
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid must gently serve the ball to the next kid in line who
catches it with their hands.
2.	 Once the next kid catches it, they turn and face the child in line behind them and now serve it
to them, who catches the ball.
3.	 This process continues until the last child in line catches it.  When the last child catches it, the
process continues in the reverse direction until it gets back to the first child in line.
4.	 Once the first child in line catches it again, the race is over and there is a winner.
2 volleyballs, floor tape

Setup
Setup
Each team lines up in a relay race format, starting on the baseline.  The child at the start line 
holds a volleyball.  Place a cone in the middle of the room in the team’s path.
1.	
On the instructor’s signal, the first kid must volley the ball while walking towards their cone, go 
around it and come back to the start line. Volleying means that the child must tap or bump the 
ball constantly as they walk.
2.	 Once they get back to the start line, they hand the ball off to the next child in line who follows 
the same process.
3.	 The first team to finish wins.
 2 volleyballs, 2 cones

fiches.title: INTRODUCTION
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
4
Sedentary lifestyles have been the increasing norm. Children are often busy with 
homework and spend time in front of screens. While they may get some physical 
activity at extra-curricular activities or sports, this is usually a small percentage of 
their day. 
With parents busy at work or with responsibilities at home, there is often little time 
remaining for physical activity or family time. As a result, many families are susceptible 
to risk factors for heart disease, obesity, high blood pressure, diabetes, or high 
cholesterol. 
It is important to make time each day for family and physical activity. Both are 
important for reducing stress and improving the quality of life. 
So what can families do to combat a sedentary lifestyle? First and foremost, make 
time each day to do some form of physical activity together!
This resource is designed as a 30 Day Family Fitness Challenge. Each day, we’ve 
provided a specific fitness or health-related task that the entire family can complete 
together. There’s a mix of fitness games, workouts, and nutritional challenges. All 
designed to help your family build healthy habits together.
You can complete the daily tasks as designed. Or, if you’d like some more flexibility, 
use these ideas to jumpstart your own health and fitness regimen.
Let’s get started!
INTRODUCTION

fiches.title: DAY 1:  COMMERCIAL-CIZE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
DAY 1: COMMERCIAL-CIZE 
SETUP: Create some open space in front of your family TV.
1.	 Pick a family friendly TV show you can all watch together.
2.	 Every time a commercial break comes on, you will get up off the couch and complete one of the 
Commercial-Cize routines below.
3.	 Your goal is to do one continuous exercise for each 30 second commercial. When a new 
commercial comes on, switch exercises and keep going!
4.	 Each time you complete a Commercial-Cize routine, check off one of the boxes below.
HOW IT WORKS
•	
To make it easier, do the exercise for 10 seconds, take a 5 second break and repeat.
•	
If there are more than 4 commercials, go back and repeat previous exercises.
•	
If you are watching a streaming show with no commercials, set a timer to take a commercial-cize 
break every 10 minutes.
•	
If you prefer to read books together, take an exercise break every 10 pages.
•	
Push-ups can be done with knees on the ground.

fiches.title: DAY 2:  FITNESS WAR
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
DAY 2: FITNESS WAR
SETUP: You’ll need a deck of cards and some open space to exercise 
(inside or outside).
1.	 Deal the full deck of cards out between the players. Each player keeps their cards in a stack face 
down.
2.	 This game is modeled after the old-fashioned card game “War.” On the command of go, each 
player flips over their top card and lays it down for the other players to see.
3.	 The player with the highest card wins the round and collects all the cards. The other players have 
to complete a fitness task according to the chart below.
4.	 The exercise and number of reps corresponds to the number shown on the winning players card. 
So for example, if the winning player had a 9 of hearts, the other players must all complete 9 
jumping jacks.
5.	 Keep playing until all the cards in the deck have been used. The winner is the player with the most 
collected cards.

DAY 3: EXERCISE BINGO
SETUP: Cut out the exercise tasks from the sheet below. Fold them in half and place 
inside a bowl. Cut out the Bingo cards on the following page. Find some empty 
space where your group can exercise.
1.	 Hand out one Bingo card and a pen to each player.
2.	 This game plays just like regular Bingo. The Bingo master will pull one exercise task out of the bowl 
and call it out. Any player who has that task on their Bingo card will complete the exercise, then 
mark it off on their card.
3.	 The first player to complete an entire row, column, or diagonal on their card wins.

fiches.title: DAY 4:  WEEKLY CARDIO CHALLENGE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
10
DAY 4: WEEKLY CARDIO CHALLENGE
SETUP: You’ll need a jump rope and some empty space to exercise. If you don’t 
have a jump rope, do “air” jumps by swinging an imaginary rope over your head.
Each week, we’ll do a quick cardio challenge to track our progress. Each person will do the exercises 
below and record the number of reps they are able to complete.
•	
Mountain Climbers: Start in a push-up position. While keeping your back flat, bring your right 
knee to your right elbow, then your left knee to your left elbow. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Squat Jumps: Bend your knees and drop into a squat position, then jump up and extend the hips 
until your body is straight. Land softly and repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Jumping Jacks: Jump while bringing your arms and legs out to the side. Jump again, bringing 
your arms and legs together. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Jump Rope: Jump in the air while swinging the rope over your head. Repeat for 1 minute.
HOW IT WORKS
NAME
1 MINUTE 
MOUNTAIN 
CLIMBERS
1 MINUTE 
SQUAT JUMPS
1 MINUTE 
JUMPING JACKS
1 MINUTE 
JUMP ROPE

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
11
•	
Today, try creating a balanced breakfast and eating it with your family.
•	
Studies show that students who eat breakfast have better concentration, attention span and 
memory. 
•	
Kids and adults who eat breakfast are more likely to take in adequate amounts of iron, the nutrient 
that helps transport oxygen in the body to help you stay energized. 
A balanced breakfast has a high-quality carbohydrate (whole grain, fruit, vegetable, milk or yogurt) 
combined with a high-quality protein (meat, eggs, beans, milk or yogurt) and a bit of fat. Notice that milk 
and yogurt alone make a balanced breakfast! 
Here are some examples to inspire you:
HOW IT WORKS
DAY 5: CREATE A BALANCED BREAKFAST
WHAT WAS IN YOUR BALANCED BREAKFAST TODAY?
• 1 cup of milk 
  with an orange
• Toast with a 
  hard-boiled egg
• English muffin w/ turkey 
  sausage & cheese
• Greek yogurt with 
  berries & nuts
• Bagel w/ eggs & 
  a slice of cheese
• Waffle w/ turkey sausage 
  & side of berries

fiches.title: DAY 6:  DRIVEWAY HOPSCOTCH
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
DAY 6: DRIVEWAY HOPSCOTCH
SETUP: You’ll need beanbags (or pebbles), chalk and a safe outdoor area where you 
can draw a hopscotch court.
1.	 Using chalk, draw a hopscotch court on a driveway or sidewalk. Make sure you are set up in a safe 
area with no traffic. 
2.	 Label each square in your hopscotch court with a number.
3.	 The first player in line tosses their beanbag into the first square. 
4.	 They enter the court alternating between hopping on 1 foot and 2 feet, continue to the end, 
then turn around and come back. On the way past their square, they pick up their beanbag and 
continue back to the start line.
5.	 The next players repeat this pattern, tossing their beanbag into the second square, third square 
and so on. 
6.	 If a player is unable to throw their beanbag into the correct square, or they touch a line or lose 
their balance while hopping, they lose their turn and have to wait until the next round to go.
HOW IT WORKS

fiches.title: DAY 7:  WEEKLY STRENGTH CHALLENGE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
13
DAY 7: WEEKLY STRENGTH CHALLENGE
SETUP: You’ll need a wall, and some empty space to exercise.
Each week, we’ll do a quick cardio challenge to track our progress. Each person will complete the 
exercises below and record their scores.
•	
Push-ups: Get down on all fours, placing your hands wider than your shoulders. Straighten your 
arms, back and legs. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the floor, then push yourself 
back up. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Lunges: Take a big step forward with your right foot and lower your body until both knees are at a 
90-degree angle. Step back into your starting position and repeat with your left foot. Repeat for 1 
minute.
•	
Wall Sit: Stand with your back against a wall, then slowly lower yourself down until your thighs are 
parallel to the ground. Hold for as long as you can.
•	
Planks: Get in a push-up position but bend your arms so your weight is resting on your forearms. 
Tighten your abs and glutes to keep your body straight. Hold for as long as you can.
HOW IT WORKS
NAME
HOW MANY 
PUSH-UPS CAN YOU 
DO IN 1 MINUTE?
HOW MANY LUNGES 
CAN YOU DO IN 1 
MINUTE?
HOW LONG CAN YOU 
HOLD A WALL SIT?
HOW LONG CAN YOU 
HOLD A PLANK?

fiches.title: DAY 8:  BOTTLE BOWLING
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
14
DAY 8: BOTTLE BOWLING
SETUP: You’ll need a long hallway or room that’s clear of space, a tennis ball, 
and 10 empty plastic bottles
1.	 Set the bottles up in a traditional 10-pin bowling layout as shown below
2.	 Mark a line about 10-20 feet away from the bowling pins.
3.	 Take turns bowling at the pins and trying to knock them all down. Each player gets 2 rolls. When 
their turn is over, record their score, set the pins back up and repeat with the next player.
4.	 Keep score using the scorecard on the next page. Every pin knocked down counts for one point. 
5.	 If you bowl a strike (1 roll to knock down all 10 pins), you get 15 points. If you bowl a spare (2 rolls 
to knock down all 10 pins), you get 12 points.
6.	 Play 10 frames and add up the scores to determine the winner.
HOW IT WORKS
•	
Allow each player to choose their own size and type of ball (soccer ball, basketball, golf ball, 
beach ball).
•	
Set the balls up in different patterns.
•	
Play “crazy bowling” – players must roll the ball backwards between their legs or with their eyes 
closed.
•	
Play “pyramid bowling” by stacking bottles or plastic cups on top of each other.
•	
Play “splash bowling outside using water guns to knock down the pins instead of a ball. Time each 
player on how long it takes them to knock down all 10 pins.
•	
Make the game a bit harder by partially filling the bottles with sand or dried beans.
•	
Use pool noodles to create “bumpers” around each side of your bowling lane. This will help keep 
the ball straight and ensure more success.
VARIATIONS

4.	 Keep score using the scorecard on the next page. Every pin knocked down counts for one point.
5.	 If you bowl a strike (1 roll to knock down all 10 pins), you get 15 points. If you bowl a spare (2 rolls
to knock down all 10 pins), you get 12 points.
6.	 Play 10 frames and add up the scores to determine the winner.
HOW IT WORKS
•
Allow each player to choose their own size and type of ball (soccer ball, basketball, golf ball,
beach ball).
•
Set the balls up in different patterns.
•
Play “crazy bowling” – players must roll the ball backwards between their legs or with their eyes
closed.
•
Play “pyramid bowling” by stacking bottles or plastic cups on top of each other.
•
Play “splash bowling outside using water guns to knock down the pins instead of a ball. Time each
player on how long it takes them to knock down all 10 pins.
•
Make the game a bit harder by partially filling the bottles with sand or dried beans.
•
Use pool noodles to create “bumpers” around each side of your bowling lane. This will help keep
the ball straight and ensure more success.

fiches.title: DAY 9:  PARACHUTE GAMES
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
16
DAY 9: PARACHUTE GAMES
SETUP: A play parachute works best, but you can substitute in an extra large bed 
sheet or light weight blanket. You’ll also need 5-10 small balls or plush toys.
Round 1: Merry Go Round
Lay the sheet on the ground. On the count of 3, each player lifts up the sheet from their corner. 
Begin walking in a clockwise direction while rotating the sheet like a merry go round. Every few 
laps, change the movement. You can try hopping, skipping, going backwards or side shuffling.
Round 2: Parachute Popcorn
Lay the sheet on the ground and place a few small balls on it. On the count of 3, each player lifts 
up the sheet from their corner. Once lifted, started flipping the parachute up and down to make 
the balls jump and bounce like popcorn. Work together to keep the balls jumping and prevent 
them from falling off the parachute.
Round 3: Treasure Hunter
Put a few toys inside a small ”treasure box” and lay the sheet on top. Choose one player to be the 
Treasure Hunter. They will stand off to the side to start. On the count of 3, lift up the sheet and start 
making ”waves” by moving it up and down. When ready, call out the name of a specific “treasure” 
that the Treasure Hunter has to find. They must quickly crawl underneath the parachute, grab the 
item out of the treasure box and bring it back up to the “surface.” Rotate in a new Treasure Hunter 
and keep playing. 
Round 4: Parachute Catcher
Lay the sheet on the ground and place one of the balls on it. Choose one player to be the Catcher. 
They will stand about 10-15 feet away from the sheet to start. On the count of 3, each player lifts 
up the sheet from their corner. Working with your teammates, toss the ball high into the air towards 
the Catcher. If it’s too easy, try tossing it longer distances, or have multiple Catchers and multiple 
balls. Rotate in a new Catcher and keep playing.
HOW IT WORKS
Go through each of the following games and see which ones you can successfully do!

DAY 10: EXTREME ROCK PAPER SCISSORS
SETUP: Find some open space to play. No equipment needed.

fiches.title: DAY 11:  WEEKLY CARDIO CHALLENGE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
18
DAY 11: WEEKLY CARDIO CHALLENGE
SETUP: You’ll need a jump rope and some empty space to exercise. If you don’t 
have a jump rope, do “air” jumps by swinging an imaginary rope over your head.
Each week, we’ll do a quick cardio challenge to track our progress. Each person will do the exercises 
below and record the number of reps they are able to complete.
•	
Mountain Climbers: Start in a push-up position. While keeping your back flat, bring your right 
knee to your right elbow, then your left knee to your left elbow. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Squat Jumps: Bend your knees and drop into a squat position, then jump up and extend the hips 
until your body is straight. Land softly and repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Jumping Jacks: Jump while bringing your arms and legs out to the side. Jump again, bringing 
your arms and legs together. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Jump Rope: Jump in the air while swinging the rope over your head. Repeat for 1 minute.
How did you do vs Day 4?
HOW IT WORKS
NAME
1 MINUTE 
MOUNTAIN 
CLIMBERS
1 MINUTE

fiches.title: DAY 12:  MAKE YOUR OWN HEALTHY TRAIL MIX
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
19
•	
Finding healthy foods to eat quickly on the go can be a challenge. Trail mix can be a great option if 
it’s made with the right ingredients.
•	
Remember that nuts and seeds are healthy fats, but if you eat too many at a time, you lose your 
meal balance and can end up filling up without eating enough of the other nutrients. 
•	
A healthy trail mix should include a balance of carbohydrates, protein and some fats. Use the 
examples below to make your own blend using foods that you love.
HOW IT WORKS
DAY 12: MAKE YOUR OWN HEALTHY TRAIL MIX
WHAT WAS IN YOUR HEALTHY TRAIL MIX TODAY?
Dry cereal
Granola
Dried fruit
Pretzel pieces
Trail Mix Examples
Pick 1 or 2 foods from each of the categories below to make your own healthy trail mix!
CARBOHYDRATES
PROTEINS
FATS
Dried soybeans
Chopped beef jerky
Roasted edamame
Roasted chickpeas
Nuts
Seeds

fiches.title: DAY 16:  SHUTTLE SERVICE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
23
DAY 16: SHUTTLE SERVICE
SETUP: You’ll need a bucket, 2 cones, several tennis balls or bean bags and about 
30-40 feet of open space. This game is best for a large back yard or playing at the 
park.
1.	 Using cones, set up a track about 10-20 meters long. Place a bucket at the start line, and 3 tennis 
balls at the opposite end.
2.	 The first competitor begins at the start line. One other player will be timing them with their watch 
or phone.
3.	 On “go” – sprint to the opposite cone, grab one of the tennis balls, then sprint back and place it 
in the bucket. Continue until all 3 tennis balls are back in the bucket. Record their time using the 
chart below.
4.	 Set the balls back up and repeat with a new competitor.
HOW IT WORKS
COMPETITOR
NAME
ROUND 1
SPRINT
ROUND 2
BEAR CRAWL
ROUND 3
CRAB WALK
ROUND 4
BUNNY HOP
•	
You can play multiple rounds with a different movement pattern each time. Use the chart below 
for inspiration.
•	
You can increase or decrease the number of balls or change the length of the track to make the 
race longer or shorter.
VARIATIONS

park.
1.	 Using cones, set up a track about 10-20 meters long. Place a bucket at the start line, and 3 tennis

2.	 The first competitor begins at the start line. One other player will be timing them with their watch

3.	 On “go” – sprint to the opposite cone, grab one of the tennis balls, then sprint back and place it
in the bucket. Continue until all 3 tennis balls are back in the bucket. Record their time using the
chart below.
4.	 Set the balls back up and repeat with a new competitor.

fiches.title: DAY 17:  P.I.G.
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
24
DAY 17: P.I.G.
SETUP: You’ll need 1 basketball and a hoop
1.	 P.I.G. is a fun basketball game where one player has to mimic the shot of another player.
2.	 The first player can take any type of shot they want.  If they miss their shot, the ball goes to the 
next player, who takes their turn at creating a shot.
3.	 If they make their shot, the next person has to mimic the shot exactly and make the same basket.  
4.	 If they miss the mimicked shot, they receive the letter “P” as a consequence.  If they make the 
mimicked shot, the next player in line has to mimic the original shot as well.
5.	 If everyone makes the mimicked shot, and the ball comes back to the original shooter, the 
process starts again with that player creating a new shot.
6.	 Players who get all 3 letters (P.I.G.) have to do 25 jumping jacks. They can then return back to the 
game with no letters and keep playing.
HOW IT WORKS
Here are some ideas for fun shots to get your creative juices flowing:
Backwards shot
Hook shot
Bounce the ball into the hoop
Long range 3-pointer
Shooting with your non-dominant hand
Shoot while sitting in a chair
Shoot while lying on your back
Shoot from behind the backboard
Underhand (granny) shot
Bank shot (off the backboard)
Eyes closed shot
SHOT IDEAS

DAY 18: WEEKLY CARDIO CHALLENGE
SETUP: You’ll need a jump rope and some empty space to exercise. If you don’t 
have a jump rope, do “air” jumps by swinging an imaginary rope over your head.
Each week, we’ll do a quick cardio challenge to track our progress. Each person will do the exercises 
below and record the number of reps they are able to complete.
•	
Mountain Climbers: Start in a push-up position. While keeping your back flat, bring your right 
knee to your right elbow, then your left knee to your left elbow. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Squat Jumps: Bend your knees and drop into a squat position, then jump up and extend the hips 
until your body is straight. Land softly and repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Jumping Jacks: Jump while bringing your arms and legs out to the side. Jump again, bringing 
your arms and legs together. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Jump Rope: Jump in the air while swinging the rope over your head. Repeat for 1 minute.
How did you do vs Day 11?
HOW IT WORKS

fiches.title: DAY 19:  TRY A BRIGHT COLORED FRUIT OR VEGGIE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
26
•	
Fruits and veggies are packed full of vitamins, minerals, phytonutrients and fiber. 
•	
Adults tend to eat the foods that we were exposed to as children, which can limit food variety. It can 
take 5–6 times of trying a food before you realize you actually like it!
•	
The bright colors that give fruits and vegetables their beauty come from phytonutrients. These are 
compounds in the food that work to minimize inflammation and keep the inside of your body healthy. 
•	
There are hundreds of phytonutrients that exist. A few of the more common ones include beta-carotene, 
anthocyanins, lycopene and lutein. 
HOW IT WORKS
DAY 19: TRY A BRIGHT COLORED FRUIT OR VEGGIE

make up your own.
1.	 To begin, read out the first clue. The players will immediately start searching the house to try and 
find the fitness task card. 
2.	 When they find it, they will call all the players into their location, where everyone will complete 
that fitness task.
3.	 Continue playing until all the task cards have been found. The player who found the most task 
cards wins.

fiches.title: DAY 21:  WEEKLY STRENGTH CHALLENGE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
29
DAY 21: WEEKLY STRENGTH CHALLENGE
SETUP: You’ll need a wall, and some empty space to exercise.
Each week, we’ll do a quick cardio challenge to track our progress. Each person will complete the 
exercises below and record their scores.
•	
Push-ups: Get down on all fours, placing your hands wider than your shoulders. Straighten your 
arms, back and legs. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the floor, then push yourself 
back up. Repeat for 1 minute.
•	
Lunges: Take a big step forward with your right foot and lower your body until both knees are at a 
90-degree angle. Step back into your starting position and repeat with your left foot. Repeat for 1 
minute.
•	
Wall Sit: Stand with your back against a wall, then slowly lower yourself down until your thighs are 
parallel to the ground. Hold for as long as you can.
•	
Planks: Get in a push-up position but bend your arms so your weight is resting on your forearms. 
Tighten your abs and glutes to keep your body straight. Hold for as long as you can.
How did you do vs Day 14?

HOLD A WALL SIT?

HOLD A PLANK?

fiches.title: DAY 22:  AROUND THE WORLD
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
30
DAY 22: AROUND THE WORLD
SETUP: You’ll need 1 basketball, 5 cones, and a hoop to play on.
1.	 Using cones or chalk, mark 5 spots in a semi-circle 
around your basketball hoop. Recommended spots are 
shown in the diagram, but you can choose whatever 
locations you want. 
2.	 The object of the game is to make shots from each of the 
locations, traveling all the way “around the world” and 
back.
3.	 The first player shoots from the first spot. If they make 
the basket, they proceed to the second spot, where they 
shoot another shot. 
4.	 If they make that shot, they proceed to the third spot.
5.	 If they miss, they have the option to “risk it” or “pass.”
6.	 If they “risk it” – they get another chance to make the 
shot. If make the “risk it” shot, they proceed to the third 
spot and continue playing. If they miss the “risk it” shot, 
they lose their turn and have to start back at the first 
spot on their next turn.
7.	 If they “pass” – they lose their turn but maintain their 
position (at the second spot) on their next turn.
8.	 The game continues until one player has traveled all the 
way around the world and back.
HOW IT WORKS
For some extra fun, you can give each location a real-world name (continent, country or city).
Parents or older players can shoot from behind the marked spots while younger players can 
shoot from in front.
VARIATIONS

fiches.title: DAY 26:  STAY HYDRATED ALL DAY
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
35
DAY 26: STAY HYDRATED ALL DAY 
•	
Even if you feel like you drink a lot of water, staying hydrated when you are at school all day and 
then at activities in the evening can be a challenge. 
•	
Creating a hydration schedule can help get you in the habit of drinking at regular times 
throughout the day.
Here are some basic guidelines for how much water you need. The more active you are, the more 
you should drink!
•	
Kids 4-8 years old – 5-6 cups per day
•	
Kids 9-13 years old – 7-8 cups per day
•	
Kids 14-18 years old – 8-11 cups per day
•	
Men 19 and older – 13 cups per day
•	
Women 19 and older – 9 cups per day
On days you exercise, you should add another cup of water before, during and after.
Here’s a sample hydration schedule you can try.
•	
6:30 am (as soon as you wake up) — 1 cup water
•	
7:30 am (as you leave for school or on the way to school) — 1 cup water
•	
9:30 am (in school) — 1 cup (8 gulps) from the school drinking fountain
•	
11:30 (Lunch at school) — 1 cup water
•	
1:30 (in school)- 1 cup (8 gulps) from the school drinking fountain
•	
3:00-3:30 (after school) — 1-2 cups water
•	
30 minutes before exercise — 1 cup water
•	
During exercise — sip 1 cup water throughout 
•	
After exercise — 1 cup water
•	
With evening meal — 1-2 cups water
•	
2 hours later — 1 cup water
HOW IT WORKS
HOW MANY CUPS OF WATER DID YOU DRINK TODAY?

fiches.title: DAY 27:  NINJA WARRIOR COURSE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

DAY 27: NINJA WARRIOR COURSE 
1.	 Set up a “Ninja Warrior” style obstacle course in your back yard or at the park. 
2.	 Create 5 different “stations” throughout the course. At each station, a different fitness skill must be 
performed.
3.	 Time each competitor and see who can finish the course fastest!
Here are some ideas for different types of fitness skills and obstacles you can set up
Strength exercises: push-ups, sit-ups, squats, lunges
Endurance exercises: jumping jacks, star jumps, squat jumps, mountain climbers
Use different movements to go from station to station: sprint, backpedal, crab walk, bear crawl
Hop over or crawl under pool noodles balanced on cones
Hop through hula hoops
Jump over kiddie pool
Walk across balance beam
Three-legged race
Potato sack race
Agility ladder
Jump rope
Score basket or a goal
Dribble a basketball or soccer ball through cones
Crawl through inflatable tunnel
Slide across slip n’ slide
Jump over hurdles
Dig through sand for treasure
HOW IT WORKS
SETUP: You’ll need some empty space to play, cones, and whatever equipment you 
decide to use in your obstacle course.

© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

DAY 29: WILD ANIMAL RACE
SETUP: Cut out the animal movement cards below. Fold them in half and place into a 
bowl. Using cones, set up a track that’s about 10-20 meters long.
1.	 In this game, you will be imitating different types of animal movements!
2.	 The first player will take one of the animal movement cards out of the bowl and open it up. All 
players will then race down to the cone and back, doing their best impression of that animal.
3.	 Repeat until you have used up all 9 animal movement cards
4.	 You can also play Animal Charades and have the other players guess what animal you are 
imitating!
HOW IT WORKS
Snake Slither
Penguin Waddle
Cheetah Run
Chicken Flap
Crab Walk
Bull Charge
Leap Frog
Turkey Trot
Bunny Hop

fiches.title: DAY 30:  FLEXIBILITY CIRCLE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
40
DAY 30: FLEXIBILITY CIRCLE
SETUP: Cut out the flexibility cards below. Fold them in half and place into a bowl. 
Put some calming music on and have your whole group sit in a loose circle with the 
bowl in the middle.
1.	 The first person will pull one of the flexibility cards out of the bowl then walk into the middle of 
the circle. They will then teach the rest of the group how to do that stretch.
2.	 Hold each stretch for 20-30 seconds.
3.	 Take turns pulling out flexibility cards and leading new stretches. 
4.	 Continue until all 9 stretches have been completed
HOW IT WORKS
Standing Toe Touch
Sitting V-Stretch
Hamstring Stretch
Sitting Neck Stretch
Triceps Stretch
Half Split Stretch
Quad Stretch
Butterfly Stretch
Triangle Stretch

fiches.title: BONUS CHALLENGE: SLEEP 8-10 HOURS TONIGHT
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
41
DAY
BONUS CHALLENGE:
SLEEP 8-10 HOURS TONIGHT
1.	 Many people assume that the body recovers immediately after exercise, but that’s not the case! 
Sleep is your body’s time to really recover. 
2.	 As you fall into a deep sleep, your body becomes more relaxed, slows down and heals. If you give 
your body the rest and time to recover that it deserves, it will work better for you during the hours 
you are awake.
3.	 Depriving your body of sleep can decrease your ability to focus, learn, and listen, which can have 
a negative effect on your performance in the classroom and activities.
Tips to Get a Better Night’s Sleep
It can be a challenge to find extra time in your day to rest & recover (sleep), but remember, this 
should be a priority. The following are some tips to help you fall asleep faster:
•	
Try to go to sleep at the same time every night, or as many nights as possible, even on weekends.
•	
Do not wait until the last minute to do your homework. Look for opportunities during the day so 
that you are not stuck staying up late to study.
•	
Turn off all electronics and leave them out of your bedroom.
•	
Avoid things that could disrupt your sleep, such as energy drinks or caffeinated beverages.
Try tracking your sleep using the chart below
HOW IT WORKS
BEDTIME
WAKE UP TIME
HOURS OF SLEEP

fiches.title: Each lesson plan is broken down into 7 sections:
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
3
Welcome to 52 PE Lesson Plans! This exciting resource was developed to help you deliver a top-
notch PE experience that will maximize engagement and participation with your kids.
The lessons are organized into the 12 months of the year. Each one corresponds to a holiday 
or seasonal event. Some are common holidays (like Christmas and Halloween) and some are less 
known (like Random Acts of Kindness Day).
Each lesson plan is broken down into 7 sections:
1.
Introduction: The theme of the lesson plan and how it relates to physical education.
2. Focus: The components of fitness or skill areas that the activity focuses on:
3. Group Size: The minimum number of participants required for the lesson.
4. Equipment: Required sports equipment or instructor materials.
5. Warmup Activity: A short activity to engage the kids and get their bodies moving.
6. Main Activity: The primary activity for the lesson plan. Includes setup, instructions, and
modifications to make the game easier or harder for different ages.
7.
Standards: How the lesson conforms to the SHAPE America Standards Level Outcomes for
K-12 Physical Education.
You can treat the lessons as a complete curriculum and follow the month by month structure. Or 
pick and choose individual activities and use them however you like!
Let’s get started!
TEAMWORK
COGNITIVE
GROSS MOTOR
FINE MOTOR
STRENGTH
ENDURANCE

fiches.title: January
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
January
FOCUS
•
Paper
•
Balloons filled with air
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
New Year’s Day always falls on January 1st! On this day, we celebrate a new year and 
put the old year behind us. In this lesson, we will look for the numbers 2-0-2-4 to win a 
race and bounce new year’s confetti.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
CONFETTI BOUNCE
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Spread the kids out around the room.  Give 
them 1 balloon for every 2 kids.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the kids will try to juggle New Year’s confetti 
around the room by tapping all the balloons into the 
air.
•
They should see how long they can bounce the 
balloons into the air and go until there is only one 
balloon left.
•
Once a balloon falls to the ground, the kids should 
focus on the balloons in the air.
•
Once completed, regroup and start again.
Easier: Use balloons that are larger.
Harder: Use beach balls instead of balloons.
NEW YEAR’S NUMBER RACE
Time: 10-15 minutes
Setup: Place the students in relay race lines. Place about
25 pieces of paper at the other end of the room. On the
back of each paper, write the individual numbers of the 
year (for example 2, 0, 2, 4) evenly on all the papers and 
place them face down.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the first student in line must run to the pile of
papers with the objective of finding the number 2.
•
If they find it, they must bring it back to their line and
place it on the side of their line.
•
If they do not find the 2, they return the paper face
down and run back to the start line.
•
Then, the next kid in line follows the same pattern.
•
Their objective is to find the numbers representing
the year (for example 2, 0, 2, 4) in sequential order.
Variation: Change the locomotor (hopping, skipping, 
bear crawl, etc.)
Easier: The kids can find the numbers in any order. 
Harder: The kids must find the numbers in order.
STANDARDS
•
Performs locomotor skills (hopping, galloping, running, sliding, skipping) while maintaining balance. (S1.E1.K)
•
Works cooperatively with others. (S4.E4.3a)
New Year’s Day

fiches.title: National Bird Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
FOCUS
•
10 sponge balls
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
National Bird Day celebrates birds and ways to protect them.  In this lesson, we will use 
some fun PE games to get kids flapping their wings to simulate flying, as well as feeding the 
birds.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
FEED THE BIRDS
Time: 10-15 minutes
Setup: Place the class on two teams, one on each side of 
the room.  Give each team 5 sponge balls to start.  Each 
kid is a bird, the balls are the bird food.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the birds on each team have to underhand
toss their balls (food) onto the other team’s half of the
room.
•
Their objective is to get all 10 balls on the other
team’s half of the room at the same time.
•
The team that can get all 10 balls onto the other
teams side first wins the round.
•
Kids cannot kick or throw the ball overhand.
Easier: Use fewer balls. 
Harder: Use more balls.
BIRD CATCHER
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Spread the students out all around you room.  Pick 
four kids to be the bird catchers, while the rest of the 
kids are birds.  Each bird catcher has a corner of the room 
which is their nest area.  
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the bird catchers must run around the room
trying to tag the birds.
•
The birds must avoid being tagged.
•
If a bird catcher tags a bird, that bird must go to the
corner of the room which is their designated nest
area.
•
At the end of the game, the last bird left wins, and
the bird catcher with the most birds in their nest also
wins.
Easier: Play with only 2 bird catchers.
Harder: Play the game with 4 bird catchers.
STANDARDS
•
Throws underhand with opposite foot forward. (S1.E13.K)
•
Performs locomotor skills (hopping, galloping, running, sliding, skipping) while maintaining balance. (S1.E1.K)

fiches.title: Opposite Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
FOCUS
•
20 large sponge balls
•
2 bowling pins
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
Opposite Day celebrates doing silly things and not meaning what you say!  In this 
lesson, we some fun games that may confuse you a little.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
OPPOSITE DAY SIMON SAYS
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Spread all the kids out facing the teacher at the 
front of the room.
Instructions:
•
This game plays just like Simon Says, except kids must 
do the OPPOSITE of what you say.
•
For example, if you say “Simon Says wave your right 
hand,” then they must wave their left hand.
•
If you say “Simon Says hop on your right foot” they 
must hop on their left foot.
•
Play until only one kid is left, then start again.
Variation: Give each kid a ball, scarf or other object and 
have them throw, catch, or dribble according to your 
commands.
Easier: Make every command a “Simon Says.”
Harder: Mix in some ”non-Simon Says” commands to see 
if you can trick the kids.
BACKWARDS BOWLING
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Divide the class into 2 teams, and place them on 
each side of your room.  Give each team an even amount 
of balls and stand up a bowling pin on each team’s end 
line.
Instructions:
•
The object of the game is to knock down the other
team’s bowling pin.
•
The catch is that the ball can ONLY be thrown
backwards through your legs (like hiking a football).
•
No student can cross the middle line to the other side.
•
The first team to knock over the opposing team’s pin
first wins the round.
Variation: Add more pins on each team’s side. 
STANDARDS
•
Recognizes the concept of open spaces in a movement context. (S2.E1.3)
•
Performs a sequence of locomotor skills, transitioning from one skill to another smoothly and without hesitation. (S1.
E6.3)

fiches.title: National Puzzle Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
8
FOCUS
•
Floor tape
•
5 red bean bags
•
5 blue bean bags
•
2 large exercise mats
•
1 large cone
•
1 tennis ball
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
National Puzzle Day is a fun day to use your brain and figure out various puzzles. In this 
lesson, we will focus on activities that will make you think and figure things out.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
TIC-TAC-TOE….TOSS
Time: 10-15 minutes
Setup: Place the children into traditional relay race lines.  
Use tape or hula hoops to create a large tic-tac-toe 
board on the floor.  Give each child a bean bag, but 
each team must have one color bean bag to represent 
their side.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the first child on team A will toss their bean 
bag at the tic-tac-toe board.
•
Once completed, the first child on team B will now 
toss their bean bag.
•
After each kid tosses their bag, they go to the end of 
the line allowing the next child to go.
•
The first team to get three of their bean bags in a row 
wins that round.
Easier: Shorten the distance they must throw the bean 
bag.
Harder: Increase the distance they must throw the bean 
bag.
GUESS WHO?
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Place the students on 2 teams, each team on one half 
of the room.  Mark off the ground with 3 lines as shown in the 
image.  Place a cone in the middle of the room with a tennis 
ball balanced on top.  Stand up a gym mat on each end of 
the room for a child to hide behind.
Instructions:
•
Each team will hide behind their mat and pick one student
to be their secret student who will permanently hide behind
the mat when the game starts.
•
On ‘go’, the rest of the team will run out to their line in front
of their mat (except for the secret kid).
•
Both teams will stare at the other team’s line with the
objective of figuring out who is the missing kid.
•
If a player on the opposing team knows the secret kid, they
run out to the cone and try to grab the ball first.
•
If they get the answer right, they receive a point, if they
guess the wrong kid, the opposing team gets a point.
•
The first team to 10 points wins the round.
Easier: Play with fewer kids per team.
Harder: Play with more students per team.
STANDARDS
•
Uses communication skills and strategies that promote team or group dynamics.50 (S4.H3.L1)
•
Solves problems and thinks critically in physical activity and/or dance settings, both as an individual and in groups. (S4.
H4.L1)

#

fiches.title: Groundhog Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
9
February
FOCUS
•
Hula Hoops
•
Music
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
Groundhog Day is celebrated on February 2nd.  As the story goes - if a groundhog emerges from 
its hole and sees its shadow, it will go back underground, and winter will last for 6 more weeks. 
If it does not see its shadow due to cloudiness, spring will arrive early! In this fun lesson, we’ll do 
two different Groundhog Day related activities.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
GROUNDHOG TAG
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Spread the kids out around the room.  Place 
hula hoops (groundhog holes) around the room. 
Assign 1-2 kids as the “gardeners” and the rest as 
“groundhogs.”
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the gardeners will start running after and
attempting to tag the groundhogs.
•
The groundhogs can be tagged anywhere in
the play area, except for when they are standing
inside a hula hoop (groundhog hole).
•
If a groundhog gets caught outside a hula hoop,
they must do 10 jumping jacks before re-entering
the game.
•
Groundhogs can only stay in a groundhog hole
for 5 seconds.
•
The gardeners cannot guard a groundhog hole.
Easier: Use more hula hoops.
Harder: Use fewer hula hoops.
GROUNDHOG TUNNEL
Time: 7-10 minutes
Setup: Place all the children in a straight line, 
standing with their feet shoulder width apart. The 
kids will crawl like a groundhog through a tunnel.
Instructions:
•
Once the kids are in a straight line, they must 
spread their feet apart because classmates will 
crawl between their legs like a groundhog.
•
When ready, the first kid in line will lay on the 
floor and crawl between everyone’s legs in line.
•
When they get to the end, they stand up as the 
next student in line follows the same pattern.
•
Have the students go from point A to point B 
and time them to see if they can get a better 
time each time they go.
Easier: Shorten the distance from point A to point B.
Harder: Increase the distance from point A to 
point B.
STANDARDS
•
Works cooperatively with others. (S4.E4.3a)
•
Performs locomotor skills (hopping, galloping, running, sliding, skipping) while maintaining balance. (S1.E1.K)
Groundhog Day

next student in line follows the same pattern.
•
Have the students go from point A to point B
and time them to see if they can get a better
time each time they go.
Easier: Shorten the distance from point A to point B.
Harder: Increase the distance from point A to
point B.
STANDARDS
•
Works cooperatively with others. (S4.E4.3a)
•
Performs locomotor skills (hopping, galloping, running, sliding, skipping) while maintaining balance. (S1.E1.K)
Groundhog Day

fiches.title: National Pizza Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
10
FOCUS
•
2 hula hoops
•
100 bean bags
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
National Pizza Day is a celebration of everything related to pizza.  In this lesson, we will have 
some fun delivering pizza and adding pepperoni to our slices.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
PIZZA DELIVERY
Time: 10-12 minutes
Setup: Spread the students out around the room 
and pick 2 kids to be the chasers (pizza deliverers). 
Place several hula hoops (pizzas) around the room.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the pizza deliverers are running around
the room delivering pizza to the other kids.
•
While they are running, they must use their feet
to kick the hula hoops (the pizza) at the other
kids in the room.
•
The kids who are not pizza deliverers can run
around freely all over the room.
•
If a pizza deliverer can kick the hoop at another
child and hit them with it, that child must now
do 10 jumping jacks before they can return to the
game.
•
After a few minutes, pause the game and assign
new pizza deliverers.
Easier: Kids should do 5 jumping jacks when hit with 
the hoop.
Harder: Kids should do 15 jumping jacks when hit 
with the hoop.
PEPPERONI PIZZA
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Divide your class into 4 teams.  Place a hula 
hoop in the middle of the room and secure it to the 
ground with tape.  Mark with tape a corner of each 
room for each team.  Give each team 25 bean bags of 
the same color to designate each team.
Instructions:
•
In this game, the hula hoop is the pizza and the
bean bags are the pepperoni.
•
On ‘go’ each team will begin to slide their bean
bags on the floor with the objective of landing in
the hula hoop.
•
If a bag ends up in the hoop, leave it there, but
kids can leave their section at any time to pick up
pepperoni that did not make it into the hoop and
bring it back to their corner.
•
The first team to land all their pepperoni on the
pizza pie (hula hoop) win that round.
Easier: Use a large hula hoop and shorten the 
distance to throw the bean bags.
Harder: Use a smaller hula hoop and lengthen the 
distance to throw the bean bags.
STANDARDS
•
Works cooperatively with others. (S4.E4.3a)
•
Throws underhand with the opposite foot forward. (S1.E13.K)
National Pizza Day

fiches.title: Valentine’s Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
11
FOCUS
•
1 red ball or bean bag
•
20 red paper hearts
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
Valentine’s Day is February 14th! It’s an annual festival to celebrate love and friendship with our 
family and friends. In this lesson, we will deliver holiday cards and pick up some hearts with an 
exercise task on it.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
PASS THE LOVE
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Put the students in small groups of 6-8 kids.  
Give them a red ball to symbolize a heart.
Instructions:
•
When ready, the student who starts off with the
ball calls out another kid’s name in their group
and gently passes them the ball.
•
That child catches the ball and will call out a new
kid’s name and pass them the ball.
•
The students should follow this pattern and see
how many passes they can make without the ball
dropping.
•
Once it becomes too easy, add another ball or
two to make it more challenging.
Easier: Use only one ball for the activity.
Harder: Add extra balls and spread out further for 
longer throws.
BE MY VALENTINE
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Place the children in traditional relay race 
lines.  Mark off with tape a start line and another line 
to place red paper hearts.  On one side of each heart, 
write a different exercise challenge.  For example, 10 
push-ups, or 25 jumping jacks. Place the hearts face 
down.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the first student in each line must run to
the hearts on the other end of the room.
•
Once there, they pick up one heart, read the
exercise listed on it, place the heart back on the
ground face down, and perform that exercise.
•
Once completed, they run back to the line and
the next child proceeds to follow the same
pattern.
•
The first team to go through the whole line wins
the race.
Easier: Easier exercises, fewer reps
Harder: Entire team must complete each exercise 
challenge.
STANDARDS
•
Demonstrates cooperation skills by establishing rules and guidelines for resolving conflicts. (S4.M4.7)
•
Performs a sequence of locomotor skills, transitioning from one skill to another smoothly and without
hesitation. (S1.E6.3)
Valentine’s Day

fiches.title: Random Acts Of Kindness Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
FOCUS
•
None
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
Random Acts Of Kindness Day is a day when we do kind things for others to show them our 
appreciation and spread positive vibes. In this lesson, we will partner up to exercise with a friend, 
and play a game of “Positive” Simon Says. 
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
PUSH-UP HIGH FIVE
Time: 5-10 minutes
Setup: Partner up students in groups of two.  Each 
group must face each other in a push-up position.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the partners will begin to perform push-
ups.
•
They must attempt to high-five each other each 
time they reach the ‘up’ position.
•
Each time they high-five, they must say 
something nice about the other person.
Easier: Kids can do the push-ups on their knees.
Harder: Kids can do the push-ups with their legs 
extended so they are not on their knees.
POSITIVE SIMON SAYS
Time: 7-10 minutes
Setup: Pick one child to be ‘Positive Simon.’  Spread 
the rest of the kids around the room.
Instructions:
•
This is a game of Simon Says with all positive 
challenges.
•
When the caller in front of the room says,
“Positive Simon Says” all the children must 
perform what was said.  If the caller does not 
state a challenge starting with Positive Simon 
Says, the children should not do it.
•
Samples of commands could be:
a. Walk over to 4 classmates and high-five them
b. Go over to 5 students and compliment them.
c. Scream out something you are thankful for.
d. Shake hands with 2 other students.
Easier: Positive Simon Says should pick easy 
challenges.
Harder: Positive Simon Says should pick more 
difficult challenges with an exercise tied into it.  For 
example, high five 2 kids then perform 2 push-ups.
STANDARDS
•
Designs and implements a strength and conditioning program that develops balance in opposing muscle 
groups (agonist/antagonist) and supports a healthy, active lifestyle. (S3.H7.L2)
•
Uses communication skills and strategies that promote a team or group dynamics. (S4.H3.L1)
Random Acts Of Kindness Day 
Positive 
Simon
