Give each child a bean bag (their vote).  Place a milk crate
or bucket on the opposite side of the room.  Tape a start
line on the floor.
sections.Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the first child in line must run to the voter’s
box (milk crate) and cast their vote by tossing their
bean bag into the milk crate.
•
One the bean bag is in the milk crate, they run back
to the start line and the next child follows the same
pattern.
•
The first team to finish wins the round.
sections.Variation:
galloping, running, sliding, and skipping.
Easier: Shorten the distance to the milk crate.
Harder: Give each kid 2 bean bags and have them go
twice.
STANDARDS
•
Runs with a mature pattern. (S1.E2.2a)
•
Performs locomotor skills (hopping, galloping, running, sliding, skipping) while maintaining balance. (S1.E1.K)
Election Day

fiches.title: World Kindness Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
48
FOCUS
•
Music
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
November 13th is World Kindness Day. On this day, we celebrate everything related to being 
kind to others.  In this lesson, we will play some fun PE games that revolve around kindness 
and great character.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
KINDNESS KID
Time: 5-7 minutes
Setup: Spread the students out around the room 
and pick one child to be the leader who stands up 
front of the class who becomes the ‘kindness kid’.
Instructions:
1.
This game is like Simon Says except it is replaced
with “kindness kid says” followed by a command.
2.
If the leader does not say “kindness kid says”
before a command, any child who follows the
command is out.
3.
Commands can be things like high five
a classmate, shake hands with someone,
compliment a friend, fist bump someone, smile
at someone, wave at someone, and so on.
Variation:  The leader can throw in some fitness skills 
along with the kindness commands.  For example, 
“kindness kid says do 2 push-ups.”
MUSICAL HIGH FIVES
Time: 5-7 minutes
Setup: Spread the students out around the room.  
Have music ready to play – something that features 
the theme of friendship, happiness and kindness.
Instructions:
1.
When the teacher puts on music, the students
must skip around the room.
2.
Each time they pass a classmate, they must give
them a gentle high five.
3.
When the music stops, the kids must freeze in
their spot like a statue.
4.
Once the music starts again, they must skip
around the room again gently high fiving their
classmates.
5.
The teacher can substitute other motor skills for
the student to do instead of skipping such as 
galloping, karaoke, hopping, or jogging.
STANDARDS
•
Follows instruction and directions when prompted. (S4.E3.K)
•
Gives corrective feedback respectfully to peers. (S4.E3.5)
World Kindness Day

fiches.title: National Take A Hike Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
49
FOCUS
•
Boxes
•
Hula hoops
•
Pins
•
Sponge balls
•
Gym mats
•
Tunnels
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
November 17th is National Take A Hike Day! This is a day where families are encouraged 
to get outside and hike while enjoying the beautiful scenery and getting fit. In this lesson, we 
will perform some activities that will require various motor skills.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
GYM HIKE
Time: 7-10 minutes
Setup: Set up a walking track in your room or outside, but 
put obstacles in the path for the students to hike through. 
You can use hula hoops, pins, boxes, gym mats, tunnels, 
or any other equipment you have access to.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the children will begin hiking in the room
moving in one direction.
•
If they encounter an obstacle, they will need to crawl
through it, jump over it, or walk between it.  A box
could represent a mountain, pins could represent a
small river that you have to maneuver through, or a
tunnel could be a cave you crawl through.
Easier: Place fewer obstacles in their path.
Harder: Place more obstacles in their path.
BEAR ENCOUNTER
Time: 20-30 minutes
Setup: Spread the kids out around the room and 
choose one student to be the bear who is on all fours.  
Leave many loose balls on the floor.  All the balls will be 
picnic baskets.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the bear will start to crawl around the room
picking up picnic baskets (balls).  If they pick up a
picnic basket, they must roll it at one of the other kids
who are moving about the room.
•
If they hit another student with the ball, the also
become a bear. As more and more kids get hit, more
kids will become a bear.
•
The last kid who is not a bear wins and starts off the
next round as a bear.
Easier: Use more balls in the game.
Harder: Use fewer balls in the game.
STANDARDS
•
Hops, gallops, jogs and slides using a mature pattern. (S1.E1.1)
•
Performs a sequence of locomotor skills, transitioning from one skill to another smoothly and without hesitation. (S1.
E6.3)
National Take A Hike Day

fiches.title: Thanksgiving
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
50
FOCUS
•
2 scarves per child or 
even clean tissues will 
work
•
Hula Hoops
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
Thanksgiving is a day when we give thanks for the things in life we may take for granted. 
In this lesson, we will have fun acting like turkeys and pilgrims.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
TURKEY TAG
Time: 10-15 minutes
Setup: Spread the students all around your room and 
pick one child to be your farmer.  The other kids are the 
turkeys who must tuck 2 scarves or tissues (feathers) into 
their pants but allow them to be visible on the outside for 
the chaser to grab.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the farmer will begin running around the room
towards the turkeys.
•
The farmer has the objective of pulling a feather
(scarf) from a turkey.
•
Once a turkey has both feathers pulled, they are
temporarily out of the game until the next round and
can now rest.
•
After a few minutes, pick a new chaser and begin a
fresh round.
Easier: Have only one chaser, give each turkey one scarf.
Harder: Have multiple chasers, give each turkey two 
scarves.
PILGRIM PLACE
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Spread the students out around the room. Give 
each child except for one a hula hoop. The kids are 
pilgrims migrating across the country. The hula hoops are 
towns where the pilgrims can rest for the night.
Instructions:
•
When ready, the teacher will play music while all the
kids in the class must skip around the room.
•
When the music goes off, all the children must get to
a hoop and stand in it.
•
The child without the hoop must do 2 ‘pilgrim push-
ups’ as the teacher starts the process again.
•
If 2 kids end up in the same hoop, they both must do
the ‘pilgrim push-ups.’
Easier: Play a few rounds with the same amount of hula 
hoops as children so nobody gets out. 
Harder: Play an elimination round where the child who is 
out is eliminated (no push-ups).  Then, take away 1 hula 
hoop as they get eliminated (like musical chairs).
STANDARDS
•
Leaps using a mature pattern. (S1.E1.3)
•
Performs jumping and landing actions with balance. (S1.E3.K)

fiches.title: Thanksgiving 2
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
51
FOCUS
•
None
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
Thanksgiving is a day when we give thanks for all the great things we have in life.   In 
this lesson, play some fun activities that revolve around the holiday of Thanksgiving.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
TURKEY TAG
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Spread the children out around the room 
and choose 1 child to be the chaser.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the students must run around the room 
as the chasers will chase all the other students.
•
If a student gets tagged by a chaser, they turn 
into a turkey.
•
As a turkey, they must squat in their spot and flap 
their arms like a turkey and make a loud gobble 
sound over and over for 10 seconds.
•
Once the 10 seconds is up, they can join back in 
the game again.
•
After a few minutes, rotate the kids so there are 
new chasers.
Variation:  Chose multiple students to be the chaser 
at one time and when a kid gets caught, have them 
act like a turkey for 30 seconds before entering back 
into the game.
MAYFLOWER TAG
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Spread the children out around the room and 
give each child a hula hoop.  The hula hoop is their 
Mayflower ship.  Pick one child to be the chaser.
Instructions:
1.
On ‘go’ the children will begin to travel around
the room, but the must always keep at least one
foot inside their hula hoop (Mayflower ship).
2.
The chaser will attempt to move their Mayflower
ship towards other students’ ships.
3.
If the chaser can successfully bump their hoop
into another student’s hoop, they caught the
student.
4.
The student who got caught must jump up and
down 10 times in their ship, and then they can
return to the game.
5.
After a few minutes, rotate a new student to be it.
Variation:  Have multiple students as chasers.
STANDARDS
•
Balances on different bases of support, combining levels and shapes. (S1.E7.2a)
•
Works cooperatively with others. (S4.E4.3a)
Thanksgiving 2

fiches.title: National Cookie Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
52
FOCUS
•
2 milk crates
•
50 fluff balls
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
National Cookie Day simply celebrates cookies as something yummy to eat as well as 
giving them to friends right before the holiday season. In this lesson, we will aim cookies 
into cookie jars as well as stack up cookies.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
THAT’S HOW THE COOKIE CRUMBLES
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Partner up students in groups of 2 or 3.  Give each 
group about 25 bean bags (cookies).  Make sure the kids 
are on a solid floor.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the groups of students will have to stack up
bean bags (cookies) as high as they can.
•
If the cookies fall and crumble, the group must start
all over again.
•
Cookies must be piled one on top of another.
•
After 2 minutes, whichever group stacked their
cookies highest, wins.
Easier: Use fewer bean bags and give them more time.
Harder: Give them more bean bags and less time.
FILL THE COOKIE JAR
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Place a milk crate (cookie jar) on both sides at 
the far end of the room.  Spread out fluff balls (cookies) 
all around the room with a dividing line in the middle to 
separate both teams.  Divide the class in half with each 
team on their side of the room.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ all the students will begin tossing their
cookies across the room with the objective of getting
a cookie into the other team’s cookie jar.
•
At no time can kids cross the middle line.
•
Each team is allowed to have one goalie, and the rest
of the kids are throwing their cookies across the room.
•
After 5 minutes, see which team got the most cookies
into the cookie jar, then start another round.
Variation: Play this game using a basketball hoop as your 
”cookie jars.”
Easier: Shorten the distance to the milk crate.
STANDARDS
•
Combines traveling with the manipulative skills of dribbling, throwing, catching, and striking in teacher and/or student-
designed small-sided practice task environments. (S1.E26.4)
•
Works cooperatively with others. (S4.E4.3a)

fiches.title: Day Of The Ninja
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
53
FOCUS
•	
Pool noodles for each student
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
December 5th is the day of the Ninja. On this day, we celebrate our love for all things 
Ninja, practicing our punches and kicks to get some exercise.  In this lesson, we will have a 
make-believe sword fight and practice our Ninja moves.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
NINJA SWORD FIGHT
Time: 10-15 minutes
Setup: Partner up kids and give each kid a pool noodle.  
Students must not travel around the room too much and 
stay in one section of the room.
Instructions:
•
When the teacher says ‘go’ the children will begin to
sword fight with the pool noodles.
•
Their objective is to tap their partner on the foot or
ankle.
•
Each time they accomplish this, they get a point.
•
The first student to 5 points wins, then they switch to
a new partner.
Easier: Play each round to 3 points.
Harder: Play each round to 7 points.
NINJA TAG
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Spread the students out all around your room.  
Pick two kids to be the chasers.  One chaser is the 
punching Ninja, the other chaser is the kicking Ninja.  
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the Ninjas will chase the other kids around the 
room.  The objective of the Ninjas is to tag other 
children who are avoiding being tagged.
•
If the punching Ninja tags a player, that player must 
perform 5 air punches with each arm.
•
If the kicking Ninja tags a player, they must do 5 air 
kicks with each leg.
•
Once they complete the tasks, they return back to the 
game.  After a few minutes, change the taggers.
Easier: Have the kids perform 2 air punches or kicks when 
tagged.
Harder: Have the kids perform 7 air punches or kicks 
when tagged.
STANDARDS
•
Performs locomotor skills (hopping, galloping, running, sliding, skipping) while maintaining balance. (S1.E1.K)
•
Participates in a variety of aerobic fitness activities such as cardio-kick, step aerobics and aerobic dance. (S3.M3.6)
Day Of The Ninja

fiches.title: National Twin Day
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
54
FOCUS
•	
50 paper plates
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
National Twin Day is December 18th! This is a day that celebrates siblings who enjoy the 
unique connection of sharing a birthday.  In this lesson, we’ll play some fun games inspired 
by twins!
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
MIRROR ME
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Spread the students out around your room and 
partner them up with another classmate.  Have music 
playing in the background.
Instructions:
•	
Within each group, one student is the leader, the 
other is the twin in the mirror who follows various 
movements.
•	
When ready, the leader will start to perform various 
movements. The twin must mimic every movement.
•	
After a few minutes, they switch roles.
•	
Have the kids repeat the process with a new partner.
Variation: Add a soccer ball or basketball and have the 
leader do moves using the ball.
TWIN PICTURES
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Provide the class with 50 paper plates and spread 
them out in a marked-off section of the room faced down. 
Write numbers on the bottom of each plate, making sure 
there are two of every number (from 1-25).  Numbers are 
facing down.  Place the kids in traditional relay race lines.
Instructions:
•	
On ‘go’ the first student in line must run to the pile 
and pick up 2 plates.
•	
If the plates have matching numbers, the child brings 
the plates back to their home base and the next child 
in line goes.  
•	
If the numbers do not match, they put the plates back 
the way they found them and return to start.
•	
In the end, the team with the most plates wins.
Easier: Use fewer plates.
Variations: Change. the locomotor. Have the kids hop, 
crawl, or crab walk to the plate area.
STANDARDS
•	
Recognizes the concept of open spaces in a movement context. (S2.E1.3)
•	
Performs a sequence of locomotor skills, transitioning from one skill to another smoothly and without hesitation. (S1.
E6.3)
National Twin Day

fiches.title: Christmas
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
55
FOCUS
•	
2 scooters
•	
Cones
•	
100 bean bags
•	
2 milk crates
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
Christmas is on December 25!  It is a day where kids love getting holiday presents and 
spend valuable time with family. In this lesson, we will make believe we are collecting presents 
and riding Santa’s sled.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
SANTA’S SLED
Time: 5-10 minutes
Setup: Place the children into traditional relay race lines.  
Each team will have one scooter (sled) and cones on the 
opposite end of their line.  Use tape to create a start line.
Instructions:
•	
On ‘go’ the first student in line must rest on their 
team’s scooter (Santa’s Sled) belly down and push 
themselves around their cone and back to the start 
line.
•	
Once they get back, the next child in line follows the 
same pattern.
•	
The first team to finish wins the race.
Easier: Shorten the distance they have to scoot.
Harder: Increase the distance they have to scoot.
I WANT MORE PRESENTS!
Time: 15-20 minutes
Setup: Place the students on 2 teams, each team on one 
half of the room.  Place exactly 50 bean bags (presents) 
on each half of the room.  Place a milk crate on each half 
of the room for the kids to drop off their presents.  Pick 
one student on each team to be the guard.
Instructions:
•	
On ‘go’ kids start to run onto the other team’s half of 
the room to steal extra presents (bean bags).
•	
They must pick up a bean bag and run it back to their 
side and place it in their milk crate.
•	
When a student runs onto the other team’s side, the 
opponent’s guard may try to tag a player.  If they get 
tagged, they must drop any bean bag, go back to 
their home side of the gym, and do 20 jumping jacks.
•	
Once they complete the jumping jacks, they come 
back in.
•	
At the end of the game, the team with the most 
presents (bean bags) wins the round.
Easier: Play with no guards 
Harder: Play with more guards and more bean bags.
STANDARDS
•	
Performs a sequence of locomotor skills, transitioning from one skill to another smoothly and without hesitation. (S1.
E6.3)
Christmas

sections.Equipment:
GROUP SIZE
Christmas is on December 25!  It is a day where kids love getting holiday presents and
spend valuable time with family. In this lesson, we will make believe we are collecting presents
and riding Santa’s sled.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
SANTA’S SLED
Time: 5-10 minutes

on each half of the room.  Place a milk crate on each half
of the room for the kids to drop off their presents.  Pick
one student on each team to be the guard.

opponent’s guard may try to tag a player.  If they get
tagged, they must drop any bean bag, go back to
their home side of the gym, and do 20 jumping jacks.
•
Once they complete the jumping jacks, they come
back in.
•
At the end of the game, the team with the most
presents (bean bags) wins the round.
Easier: Play with no guards
Harder: Play with more guards and more bean bags.
STANDARDS
•
Performs a sequence of locomotor skills, transitioning from one skill to another smoothly and without hesitation. (S1.
E6.3)
Christmas

fiches.title: Christmas 2
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
56
FOCUS
•
1 bowling pin for each
student
•
25-40 medium size kick balls
EQUIPMENT
GROUP SIZE
December 25th is Christmas Day. In this lesson, we’ll play some fun games that revolve 
around this beloved holiday.
WARMUP ACTIVITY
MAIN ACTIVITY
REINDEER TAG
Time: 8-10 minutes
Setup: Spread the children out around the room and 
choose 1 child to be the chaser.  All the children are 
reindeer, the chaser is an elf.
Instructions:
•
On ‘go’ the students must run around the room
as the chasers will pursue all the other students.
•
If a student gets tagged by a chaser, they
become frozen with their hands in the air and
their feet spread apart.
•
To get unfrozen, a child who is not tagged must
run and crawl between the legs of a frozen
student to release them back into the game.
•
After a few minutes, rotate the chaser to a new
student.
Variation:  Choose multiple students to be the 
chaser at one time.
SNOWMAN TIP OVER
Time: 12-14 minutes
Setup: Spread the children out around the room 
and give each child a hula hoop.  Stand up a white 
bowling pin (snowman) in each hula hoop.
Instructions:
1.
On ‘go’ the children will begin to underhand roll 
balls at the other kids’ snowmen (pins).
2.
At the same time, each child must guard their 
own snowman inside their hula hoop.
3.
When they guard, they cannot step into their 
own hula hoop, and must always stand around 
the edge of their hoop protecting their snowman.
4.
If a ball comes near them, they may pick it up
and roll it at another kid’s snowman.  They can
leave their hoop area if needed to pick up a loose
ball near their hoop.
5.
If their pin gets knocked over, they can stand it
up and continue playing.
6.
Students should keep track of how many times
their pin gets knocked down.  The student with
the lowest score at the end wins the round.
STANDARDS
•
Throws underhand with opposite foot forward. (S1.E13.K)
•
Performs a sequence of locomotor skills, transitioning from one skill to another smoothly and without
hesitation. (S1.E6.3)
Christmas 2

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Base Games! 
Inside this resource, you’ll get 13 fun activities you can use with Kindergarten through 8th Grade. These are designed to 
build skills for any type of bat, ball and base game - including baseball, softball, tee-ball, kickball and cricket. Each 
activity is explained with simple instructions, equipment lists, diagrams and modifications. 
Skill Focus 
Each activity will have a skill focus, focused mostly on striking, throwing, catching and fielding. 
Group Size 
All of these activities are designed to work with smaller or larger groups. A group size is provided, but this is just the 
minimum number of students you will need for the game to work. For larger groups, you can increase the number of 
players in the game, or create multiple stations with smaller groups. 
Complexity 
Each activity is rated on a Complexity scale indicating how difficult the skills are to perform. 
  Green - recommended for Kindergarten and up (or with modifications for older children) 
  Yellow - recommended for 3rd grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
  Red - recommended for 5th grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
Modifications 
We’ve also included modifications to make the games easier or harder, for different age levels. 
Ask Students 
Some games also include review questions you can ask your students - allowing you to reinforce key coaching points, 
rules or skills.

fiches.title: Four Corners
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown 
• 4 fielders and 4 batters 
• The aim is for batters to all strike at the same time and run around their designated tall 
orange Cone 
• Batters score a run for each time they run around their Cone and back to their hitting tee 
• Fielders aim to stop the ball and return it to the tee, if they do so before the batter returns 
then no run is scored for that batter 
• Batters have 3 swings each and calculate their runs. Add up the totals of all 4 batters to 
determine a team score 
• Switch roles after 3 swings
Where should you aim to strike 
the ball to score maximum runs? 
As a fielder, how can you prevent 
runs?
8
Bats 
Balls 
Cones
Four Corners
Make fielding space smaller, so 
there is less running 
Add another fielder to each of 
the 4 sides (8 fielders in total) 
Students can strike a smaller or 
large ball 
Change the striking implement 
Add a target on the floor for 
bonus point e.g a tall Cone
Striking
Fielding
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Parallel
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown 
• The pair of batters aim to hit into the field of play at the same time 
• If a hit is successful, they turn around and run around a numbered Cone of their choice.  
• Batters score that many points if they successfully reach it back to their tee before the 
fielders can return the ball to the tee 
• If a batter does not make it back in time, no points are awarded.  
• Catches = 3 points off 
• Each batting team adds up their points after 3 swings and another pair become batters 
• Rotate until all players have batted 
• Winning team is the team that scored the most points
Where should you aim to strike 
the ball to score maximum runs? 
As a fielder, how can you work 
with your team to prevent runs?
Gameplay
Passing and moving

hoop placed in the middle of the 
fielding area (a designated 
student can field here and act as 
the ‘catcher’). Students must 
throw or roll the fielded ball to the 
‘catcher’

fiches.title: Race the Base
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown 
• In each group, one participant begins as the batter and the other participants are the 
fielders. The batter hits a ball off the tee and attempts to run to the base and back to the 
tee as many times as possible before one of the fielders places the ball back on top of the 
tee.  
• The batter is out if the ball is caught before it hits the ground or when the ball is returned to 
the tee and the batter is between the tee and the other base. 
• 1 point for each time the batter returns successfully to the tee 
• Switch turns after 3 at bats 
Where did you decide to place 
the ball for maximum runs? 
As a fielder, how can you ready 
and position your body to 
successfully field a ball?
4-6
Bats 
Balls 
Cones

distance between the base and 
the tee 
Student chooses which 
implement they want to strike 
with 
Students chooses object they 
want to strike e.g teeball, 
volleyball, soft skinned gator ball 
Pass the ball to all fielders before 
returning the ball 
Striking
Fielding

fiches.title: Team Race the Base
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown 
• In each group their is a fielding team and a batting team 
• Batters aim to strike the ball into the field of play, if successful the whole team follows the 
batter around the bases, batters score 1 point for every base the team touches 
• Meanwhile fielders stop the ball, then form a line behind the fielder who stooped the ball 
and pass the ball under, over, under, over 
• Once the last fielder in line has the ball he/she runs to the front of the line and yells ‘stop’ 
• The batting team stops running and calculates their points 
• Each student in the batting team has a bat 
• Once everyone has had a bat, teams switch roles 
Where did you decide to place 
the ball for maximum runs? 
As a fielder, how can you ready 
and position your body to 
successfully field a ball?

fiches.title: Rapid Fire
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown 
• 4 batters and 4 fielders 
• Batters aim is to hit each ball on one of the 3 tees in rapid succession, when the last ball is hit 
the drop the base and run around the bases as quickly as possible 
• The fielding team has to collect the balls and throw them to the ‘catcher’ who is positioned 
near the central bucket.  
• Once all 3 balls are in the bucket the fielding team yells stop and the batter stops.  
• If the batter is between bases they had back to the last base they touched and wait for the 
next batter to hit all 3 balls before running 
• If the batter makes it all the way around to the home base, 1 run is scored 
• Switch after everyone has had a turn at bat 
How can you work together to 
field the ball quickly and get it to 
the hoop? 
Why is accurate throwing 
between fielders important?
8

implement e.g kicking a ball or 
using a bat

teams total  or 3 runs to the 
fielding teams total. Not both

fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown 
• 1 batter and 4 fielders 
• The batter aims to hit the ball in any direction and run around any base and back to the tee 
• The fielding team must field the ball, place a foot on the closest base and then throw to 
another fielder at any other base. They yell ‘ stop’ once done 
• If the batter make it back before they score 1 run 
• If the fielding team beats the batter no run is scored.  
• Each student has 3 at bats and then rotates so everyone gets a bat. Student with the 
highest score wins 
How do you decide when to 
keep running and when to stay at 
the tee

do to avoid being ‘caught’ out?

• Setup multiple playing areas as shown
• 1 batter and 4 fielders
• The batter aims to hit the ball in any direction and run around any base and back to the tee
• The fielding team must field the ball, place a foot on the closest base and then throw to
another fielder at any other base. They yell ‘ stop’ once done
• If the batter make it back before they score 1 run
• If the fielding team beats the batter no run is scored.
• Each student has 3 at bats and then rotates so everyone gets a bat. Student with the
highest score wins

keep running and when to stay at
the tee

do to avoid being ‘caught’ out?

fiches.title: Longball
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup playing area as shown. Place a line at each end as the ‘crossing lines’  
• Divide group into batting teams and fielding teams 
• The aim is for the batting team to strike a pitched ball out into the playing area and then run 
to the back base 
• Fielding team aims to get the batter out  by: Catching the ball, tagging the batter when 
running to the back base, throwing (underarm)and hitting batter below the knees when 
running to the back base 
• Fielding team can also tag and throw at a runner heading back to the home base 
• Batting team scores a run if they successfully make it back to the batting base 
• Once a runner crosses the ‘crossing line’ they cannot head back to the back base and 
must run to the home base.  
• Switch after each person in the batting team has had a bat  
Where did you decide to place 
the ball for maximum runs? 
How can you decide when to run 
for home base?

fiches.title: Quick Kicks
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup playing area as shown. Place a line at each end as the bases for the kicking team 
• Split into 2 teams. One kicking team and one fielding team 
• The game begins with one student from the kicking team kicking a ball to into the field of play. 
Every player from the kicking team then attempts to run to the ‘safe zone’ at the opposite end 
without being tagged with the ball 
• Fielders try and collect the ball and tag a student with the ball or throw (underarm) and hit a 
runner below the waist 
• Fielders can only run 3 steps with the ball and can pass to other fielders if needed 
• If a runner is hit or tagged, they stop where they are tagged and continue to run from that spot 
when the next kicker kicks the ball 
• 1 point for every time a runner reaches a safety zone 
• Teams switch after every player has had a kick
How can you avoid fielders with 
the ball? 
Where is a good place to kick the 
ball/

team

fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing are as shown 
• 1 batter and 3-4 fielders 
• Batters bat as individuals to see how many runs they can score 
• The batter gets 4 hits regardless of being caught or bowled out etc.  
• The batter chooses a Cone to run around after a successful hit. Each cone is worth 5 points.  
• Batter also gets to choose which ball they hit. At each playing area, have a small ball, a medium 
ball and a larger ball 
• Fielders can get the batter out in the following ways, bowled/struck out, caught out, run out 
• After each person from the batt g team has had a go change team roles

fiches.title: Team Bucketball
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup playing area as shown 
• Divide class or group into 3 teams. Throwing/Running team (blue), Fielding team (yellow), Catchers 
(red) 
• The aim of the game is for the throwing team to throw the balls out into the field of play (same 
time). Once all balls have been thrown a runner from the throwing team continuously runs 
between two Cones. 1 run is scored for each time they make a run 
• The fielders collect the balls and throw, roll or pass them to the catchers who place all the balls in 
the bucket. Once all balls are in the bucket they yell freeze.  
• Throwers rotate so everyone has had a go at ‘running’ 
• Switch team roles when everyone in each team has had a ‘run’ 
How can you make sure you 
return the ball to the catchers 
quickly? 
Where is a good place to throw 
the ball out into the field of play?
12-15
Various Balls 
Cones 
Hoops    Bucket
Team Bucketball
Increase or decrease the 
distance of the running Cones 
Place a different shaped ball in 
each hoop e.g soccer ball that 
can be kicked, beanbag, tennis 
ball, vortex etc 
Catching team must return each 
ball to the right hoop in 10 
seconds or a point is awarded to 
the throwing team 
Fielders cannot run with the ball 
and must throw the ball to the 
catchers 
Striking
Fielding
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: There and back
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing are as shown  
• Two teams of four. One running team and one bowling/fielding team 
• Fielding team has one ‘bowler/pitcher’ who aims to throw the ball through the hoop. The 
‘backstop’ catches, and returns the ball to the fielder who throws the ball to back to the bowler. 
The bowler/pitcher gets 6 turns before fielding team rotates roles. Rotate so everyone has a bowl. 
• While this is happening the batting team aims to run around (one a time) around a cone and 
back. They don’t stop until the bowler/pitcher finishes their 6th bowl. Score a run for each time a 
runner gets around the end cone and back 
• If the bowler/pitcher manages to throw one through the hoop, 2 runs are deducted from the 
running for every successful throw  
• Switch after everyone on the fielding has had a bowl/pitch.
Why is grounding our bat 
important when running between 
the wickets in cricket? 
Why is accurate throwing and 
catching important when 
fielding?
8
There and back
Increase or decrease the 
distance for the runny g team 
Increase or decrease the 
distance of the thrower to/from 
the hoop 
Underarm or overarm throwing  
Instead of run-in around the 
cone, the runny g team must 
ground their bat like in cricket 
when reaching a cone
Striking
Fielding

fiches.title: Strike Zone
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown 
• 4  batters and 4 fielders 
• Each  batter gets 3 attempts to hit a ball out into the playing area. If a batter hits a ball into the 
designated zone as shown below, they score that amount points plus any runs they make.  
• After a hit, the batter runs around either of the orange Cones and back to the batting tee. 
• The fielding team stop the ball and aim to pass to a player in each of the scoring zone, they yell 
freeze when they have done that.  
• If the batter is in between the Cones and the tee, they forfeit their runs but still count the score from 
the zone they hit the ball in 
• If the fielders catch the ball, they add 3 points to their teams score and the batter is out  
• Switch after everyone on the batting team has had 2 at bats.  
Where are you trying to to hit the 
ball? Why? 
As the fielding team, how do you 
defend the space?

fiches.title: Rapid Rounders
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
•
Setup multiple playing areas as shown or simply as a whole class  
•
Two teams a batting team and a fielding team 
•
The aim of the game is for the batters to strike a ball into the field of play and run around as many bases 
(white) as they can.  
•
The object of the fielding team is to catch the ball to get the batter out or to stop the runner by passing the 
ball to any of the fielders at a ‘stop’ (red) base. When they do they yell ‘freeze’ 
•
When freeze is yelled the runner stops at or returns to the last white base they touched and waits for the 
next batter 
•
Runners on white bases run when a batter form their team hits a ball into play. The aim is to get back to the 
‘home’ base. Overtaking is allowed but all bases must be touched by the runners foot. More than one 
person on a white base is allowed. 1 point awarded for a home run 
•
Rotate after everyone on the batting team has had a hit 
Where are you trying to to hit the 
ball? Why? 
As the fielding team, how do you 
decide which ‘stop’ base to 
throw to?
12+ or Whole Class

Cones

Change the striking implement

ball e.g throwing/kicking 
Fielders have to throw to all 3 
‘stop’ bases’  
Last hitter = fielders have to throw 
to all 4 stop bases (gives time for 
last runner to get around the 
white bases) 
Striking
Fielding

Ask STudents
• Setup multiple playing areas as shown
• In each group their is a fielding team and a batting team
• Batting team lines up at 3rd base with one hitter and a team mate who pitches them the ball
• If a batter successfully hits the ball into the field of play the aim to run to either the 1st, 2nd or 3rd
base. 1 points for 1st base, 2 points for 2nd base and 3 points for 3rd base. If a batter reaches 3rd
they join the end of their line
• The fielding team aims stop the batter from making it to 3rd base by fielding and throwing ahead
to a base.
• If a batter is caught out or run out, no points are awarded.
• Each batter has two at bats and then teams switch over

6
Instructions:
From a marked line on the floor, throw a baseball or softball at another ball resting 
on top of a cone.  See how many times you can knock it over.  When it becomes too 
easy, throw the ball from further away.
Target Ball

From a marked line on the floor, throw a baseball or softball at another ball resting
on top of a cone.  See how many times you can knock it over.  When it becomes too
easy, throw the ball from further away.

fiches.title: Underhand Fielding
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
8
Instructions:
Underhand toss or roll a ball back and forth with a partner.  Remember to always get 
your mitt down and keep your eyes on the ball.  Watch it go into your mitt.
Underhand Fielding

sections.Instructions:
Underhand toss or roll a ball back and forth with a partner.  Remember to always get

fiches.title: Running Bases
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
9
Instructions:
Run as fast as you can between 2 bases for 30 seconds, rest and then do it again.  
Attempt to do this 5 times.  Make sure you touch the base each time you run to it.
Running Bases

sections.Instructions:
Run as fast as you can between 2 bases for 30 seconds, rest and then do it again.
Attempt to do this 5 times.  Make sure you touch the base each time you run to it.
Running Bases

fiches.title: Scarf Juggling
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
10
Instructions:
Try juggling 2 scarves at a time to improve your hand-eye coordination.  See how long 
you can do it without letting them drop.  When it becomes too easy you can switch to 
3 scarves or use 2 tennis balls.
Scarf Juggling

sections.Instructions:
Try juggling 2 scarves at a time to improve your hand-eye coordination.  See how long
you can do it without letting them drop.  When it becomes too easy you can switch to
3 scarves or use 2 tennis balls.
Scarf Juggling

fiches.title: Slide into Home
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
11
Instructions:
At this station, practice sliding into a base on top of a safety mat.  Start a few steps 
away from the mat and then run hard to the mat and slide so you land safely on the 
mat.  Remember to tuck your lower leg by bending it at the knee as shown in the 
image below.
Slide into Home

sections.Instructions:

away from the mat and then run hard to the mat and slide so you land safely on the
mat.  Remember to tuck your lower leg by bending it at the knee as shown in the
image below.
Slide into Home

and try to throw strikes with overhand tosses at the hoop.  Try not to knock over the 
hoop when you throw it.

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
14
Instructions:
Practice fielding ground balls by sidestepping back and forth between 2 cones.  
Crouch down and bend your knees and be mindful not to cross your legs as you 
move from side to side.
Fancy Footwork

sections.Instructions:

fiches.title: Tee Work
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
15
Instructions:
Practice your batting stance and swing by hitting a whiffle ball off a tee or cone into 
a safety net.  Remember to keep your back elbow up and make a level swing forward.  
Keep your eye on the ball while you swing.
Tee Work

sections.Instructions:
Practice your batting stance and swing by hitting a whiffle ball off a tee or cone into
a safety net.  Remember to keep your back elbow up and make a level swing forward.
Keep your eye on the ball while you swing.
Tee Work

fiches.title: Soft Hands
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
16
Instructions:
In this activity, you will be working on having ‘soft hands.’  Using floor tape, mark off 
a small box on the floor as seen in the image.  Place your knees on one end of the 
box and place your hands in the interior of the box. When ready, your partner will roll 
you the ball.  You need to scoop it up with your ‘soft hands’ and bring the ball into 
your chest area.  Give the ball back to your partner and do it again.
Soft Hands

sections.Instructions:
In this activity, you will be working on having ‘soft hands.’  Using floor tape, mark off
a small box on the floor as seen in the image.  Place your knees on one end of the
box and place your hands in the interior of the box. When ready, your partner will roll
you the ball.  You need to scoop it up with your ‘soft hands’ and bring the ball into
your chest area.  Give the ball back to your partner and do it again.
Soft Hands

fiches.title: Soft Toss
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
17
Instructions:
At this station, your partner will be standing to the side of you.  They will toss a whiffle 
ball underhand in your direction.  When it lands in your strike zone, swing at it as you 
hit it into your safety net.  This will work on your batting stance and swing.  Make sure 
your partner is out of harm’s way when they toss.
Soft Toss

sections.Instructions:
At this station, your partner will be standing to the side of you.  They will toss a whiffle
ball underhand in your direction.  When it lands in your strike zone, swing at it as you
hit it into your safety net.  This will work on your batting stance and swing.  Make sure
your partner is out of harm’s way when they toss.
Soft Toss

fiches.title: Footwork Drill
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
18
Instructions:
Spread out 5 balls in front of you as well as some type of net to catch the balls (as 
seen in image below).  When ready, use the proper footwork to move towards the 
first ball.  Pick it up and throw it with velocity into the net.  Then, follow this same 
pattern with the remaining 4 balls.  This is a great drill to improve your footwork 
Footwork Drill

sections.Instructions:
Spread out 5 balls in front of you as well as some type of net to catch the balls (as
seen in image below).  When ready, use the proper footwork to move towards the
first ball.  Pick it up and throw it with velocity into the net.  Then, follow this same
pattern with the remaining 4 balls.  This is a great drill to improve your footwork
Footwork Drill

fiches.title: Self Toss
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
19
Instructions:
In this drill, use a yarn ball (or no-fly ball) which will not travel far or hurt anyone 
nearby.  Self toss the ball into the air and hit it in the direction of a wall.  This is a great 
drill to work on timing, bat speed, hand-eye coordination and batting stance.
Self Toss

sections.Instructions:
In this drill, use a yarn ball (or no-fly ball) which will not travel far or hurt anyone
nearby.  Self toss the ball into the air and hit it in the direction of a wall.  This is a great
drill to work on timing, bat speed, hand-eye coordination and batting stance.
Self Toss

fiches.title: Reflex Drill
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
20
Instructions:
This is a great drill to work on your baseball reflexes.  Take a baseball and hold it in 
your right hand about chest height. Then simply drop it and allow your left hand to 
catch it.  Once caught, keep reversing this process over and over for about a minute, 
rest and do it again.
Reflex Drill

sections.Instructions:
This is a great drill to work on your baseball reflexes.  Take a baseball and hold it in
your right hand about chest height. Then simply drop it and allow your left hand to
catch it.  Once caught, keep reversing this process over and over for about a minute,
rest and do it again.
Reflex Drill

fiches.title: Mini Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
21
Instructions:
Play catch with your partner using a small whiffle ball (sometimes used for golf).  
Underhand and overhand toss the ball back and forth to improve your hand-eye 
coordination.  After using the small balls, the bigger balls will be much easier to catch.
Mini Ball

sections.Instructions:
Play catch with your partner using a small whiffle ball (sometimes used for golf).
Underhand and overhand toss the ball back and forth to improve your hand-eye
coordination.  After using the small balls, the bigger balls will be much easier to catch.
Mini Ball

fiches.title: Fly Ball Catch
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
22
Instructions:
At this station, underhand toss a ball high into the air and catch it like it is a fly ball.  
As it lands in your mitt, secure it with your other hand.  Keep your eye on the ball the 
entire time it is in the air.
Fly Ball Catch

sections.Instructions:
At this station, underhand toss a ball high into the air and catch it like it is a fly ball.
As it lands in your mitt, secure it with your other hand.  Keep your eye on the ball the
entire time it is in the air.
Fly Ball Catch

fiches.title: Lie Flat Drill
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
23
Instructions:
This is a great drill to improve hand-eye coordination.  Lie flat on your back and toss 
a whiffle ball into the air.  Without moving, catch it with either hand and repeat the 
process.  You can work on aim and catching at the same time.
Lie Flat Drill

sections.Instructions:
This is a great drill to improve hand-eye coordination.  Lie flat on your back and toss
a whiffle ball into the air.  Without moving, catch it with either hand and repeat the
process.  You can work on aim and catching at the same time.
Lie Flat Drill

fiches.title: Wall Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
24
Instructions:
Underhand toss a ball against a wall with a partner.  To start, one of you will 
underhand toss the ball against the wall while the partner catches it.  Keep alternating 
between throwing and catching.  When it becomes too easy, make difficult tosses 
that will be more challenging to catch.
Wall Ball

A

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Basketball Games! 
Inside this resource, you’ll get 22 fun activities you can use with Kindergarten through 8th Grade. Each activity is explained 
with simple instructions, equipment lists, diagrams and modifications. 
Skill Focus 
Each activity will have a skill focus, based on one of the fundamental skills of basketball. 
Group Size 
All of these activities are designed to work with smaller or larger groups. A group size is provided, but this is just the 
minimum number of students you will need for the game to work. For larger groups, you can increase the number of 
players in the game, or create multiple stations with smaller groups. 
Complexity 
Each activity is rated on a Complexity scale indicating how difficult the skills are to perform. 
  Green - recommended for Kindergarten and up (or with modifications for older children) 
  Yellow - recommended for 3rd grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
  Red - recommended for 5th grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
Modifications 
We’ve also included modifications to make the games easier or harder, for different age levels. 
Ask Students 
Some games also include review questions you can ask your students - allowing you to reinforce key coaching points, 
rules or skills.

sections.Modifications:
We’ve also included modifications to make the games easier or harder, for different age levels.
Ask Students
Some games also include review questions you can ask your students - allowing you to reinforce key coaching points,
rules or skills.

fiches.title: Dribble Knockout
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Dribble Knockout
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Dribbling 
Finding Space 
Whole Class
Cones
Balls
• Each student receives a ball and starts anywhere they wish on the court 
• On the teachers commands students begin dribbling around the playing area attempting 
to knock other students balls out of the playing area 
• Players cannot protect their ball by picking it up, no double dribbles allowed 
• No body contact permitted 
• If you ball goes out of the playing area students must dribble around the outside of the 
court and then re-enter the game.
Shorten the playing area 
Break students into smaller

fiches.title: Square Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Square Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Gameplay
Passing and moving
• Students formed into groups of 4. 
• 3 attackers (blue shirts) and one defender (red shirt) 
• The object of the game is for the attackers to pass the ball around the square without the 
defender intercepting the ball. Attackers cannot dribble but can move without the ball 
• The defender cannot make body contact and cannot steal the ball out of the hands of 
the attackers 
• Attackers aim to get to 5-10 passes 
• Play for one minute and switch
Have a group of 5 and play 3 vs 
2 
Allow attackers up to 5 dribbles 
Attackers count how many 
passes they make in a minute  
Defender counts how many 
‘steals’ they get 
Make the playing are larger or 
smaller 
What is a pivot? How does it 
help in this game? 
When should you call for a 
pass? 
How can you trick the 
defender? (Pass fakes)
4
Cones
Balls
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

Group Size

Balls
• Split students into group of approximately 5-6 depending on how many hoops 
you have 
• Setup 9 spots each each hoop as shown or in spots of yours or students choice 
• Students take it in turns to shoot starting at spot number 1 
• If a student makes a shot they had to spot two and so on

• First person to make a shot from each spot is the winner

fiches.title: Four Corners
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Four Corners
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
• Setup playing area as shown 
• Have 2 students from each team stand in a marked zone diagonally from each. These are 
the ‘receivers’. The aim is for each team to get the ball to one of their receivers inside the 
colored zone.  
• If a receiver collects a pass from a team mate their team scores a point.  
• Teams must make at least 3 passes before they can attempt to pass the ball to a receiver.  
• Defensive team can only intercept not steal from hands, attackers cannot dribble but can 
move without the ball
Increase the number of passes 
before allowing a pass to a 
receiver 
Only have one receiver at 
each end, this receiver can 
then alternate between both 
colored area zones to receive 
a pass 
Start with no designated 
receiver and allow any player 
to move into the colored area 
to receive a pass to score
How does having ‘two 
goals’ make it easier to 
score? 
How does accurate 
passing help in this game?
Gameplay
Passing and moving
5 vs 5
Cones
Balls
Bibs

fiches.title: Hoop Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Hoop Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Gameplay
Passing and moving
• Have students form teams of 3-4. This will make either a 3 on 3 game or a 4 on 4 game 
• The object of this game is to receive a ball inside one of your colored hoops with at least 
one foot inside the hoop 
• Offensive team must make at least 3 passes before attempting to pass to a teammate 
inside a hula hoop. No dribbling allowed, passing only. 
• Defensive team can intercept a ball but cannot steal the ball from hands of offence 
• Players have 3 seconds to pass the ball 
• Ball out of bounds = turnover 
• Teams change offence and defence if there is a turnover or a successful pass to a hoop  
• Students cannot ‘hang’ since a hoop for longer than 5-10 seconds
Cones
Balls
6-8
Hula Hoops
Increase or decrease the 
amount of passes before 
passing to a hoop 
Have a dedicated ‘catcher’ 
who can float between 
hoops and gameplay 
Allow dribbling and stealing 
Which players ate the safest 
to pass to? 
If the defence is covering 
you, what can you do?
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Tag Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Tag Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Gameplay
Passing and moving
10-12
• Split groups in two teams (4-5 students per team) 
• One team is the taggers, the other is the dodgers. The dodgers each get a ball and the 
taggers get one ball to share between them 
• The aim of the game is for the dodgers to dribble around the playing area and  avoid 
getting tagged (with the ball) by the taggers 
• Taggers can only pass the ball (no dribbling), but they can move without the ball 
• Taggers can only tag with the ball in hand, not by throwing it at the dribblers 
• Play to 3 ‘tags’ and then switch
Play with larger groups e.g the 
whole class and designated 
half as dodgers and the other 
half as taggers 
Increase or decrease the 
playing area, depending on 
student numbers 
Add more basketballs 
Allow dodgers to dribble 5 times 
before tagging or passing
How can making accurate 
passes help in this game? 
Why are quick passes 
important?
Cones
Balls
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Steal the Pinnie
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Steal the Pinnie
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Cones
Balls
• Each student receives a ball and a pinnie/bib that they tuck into the back of their shorts/
pants (Like a tail) 
• The aim of the game is to keep your ‘tail’ for as long as possible 
• Students dribble around the playing area continuously trying to steal others bibs, whilst 
protecting their own. Students must not hold their own bib to protect it 
• If a student loses their bib they continue trying to steal others  
• When students steal a bib they must quickly tuck it and continue playing 
• Students with more than one bib may hold it as insurance for a future lost bib or 
thoughtfully give it someone who doesn’t have one. 
Shorten the playing area 
Break students into smaller 
groups according to ability / 
play in a smaller area 
See who can collect the most 
bibs (no donating a bib) 
If you have different colored 
bibs have 4 different teams 
Why should you keep your head 
up while dribbling? 
How do you protect your 
dribble/bib in this game?
Dribbling 
Finding Space 
Whole Class
Bibs/Pinnies
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

Complexity
Equipment

Balls
Pinnnies
•
Split group into two even teams. 
•
At each end have an ‘end zone’ that teams try aim to catch a ball within 
•
Offensive team start with the ball and must male at least 3 passes before going for the 
end zone 
•
Only one person from each team, is allowed inside the end zone at any given time. If the 
offensive team has 2 or more in the end zone it = a turnover 
•
Players cannot ‘camp’ in the end zone (10  seconds only allowed in the end zone) 
•
Defenders can intercept the ball but cannot ‘steal’ from attackers hands 
•
Offensive team cannot run with the ball 
•
No body contact allowed 
•
Defners must give attacker in possession of the ball 3 feet space (arms reach). No 
crowding

players

area

the ball and then must pass

in the end zone

run in to the end zone for a 
catch?

defender?

fiches.title: Bump
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Bump
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Cones
Balls
Bibs / Pinnies
• Each student receives a ball and lines up outside the 3point line 
• The object of the game is to not let the person behind you score before you. If 
they do you are out 
• To start the first person in line shoots from the free throw line, immediately after 
that the 2nd person shoots 
• If players miss they keep going until they get it in, players can shoot from 
anywhere. 
• Keep going until one person is left as the winner
Easier: Shoot from the foul line or 
dotted line inside the paint 
Harder: Shoot from the 3-point 
line 
Allow students to “bump” the ball 
out of the air using their own ball 
Students who are eliminated can 
stand under the basket. If they 
catch an airball by another 
student they take that student’s 
place in the game
Shooting, Rebounding
6+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Golden Child
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Golden Child
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Cones
Balls
Bibs / Pinnies
• Break students into two teams. One team is the ‘dribbling' team and the other is the 
‘shooting’ team 
• The object of the dribbling team is to get around the outside Cones as fast as possible one 
at a time.  
• If the shooting team makes a basket from the free throw line the dribbler stops where they 
are and sits down. If the dribbler makes it all around each Cone before the shooting team 
makes a shot they score a ‘run' for their team  
• The last runner for the dribbling team is the ‘golden child. When they go they dribble 
around the Cones and as they go pas the player who are sitting down they revive them 
and they also try to make it to the last Cone. 
• Switch over teams after everyone has had a go
Easier: Shoot from the foul line or 
dotted line inside the paint 
Harder: Shoot from the 3-point 
line 
Increase or decrease the 
distance the student has to run 
around the cones
Dribbling, Shooting
6+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Back to Back Bandits
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Back to Back Bandits
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Cones
Balls
Bibs / Pinnies
• Each student gets a ball and finds a partner 
• Students stand back to back and performs a stationary dribble.  
• On teachers command students, try and poke the ball away from their partner 
whilst keeping control of their dribble. Students must keep stationary and only 
turn their body when they try and poke the ball away 
• After a minute, students find another partner
Easier: Have one student dribble 
while the other tries to poke it 
away 
Harder: group 3 or 4 students 
together and have them all go 
back to back to compete
Dribbling
6+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.body:

Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment

Cones
Balls
• Split class into two lines 
• Each student has a partner that they face in the other line 
• Students pass back and forth all the way down court and finish with a 
layup 
• No dribbling allowed just pass and move 
• Emphasize moving ahead after a pass and calling for the ball.  
• Try not to let the ball come to a stop
Adjust the distance between the 
two passing lines 
Have students practice chest 
passes, bounce passes, overhead 
passes and baseball passes 
Harder: Add a third player in the 
middle that must relay the ball 
back and forth between the two 
outside players
Passing
6+

fiches.title: Circle Chase
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Circle Chase
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Balls
• Create a circle with approx 8-10 students. Make sure the circle is wide enough 
so students are approx 2-4 metres apart 
• Have two players on opposite sides of the circle start with the ball 
• Players pass the ball around the circle (without skipping a person) and try to 
overtake the ball in front  
• Teacher calls ‘change’ and players must change the direction of the pass
Adjust the distance between the 
passers 
Have students practice chest 
passes, bounce passes, overhead 
passes and baseball passes 
Add a third ball to the mix
Passing
6+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Pacman
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Pacman
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Balls
• Choose 4-5 taggers (red), the rest are Pacmen (Blue) 
• Ghosts and Pacmen must stay on lines whilst running with no cutting of corners 
• If tagged, a pacman is  not out they must simply perform 5 star jumps (off of a 
line) and then return to the game.  
• If whistle is blown the roles reverse and pacmen can tag the ghosts (10 
seconds only), if tagged a ghost must perform 10 star jumps 
• All students must dribble a ball
Adjust the ratio of taggers to 
overall players to make the game 
easier or harder 
Increase or decrease the size of 
the playing area
Dribbling
10+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Star Wars
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Star Wars
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Cones
Balls
• Start with a group of 8-10 students inside a 20m x 20m playing area 
• Have 4 students on the outside of each side of the square as interchange players 
• Students each have a tall Cone (‘planet’) they have to defend 
• Students aim to knock over their opponents planet using a pass 
• Students may run to collect a ball that is open space but must be aware that 
their cone can be knocked over with a pass if so 
• If a cone is knocked over that students change over with an interchange player
Adjust the distance between 
players 
Use larger or smaller targets for 
players to aim at
Passing
8+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Titanic
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Titanic
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Cones
Balls
• Split group into two teams, if you have access to more than two hoops split evenly 
across each hoop 
• Choose a spot that students have to shoot from. 
• On teachers go students shoot from this Cone, if they make it they go to the end 
of the line, if they miss they head over to the ‘titanic’ ship (a Cone in the corner) 
• If a student makes a shot they release the first person in line on their ‘titanic’ 
• If all students are on the titanic ship, that team loses. Keep playing until one team 
is left. 
Adjust the distance of the hula 
hoops to the basket 
Have players make 2 consecutive 
shots to progress to the next hoop
Shooting
8+

fiches.title: Number Challenge
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Number Challenge
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Cones
Balls
Bibs / Pinnies
• Split class into two even teams or 4 teams to keep numbers lower 
• Give each students in each team a number e.g 1-8 
• The teacher calls out a number and each student with that number races out 
and collects a ball and tries to score before the other teams.  
• The student who scores first scores a point for their team and all players head 
back to their line 
• To make it more challenging 
call 2-3 numbers at a time 
and players must transport 
the ball via pass only (no 
dribbling) and all players 
must score
Gameplay
6+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Jail Break
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Jail Break
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Balls
• Split students into groups of approximately 5-6 and have each group go to a hoop 
each 
• Students line up and shoot from the free throw line. The first student to miss starts in the 
keyway (jail) 
• The next student shoots a free throw, if they miss the person in the ‘jail’ grabs the 
rebound and dribbles out to the 3 point line. The student who just missed plays defense 
to prevent them from scoring. Making game of 1 vs 1. 
• The first student who scores, wins. The student who lost goes to or stays in jail. 
• Each student starts with 5 points. Every time you lose a game you lose a point.  
• If you are on zero points you still play. The last person to lose all their points is the winner.
Easier: shoot from dotted line 
inside paint 
Harder: shoot from 3-point-line 
Set a time limit for the 1v1 game 
to be completed (roughly 10-15 
seconds)
Shooting
5+
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Jumping Rope
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
Instructions:
Try jump roping for 55 seconds without stopping.  Rest, then try it again.  Jump 
forward and backward.  Start out with 2 small jumps between each rotation of the 
rope.  When that becomes too easy, switch to 1 small jump between each rotation. 
If you have trouble jumping with the rope, experiment using the same fundamentals, 
but with no rope.
Jumping Rope
55
Seconds of Jump 
Rope

rope.  When that becomes too easy, switch to 1 small jump between each rotation.
If you have trouble jumping with the rope, experiment using the same fundamentals,
but with no rope.

Try to hula hoop for 55 seconds without dropping the hoop.  Move your hips in a 
circular motion.  Get yourself into a rhythm to balance the hoop around your waist.  If 
it becomes too hard, you can try to spin the hoop around your arm.

Welcome to Classroom PE Games! This resource is designed to help teachers and educators create 
a fun and engaging physical education experience inside a classroom or other confined space.
Inside, you’ll find 22 simple activities to engage students of all ages and abilities, while also promoting 
fitness, coordination, and teamwork. 
Whether you’re a seasoned PE teacher, an after-school coordinator, or a classroom teacher looking 
for new and exciting ways to engage your students, this resource has something for everyone.
Each game includes the following info:
1.	 Focus: The components of fitness or skill areas that the activity focuses on: 
2.	 Group Size: The minimum number of participants required for the lesson.
3.	 Difficulty:  A rating from 1 (easiest) to 5 (hardest). Harder games require more skill, more setup 
or more complex rules. 
4.	 Equipment: Required sports equipment or instructor materials.
5.	 Instructions:  How to play the game, step by step.
6.	 Variations: Modifications to make the game easier or harder. Or just a different way to play.

fiches.title: Freeze Dance Fitness Freeze Dance Fitness
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Change up the exercise with every round. For example, jumping jacks, push-ups, crunches, yoga 
poses, running in place or laps around the room. You can also teach the kids a specific dance move 
or animal movement to do while the music is on, or just let them freestyle.
Freeze Dance Fitness
Freeze Dance Fitness
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Music
Setup
•	
Have the students stand up and spread out around the 
room, making sure they have space not to bump into 
each other.
Instructions
1.	 As soon as the teacher plays music, the students will begin to freestyle dance in their spots, 
being careful not to bump into the other children.
2.	 As soon as the music stops, they must freeze in their spot and stand on one foot.
3.	 Next, the teacher puts the music back on as the same process will continue.

sections.Variation:

poses, running in place or laps around the room. You can also teach the kids a specific dance move
or animal movement to do while the music is on, or just let them freestyle.

fiches.title: Wolf in Sheep’s Clothing Wolf in Sheep’s Clothing
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Change the locomotor so kids must hop, craw, or tiptoe while they change desks. Put on some fun 
music while they are moving and turn it off when they have to sit down.
Wolf in Sheep’s Clothing
Wolf in Sheep’s Clothing
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
All the kids sit at their desks or on the floors, with their 
heads down and eyes closed.  
Instructions
1.	 Once the kids have their eyes closed, tap one kid on the head to become the wolf. No one else 
should know who the wolf is.
2.	 Tell everyone else to secretly decide if they are a dog, cat, or chicken.
3.	 Instruct them to maintain their chosen animal identity for the entire game.
4.	 When the teacher calls out an animal, those who identify as that animal must open their eyes, 
get up, and switch to a new desk within 10 seconds. All the other kids keep their eyes closed and 
heads down.
5.	 The wolf must switch desks EVERY time, regardless of the animal called.
6.	 The objective of the game is for the players to figure out who the wolf is.
7.	 After a predetermined time, allow the kids to guess who they think the wolf is.

sections.Variation:

music while they are moving and turn it off when they have to sit down.

fiches.title: Hoop Slinger Hoop Slinger
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
You can move the cones closer or farther away to make it easier or more challenging depending on 
the age or skill of the class. 
Hoop Slinger
Hoop Slinger
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Hula Hoops
•	
Cones or Chairs
Setup
•	
Move the desks out of the way.  Place the kids in 2 
lines.  Place an empty chair or cone if you have it across 
from each line.  Mark the floor with tape to designate a 
tossing line.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first child in line will toss their hoop and try to land it around the chair.
2.	 After the hoop is tossed, they must retrieve the hoop and bring it to the next child in line, then 
go to the end of their line to wait their turn.
3.	 If they land the hoop around the cone, their team receives a point.
4.	 After a 5 or 10 minute period, the team with the most points wins that round.

sections.Variation:
You can move the cones closer or farther away to make it easier or more challenging depending on
the age or skill of the class.

Hoop Slinger

3.	 If they land the hoop around the cone, their team receives a point.

fiches.title: Secret Handshake Secret Handshake
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
8
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Make this activity a competition.  Have the other kids vote on the coolest secret handshake and 
pick a winner (they cannot vote for themselves). 
Secret Handshake
Secret Handshake
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Partner up students in groups of two or three.
Instructions
1.	 This is a fun activity where the children will use their creativity and strengthen their memory.
In their groups, the kids will create a secret handshake that can include as many movements as 
they can remember.
2.	 The routine must include some type of exercise (push-up, jumping jack, squat, yoga pose, etc.)
3.	 Give each group about 5 minutes to create their handshake routine.
4.	 When ready, have them go in front of the class and show off their handshake to the entire class.

fiches.title: Fitness Detective Fitness Detective
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
9
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Play the game but only allow the children to perform simple locomotor movements while sitting at 
their desks and not standing up at all to maintain greater control of the class.
Fitness Detective
Fitness Detective
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the kids out around your room.  They can sit 
at their desks or on the floor to begin.  Choose one 
student to be the Detective who will close their eyes 
in front of the room and not peek.  Then, pick another 
child to be the leader.
Instructions
1.	 Once the kids are set up around the room, the Detective will stand in front of the room with 
their back towards a wall and their eyes closed.
2.	 Next, the teacher will secretly pick one student to be the leader.
3.	 Once the leader is chosen, they will now start to perform various locomotor movements in their 
spot such as clapping their hands, snapping their fingers, jumping in place, jumping jacks, push-
ups, or jogging in place.  They can be as creative as they would like.
4.	 The other children will begin to mirror all the movements of the leader.
5.	 The Detective will turn around and now begin to watch the children do their locomotor 
movements.  Nobody is allowed to talk.
6.	 The leader has to do a locomotor movement from 10-30 seconds.
7.	 The Detective will now get 3 guesses of who the leader is by watching the movements of the 
class carefully trying to figure out who the leader may be.  
The
Detective

Play the game but only allow the children to perform simple locomotor movements while sitting at
their desks and not standing up at all to maintain greater control of the class.

fiches.title: Silent Ball Silent Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
10
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To add a level of difficulty, you can play the game with 2 balls with the same rules in place.
Silent Ball
Silent Ball
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
A medium size ball
Setup
•	
Spread out the students around the room, standing up 
near their desks.  Pick one child to start off with a ball.
Instructions
1.	 During the game, the only child allowed to talk is the student with the ball.
2.	 To start the game, the student with the ball will call out another child’s name and gently toss 
them the ball.
3.	 The student who catches the ball now calls out another child’s name, and then tosses to the next 
child.
4.	 You cannot toss the ball directly back to the student who tossed it to you.
5.	 If a child misses the ball or a child makes a bad toss, that child is now out and must sit down at 
their desk.
6.	 The teacher can determine if it was a bad throw or a bad catch.
7.	 The last 5 kids left won the round and then you can start a new round.

To add a level of difficulty, you can play the game with 2 balls with the same rules in place.

4.	 You cannot toss the ball directly back to the student who tossed it to you.

their desk.
6.	 The teacher can determine if it was a bad throw or a bad catch.
7.	 The last 5 kids left won the round and then you can start a new round.

fiches.title: FOCUS GROUP SIZE “Guess Who” Fitness “Guess Who” Fitness
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
11
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
“Guess Who” Fitness
“Guess Who” Fitness
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Pick half the class to sit at their desks, and the other 
half to stand in front of the room.
Instructions
1.	 Once ready, the teacher will say ‘heads down, thumbs up.”  Once this happens, all the students 
at their desks will close their eyes and bring their head down to their desk, resting their forehead 
on their arms, making sure they are not peeking. One of their arms should be extended, thumb 
up.
2.	 On “go” the kids at the front of the room will perform some type of locomotor movement as 
they move about the room. For example, high-knee marching, hopping on one foot, tip-toeing, 
bunny hopping, walking lunges, etc.
3.	 Each standing kid will choose one sitting kid and press their thumb down. They will then return to 
the front of the room.
4.	 Once all the standing kids are back at the front of the room, ask the sitting kids to open their 
eyes.
5.	 Ask each sitting kid (one at a time) who they think pressed their thumb.  They get one guess.
6.	 If they guess correctly, they now replace the child in front of the room who pressed down their 
thumb. If not, they stay seated.
7.	 Reset and repeat.

•
None

half to stand in front of the room.

fiches.title: Doggy’s Bone Doggy’s Bone
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
You can change the locomotor to match a different animal and the item to match. For example, use 
a bunny hop to steal a carrot, a bear crawl to steal a fish, a snake slither to steal a bug, etc.
Doggy’s Bone
Doggy’s Bone
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
An eraser
Setup
•	
All the kids sit at their desks or on the floor.  Pick one 
child (the dog) to stand in front of the class with their 
eyes closed.  Place a big whiteboard eraser, or a pink 
pencil eraser (a dog’s bone) on the floor behind them.
Instructions
1.	 Once ready, the child in front of the class will close their eyes with their back facing the rest of 
the class at their desks.
2.	 The teacher will pick one child to be the ‘selected’ student.
3.	 Once the child is selected, the class will begin a countdown out loud going from 10 seconds to 1 
second.
4.	 As they are counting, the ‘selected’ child must crawl up like a dog and take the bone (the eraser) 
without being seen and quietly bring it back to their desk.  They can sit on the bone or hide it at 
their desk.
5.	 As soon as the students complete their countdown, the dog (the kid in front of the class) turns 
around and gets 3 guesses to figure out who stole the bone.

sections.Variation:
You can change the locomotor to match a different animal and the item to match. For example, use
a bunny hop to steal a carrot, a bear crawl to steal a fish, a snake slither to steal a bug, etc.

4.	 As they are counting, the ‘selected’ child must crawl up like a dog and take the bone (the eraser)
without being seen and quietly bring it back to their desk.  They can sit on the bone or hide it at
their desk.
5.	 As soon as the students complete their countdown, the dog (the kid in front of the class) turns
around and gets 3 guesses to figure out who stole the bone.

fiches.title: Move and Groove Move and Groove
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
13
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of running around the room, the teacher can change the locomotor movement to 
something different such as skipping, galloping, side-stepping, or carioca.
Move and Groove
Move and Groove
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Music
Setup
•	
Designate each corner of the room with a number.  Pick 
one child to be the ‘guesser’ for the game.  The rest of 
the kids are scatterers.
Instructions
1.	 The guesser must stand up in front of the room with their back to the rest of the class who are 
the scatterers.
2.	 When the teacher puts on music, the scatterers must run around the room carefully.  As soon as 
the teacher stops the music, the scatterers must go to a corner and commit to that corner.
3.	 Next, the guesser who is not peeking must call out corner 1, 2, 3, or 4.  The children at the corner 
picked are out, and must sit in the middle of the room or at their desks.
4.	 Next, the music begins again and the entire process starts over.
5.	 If the guesser picks a corner, and no children are at the corner, all the students who are out 
come back into the game.
6.	 The last student not out wins the round and then the class can start a new round.
Corner 
1
Corner 
2
Corner 
3
Corner 
4
Guesser

fiches.title: Rock, Paper, Scissors Battle Rock, Paper, Scissors Battle
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
14
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of just doing jumping jacks, the teacher can select different exercises for the kids to 
perform after each rock, paper, scissor battle. For example, push-ups, crunches, burpees, laps 
around the room or running in place.
Rock, Paper, Scissors Battle
Rock, Paper, Scissors Battle
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
20 New Pencils
Setup
•	
Divide the class into 2 teams.  Give each child a number 
that matches a number of a child on the other team.  
Place 20 pencils in front of the class in a pile. 
Instructions
1.	 The teacher will call out a number.  The child on each team with the corresponding number must 
come in front of the room and do rock, paper, scissor against each other.
2.	 The child who wins first will take a pencil from the pile and go back to their seat.
3.	 Next, all the children on the team of the child who lost the rock, paper, scissor battle have to 
stand up and perform 10 jumping jacks, then return back to their chairs.
4.	 When completed, the teacher will call out a new number, and the students will follow the same 
process.
5.	 The team that collects 10 pencils first wins the round.  After the round is completed, mix the 
teams up and start again.

sections.Variation:
Instead of just doing jumping jacks, the teacher can select different exercises for the kids to
perform after each rock, paper, scissor battle. For example, push-ups, crunches, burpees, laps
around the room or running in place.

Rock, Paper, Scissors Battle
DIFFICULTY

that matches a number of a child on the other team.
