1.	 The teacher will call out a number.  The child on each team with the corresponding number must
come in front of the room and do rock, paper, scissor against each other.
2.	 The child who wins first will take a pencil from the pile and go back to their seat.
3.	 Next, all the children on the team of the child who lost the rock, paper, scissor battle have to
stand up and perform 10 jumping jacks, then return back to their chairs.
4.	 When completed, the teacher will call out a new number, and the students will follow the same
process.
5.	 The team that collects 10 pencils first wins the round.  After the round is completed, mix the
teams up and start again.

fiches.title: Tic-Tac-Toe Rumble Tic-Tac-Toe Rumble
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
15
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of the teacher calling out a number, the children simply form a line and keep taking their 
turn tossing the bean bags at the hash tag.   They follow the same rules, but the pace of the game 
is much faster.
Tic-Tac-Toe Rumble
Tic-Tac-Toe Rumble
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Bean Bags
•	
Floor Tape
Setup
•	
Move the desks out of the way and use floor tape to 
create a hash tag mark on the floor for tic-tac-toe.  Give 
each team bean bags but make sure they are all the 
same color.  For example, one team will use red bean 
bags, the other blue bean bags.  Give each child on 
each team a number that matches a player on the other 
team.  Use tape to mark off tossing lines on the floor.
Instructions
1.	 When the teacher calls out a number, those children come out to the tossing line and pick up a 
bean bag.  The teacher will alternate throws from team to team.
2.	 When a child gets a turn to toss their bean bag, they should aim at the hash tag to land the bag 
in a strategic spot.
3.	 If the bean bag lands in a box on the hash tag, the child leaves it there.  If they miss, they must 
retrieve it and place it back in their pile at the start line.
4.	 After both kids have a turn the teacher will call out another number to continue to process.
5.	 If a child tosses a bean bag that knocks a previous bean bag out of the hash tag square, they 
take over that square.  If their bean bag lands on top of an existing bean bag, again, they take 
over that square.
6.	 The first team to get 3 bean bags in a row wins the round.
#

team.  Use tape to mark off tossing lines on the floor.

fiches.title: Basket Busters Basket Busters
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
16
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Adjust the distance to the wastebasket to make it harder or easier. You can also set up multiple 
shooting lines with different values (1 point, 2 points, or 3 points). Kids can choose where to shoot 
from. The farther away the shot, the more points it is worth.
Basket Busters
Basket Busters
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 large, crumpled pieces of 
paper
•	
1 clean wastebasket
Setup
•	
Divide the kids into 2 single file lines. Mark a shooting 
line on the floor using tape. Place a large wastebasket 
about 5-10 feet away. The first kid in each line has a 
crumpled paper “ball.”
Instructions
1.	 On “go” the first kid in each line will try to toss their crumpled paper “ball” into the wastebasket. 
2.	 If they make the toss, their team gets a point. If they miss, they retrieve the “ball” and give it to 
the next kid in line.
3.	 The first team to score 10 points wins.

sections.Variation:
Adjust the distance to the wastebasket to make it harder or easier. You can also set up multiple
shooting lines with different values (1 point, 2 points, or 3 points). Kids can choose where to shoot
from. The farther away the shot, the more points it is worth.
Basket Busters
Basket Busters
DIFFICULTY

paper
•
1 clean wastebasket

line on the floor using tape. Place a large wastebasket
about 5-10 feet away. The first kid in each line has a
crumpled paper “ball.”

1.	 On “go” the first kid in each line will try to toss their crumpled paper “ball” into the wastebasket.
2.	 If they make the toss, their team gets a point. If they miss, they retrieve the “ball” and give it to
the next kid in line.
3.	 The first team to score 10 points wins.

fiches.title: Bottle Flip Frenzy Bottle Flip Frenzy
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
17
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of rewarding a child with a point each time they land the bottle standing up, give them a 
second turn when they land a bottle.  The first kid to land a bottle 2 times in a row wins the round.  
If a child is unsuccessful, the next child takes their turn.
Bottle Flip Frenzy
Bottle Flip Frenzy
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
One plastic water bottle per 
group, filled one-third full 
with the cap tightly screwed 
on
Setup
•	
Separate the kids into groups of 2 or 3. Give each group 
a water bottle.
Instructions
1.	 Once ready, the kids at each group will take turns flipping their water bottle onto the floor.
2.	 Their objective is to land it standing up.
3.	 Each time they land the bottle standing up straight, they will receive a point.
4.	 The first kid at each group to get to 3 points wins the round.
5.	 After each round, rotate the children to a new group and have them start again.

sections.Variation:
Instead of rewarding a child with a point each time they land the bottle standing up, give them a
second turn when they land a bottle.  The first kid to land a bottle 2 times in a row wins the round.
If a child is unsuccessful, the next child takes their turn.
Bottle Flip Frenzy
Bottle Flip Frenzy

group, filled one-third full

on

1.	 Once ready, the kids at each group will take turns flipping their water bottle onto the floor.
2.	 Their objective is to land it standing up.
3.	 Each time they land the bottle standing up straight, they will receive a point.
4.	 The first kid at each group to get to 3 points wins the round.
5.	 After each round, rotate the children to a new group and have them start again.

fiches.title: Pong-a-Palooza Pong-a-Palooza
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
18
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Have the students shoot the ball with their non-dominant hand or even bounce the ball on the 
floor first and have it bounce up to the cup.  This will make it more challenging to the older 
students.
Pong-a-Palooza
Pong-a-Palooza
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Ping Pong Balls
•	
Large Cup
Setup
•	
Partner up children in groups of 2 or 3 at a desk.  Give 
each group a ping pong ball and place a cup in the 
middle of the desk.
Instructions
1.	 Each group will have a cup and a ping pong ball.
2.	 When ready, the first child with the ping pong ball will underhand toss the ball at the cup at the 
desk.
3.	 Their objective is to land the ball into the cup on the desk, whether in the air or on a bounce.
4.	 After they shoot, it is now their partner’s turn.
5.	 If they bounce the ball into the cup, they receive one point.  If they land the ball in the cup with 
no bounces, they will receive two points.
6.	 The first student to ten points wins the round.

sections.Variation:
Have the students shoot the ball with their non-dominant hand or even bounce the ball on the
floor first and have it bounce up to the cup.  This will make it more challenging to the older
students.
Pong-a-Palooza
Pong-a-Palooza
DIFFICULTY

•

•

•

middle of the desk.

1.	 Each group will have a cup and a ping pong ball.
2.	 When ready, the first child with the ping pong ball will underhand toss the ball at the cup at the

3.	 Their objective is to land the ball into the cup on the desk, whether in the air or on a bounce.
4.	 After they shoot, it is now their partner’s turn.
5.	 If they bounce the ball into the cup, they receive one point.  If they land the ball in the cup with
no bounces, they will receive two points.
6.	 The first student to ten points wins the round.

fiches.title: Human Knot Human Knot
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
19
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To make it more challenging, give the class a time limit to complete the tasks.  For example, tell the 
class they have 2 minutes to complete the challenge.
Human Knot
Human Knot
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Arrange the kids in a circle and instruct the children 
to grab hands with two other students. The children 
should not hold both hands with the same person. 
Encourage them to grab hands across the circle, 
creating a big knot rather than a simple circle.
Instructions
1.	 Place the students in a circle. 
2.	 When they are ready, ask them to hold hands.
3.	 They should hold hands of two different students who are NOT directly beside then, so all their 
arms are intwined into a knot.
4.	 On ‘go’ they must now begin to untangle themselves to create a simple circle.
5.	 Their objective is to not let go of hands, but to untangle themselves to form a complete circle 
holding the hands of the ‘new’ person next to them.  They will need to maneuver their bodies 
going over and under various arms and legs.

sections.Variation:
To make it more challenging, give the class a time limit to complete the tasks.  For example, tell the
class they have 2 minutes to complete the challenge.
Human Knot
Human Knot
DIFFICULTY

•
None

•

to grab hands with two other students. The children
should not hold both hands with the same person.

creating a big knot rather than a simple circle.

1.	 Place the students in a circle.
2.	 When they are ready, ask them to hold hands.
3.	 They should hold hands of two different students who are NOT directly beside then, so all their
arms are intwined into a knot.
4.	 On ‘go’ they must now begin to untangle themselves to create a simple circle.
5.	 Their objective is to not let go of hands, but to untangle themselves to form a complete circle
holding the hands of the ‘new’ person next to them.  They will need to maneuver their bodies
going over and under various arms and legs.

fiches.title: Balloon Bonanza Balloon Bonanza
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
20
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Use more than one balloon while participating in this activity to add a degree of difficulty. You 
can also play while seated at your desks. You can also try tapping the balloon using any body part 
except for your hand (soccer rules).
Balloon Bonanza
Balloon Bonanza
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
1 or more filled balloons
Setup
•	
Spread the kids out around the room.  Give one child a 
filled balloon.
Instructions
1.	 Once ready, the child with the balloon will tap the balloon into the air.
2.	 A different student must now tap the balloon back into the air.
3.	 No student can tap the balloon two times in a row.
4.	 Once the balloon starts to come back down, another student must tap the balloon.
5.	 The class should see how many times in a row they can tap the balloon without letting it fall to 
the ground.

sections.Variation:
Use more than one balloon while participating in this activity to add a degree of difficulty. You
can also play while seated at your desks. You can also try tapping the balloon using any body part
except for your hand (soccer rules).
Balloon Bonanza
Balloon Bonanza
DIFFICULTY

•
1 or more filled balloons
sections.Setup:
•

filled balloon.

1.	 Once ready, the child with the balloon will tap the balloon into the air.
2.	 A different student must now tap the balloon back into the air.
3.	 No student can tap the balloon two times in a row.
4.	 Once the balloon starts to come back down, another student must tap the balloon.
5.	 The class should see how many times in a row they can tap the balloon without letting it fall to
the ground.

fiches.title: Mindful Movement Mindful Movement
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
21
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Find a YouTub video with kids’ yoga poses and follow along. You can also look for a “desk yoga” 
video and follow the movements while seated at your desk.
Mindful Movement
Mindful Movement
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Mats (optional) if you have 
them
•	
Calming music
Setup
•	
Move the desks out of the way and spread the kids out 
around the room.
Instructions
1.	 Spread the kids out around the room.
2.	 Give each child a chance to be the class leader. The leader performs a yoga pose or stretch of 
their choice while the class follows the exact movement.
3.	 Have them focus on their breathing and count slowly down from 20 for each movement.
4.	 Appoint a new leader to introduce a yoga move or stretch that must be followed.
5.	 With each new leader, a new yoga pose will be introduced.

sections.Variation:
Find a YouTub video with kids’ yoga poses and follow along. You can also look for a “desk yoga”
video and follow the movements while seated at your desk.
Mindful Movement
Mindful Movement
DIFFICULTY

•
Mats (optional) if you have
them
•
Calming music

•
Move the desks out of the way and spread the kids out
around the room.

1.	 Spread the kids out around the room.
2.	 Give each child a chance to be the class leader. The leader performs a yoga pose or stretch of
their choice while the class follows the exact movement.
3.	 Have them focus on their breathing and count slowly down from 20 for each movement.
4.	 Appoint a new leader to introduce a yoga move or stretch that must be followed.
5.	 With each new leader, a new yoga pose will be introduced.

fiches.title: Defying Gravity Defying Gravity
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
22
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To make it easier, allow the ball to bounce on the floor one time before they catch it.  This will 
increase the success rate of the students who are having difficulty catching it in the air.
Defying Gravity
Defying Gravity
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
A tennis ball for each child
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room and give 
each child a tennis ball.
Instructions
1.	 When ready, they must toss their ball into the air from a stationary position.
2.	 While the ball is in the air, they must clap their hands one time and then catch it before it hits the 
ground.
3.	 Once the skill becomes too easy, the teacher will instruct the class to clap two times and catch it 
before it hits the ground.
4.	 The teacher can continually increase the number of claps for each student depending on the 
skill level of the class or each child.

sections.Variation:
To make it easier, allow the ball to bounce on the floor one time before they catch it.  This will
increase the success rate of the students who are having difficulty catching it in the air.

Defying Gravity

each child a tennis ball.

2.	 While the ball is in the air, they must clap their hands one time and then catch it before it hits the
ground.
3.	 Once the skill becomes too easy, the teacher will instruct the class to clap two times and catch it
before it hits the ground.
4.	 The teacher can continually increase the number of claps for each student depending on the
skill level of the class or each child.

fiches.title: Stack Attack Stack Attack
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
23
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Try to do the activity in complete silence. Students have to communicate using signals and 
gestures only. Provide more cups and see how much higher the towers become.
Stack Attack
Stack Attack
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
100 Plastic Cups
Setup
•	
Divide the class into two even groups and give each 
group 50 cups.  
Instructions
1.	 Once the students are placed in two even groups, give them 50 plastic cups.
2.	 Set a timer for a fixed amount of time.
3.	 On ‘go’ the kids have the objective of building the highest cup tower possible.
4.	 If the cups tip over, they must start over and continue to build.
5.	 They can put as many cups on the base as they want.
6.	 Once completed, the group with the highest tower wins the round.

sections.Variation:
Try to do the activity in complete silence. Students have to communicate using signals and
gestures only. Provide more cups and see how much higher the towers become.
Stack Attack
Stack Attack
DIFFICULTY
sections.Equipment:
•
100 Plastic Cups
sections.Setup:
•
Divide the class into two even groups and give each
group 50 cups.
sections.Instructions:
1.	 Once the students are placed in two even groups, give them 50 plastic cups.
2.	 Set a timer for a fixed amount of time.
3.	 On ‘go’ the kids have the objective of building the highest cup tower possible.
4.	 If the cups tip over, they must start over and continue to build.
5.	 They can put as many cups on the base as they want.
6.	 Once completed, the group with the highest tower wins the round.

fiches.title: Memory Quest Memory Quest
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
24
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To increase the level of difficulty, instead of one repetition of each exercise, have the children 
perform 2-5 reps of each exercise while remembering the correct order of exercises performed.
Memory Quest
Memory Quest
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Have all the children stand in a circle.
Instructions
1.	 Once the kids have formed a circle, designate one child to be the leader.
2.	 The leader will perform an exercise.  For our example, they will do a single jumping jack.
3.	 Moving clockwise, the next child in the circle must do a single jumping jack, then add a new 
exercise.  For our example, they will do a push-up.
4.	 Then, moving clockwise again, the next student will do a jumping jack, push-up, then add their 
new exercise.
5.	 This pattern will continue around the circle.
6.	 If a child gets the memorized pattern incorrect, they must sit down, and the game starts again 
from scratch with a clean slate.
7.	 Follow this pattern until there is one child left who won the round.

sections.Variation:
To increase the level of difficulty, instead of one repetition of each exercise, have the children
perform 2-5 reps of each exercise while remembering the correct order of exercises performed.
Memory Quest
Memory Quest
DIFFICULTY
sections.Equipment:
•
None
sections.Setup:
•
Have all the children stand in a circle.
sections.Instructions:
1.	 Once the kids have formed a circle, designate one child to be the leader.
2.	 The leader will perform an exercise.  For our example, they will do a single jumping jack.
3.	 Moving clockwise, the next child in the circle must do a single jumping jack, then add a new
exercise.  For our example, they will do a push-up.
4.	 Then, moving clockwise again, the next student will do a jumping jack, push-up, then add their
new exercise.
5.	 This pattern will continue around the circle.
6.	 If a child gets the memorized pattern incorrect, they must sit down, and the game starts again
from scratch with a clean slate.
7.	 Follow this pattern until there is one child left who won the round.

fiches.title: Mind Your Melon Mind Your Melon
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
25
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
When the music stops, instead of balancing on one foot, have the kids do a squat or a lunge to 
increase difficulty.
Mind Your Melon
Mind Your Melon
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Bean Bags
•	
Music
Setup
•	
Spread the kids out around the room.  Give each child 
a bean bag and ask them to balance it on top of their 
head.  Move desks and chairs out of the way.
Instructions
1.	 This is an activity to work on balance and coordination skills.
2.	 When the teacher turns on the music, the children must walk around the room balancing a bean 
bag on top of their head without using their hands.
3.	 When the music stops, the children must stop in their spot and now balance on one foot.
4.	 When the music begins again, the children start walking around the room with the bean bag 
balanced on top of their head.
5.	 If a child uses their hands to hold the bean bag in place, or if it drops to the floor, they are 
eliminated and must go sit at their desk.
6.	 The last kids left win the round.

sections.Variation:
When the music stops, instead of balancing on one foot, have the kids do a squat or a lunge to
increase difficulty.
Mind Your Melon
Mind Your Melon
DIFFICULTY
sections.Equipment:
•
Bean Bags
•
Music
sections.Setup:
•
Spread the kids out around the room.  Give each child
a bean bag and ask them to balance it on top of their
head.  Move desks and chairs out of the way.
sections.Instructions:
1.	 This is an activity to work on balance and coordination skills.
2.	 When the teacher turns on the music, the children must walk around the room balancing a bean
bag on top of their head without using their hands.
3.	 When the music stops, the children must stop in their spot and now balance on one foot.
4.	 When the music begins again, the children start walking around the room with the bean bag
balanced on top of their head.
5.	 If a child uses their hands to hold the bean bag in place, or if it drops to the floor, they are
eliminated and must go sit at their desk.
6.	 The last kids left win the round.

fiches.title: Hula Hustle Hula Hustle
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
26
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Time the line to see how fast they can do it.  Then, have them repeat this activity multiple times to 
get the best possible time.
Hula Hustle
Hula Hustle
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Hula hoop
Setup
•	
Have the students form a single line and hold hands.  
The first child in line should be holding a hula hoop.
Instructions
1.	 During this activity, the students must hold hands in their line without letting go.
2.	 When ready, the first child in line will step through the hula hoop in an effort to pass along the 
hula hoop to the next child in line, who will also have to step through it to pass it to the next 
child in line.
3.	 The objective is to pass the hoop from the first person to the last person without letting go of 
their hands.
4.	 Children will have to work together and communicate with each other to move the hoop all the 
way across.

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Dodgeball Games! 
In this resource, we’ve provided 14 fun Dodgeball-based games you can use for PE class, recess, 
field days, summer camps, or after school sports programs. 
Each game is explained with step by step instructions and rules for running the game. Along with 
a detailed diagram and equipment list showing you exactly how to set up the space. The 
activities are appropriate for Kindergarten through 8th grade students, and can be done with 
small groups or large groups, indoors or outdoors. 
Let’s get started!

fiches.title: Crossfire
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Crossfire
Dodgeballs
1.
Mark out playing area as shown. This game is best played indoors with walls 
2.
Split group into 4 even teams 
3.
Players must stay in their square and throw the dodgeball at any opposing player (any direction) 
4.
Players can only be hit from the shoulders down.  
5.
If a player is hit they sit on the bench nearest their teams square. Players can rejoin the game if a team mate catches the ball or the 
teacher says “everyone back in’ 
6.
If a throw is caught, that player is out and sits on the bench.  
7.
After a certain time, the teacher rolls the ‘special dodgeball (give it a name, I call ours ‘bumblebee’ as it is patterned like a bumblebee) 
8.
If a student is hit by the ‘special dodgeball’ that entire team is out. If the ‘special dodgeball’ is caught, the entire throwers team is out.  
9.
The last square left are the winners. 
1 x Special Dodgeball
Setup

fiches.title: Mushroom Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Mushroom Ball
6- 8 Dodgeballs
1.
Arrange playing area as shown, players must stay in boundaries. Inside with walls is best for this game. 
2.
This is an ‘all against all’ dodgeball type game. The object of the game is to throw dodgeballs at other players whilst avoiding getting hit by one, 
having your throw caught or being tagged. . 
3.
To start, throw dodgeballs into the playing area. Students can stand whoever they choose.  
4.
Students cannot run with the dodgeballs once they have collected them, they must throw form where they collect the ball. Running without 
the ball is permitted. 
5.
Players are free to pick up any ball that is close to them. 
6.
If you are hit by a dodgeball or your throw is caught you turn into a ‘mushroom’ (kneeling). Mushrooms can get back in to the game by either 
picking up a ball, throwing and hitting a player that is standing/running. They can also get back into the game by tagging a player running past. If 
a player is tagged by a mushroom, they turn into a mushroom and the tagging player is back to normal.  
7.
Teachers can call ‘all mushrooms back in to the game’ to keep it continuous. 
Setup

fiches.title: Jet BAll
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Jet BAll
Setup
Cones
Setup area as shown. Split class into 4-6 even groups. The aim is to race out and collect as many beanbags as possible from the 
end (safe) zone. The throwers aim to hit runners below the waist, if a throw hits a runner they head back to their group and tag 
the next person in line who races out and tries to collect a beanbag. Any runners that have a beanbag and get hit on the way back 
must throw/place the beanbag back into the end (safe) zone. The team that collects the most are the winners. Tip: have more 
throwers to make it harder or or less to make it easier. This game is best played indoors with walls. If you play outside you can 
have ‘collectors’ on the side lines who throw the balls back in or try to hit runners as well but they cannot move into the field of 
play. 
 Safe Zone
Thrower
Thrower
Hula Hoops

must throw/place the beanbag back into the end (safe) zone. The team that collects the most are the winners. Tip: have more

fiches.title: Tag Ball Turbo
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Tag Ball Turbo
In this game there are two teams. The ‘Taggers and the ‘Dodgers’. The aim of the taggers is to tag a student 
with a ball in hand. The aim for the dodgers is to dodge the taggers for as long as possible. Taggers cannot run 
with the ball but can run without the ball to call for a pass to tag a ‘dodger’ (emphasise moving without the ball) 
After 3 tags teams switch roles. To make it easier to tag have more balls and a smaller playing area. To make it 
easier to dodge have less balls and a larger playing area.
Foam Balls (For Tagging Team)
= dodgers
= taggers

with a ball in hand. The aim for the dodgers is to dodge the taggers for as long as possible. Taggers cannot run
with the ball but can run without the ball to call for a pass to tag a ‘dodger’ (emphasise moving without the ball)
After 3 tags teams switch roles. To make it easier to tag have more balls and a smaller playing area. To make it
easier to dodge have less balls and a larger playing area.
Foam Balls (For Tagging Team)
= dodgers
= taggers

fiches.title: Tag Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Tag Ball
1 x Tall Cone each
In this game taggers try to tag students with pool noodles. If tagged a student must stand still and call 
for the ball from one of the ‘throwers’. If caught the player is released (and is the new thrower) and both 
catcher and thrower score a point. If dropped, the tagged person still becomes the new thrower but no 
point is scored for either student. Rule: Throwers cannot run with the ball. Mix up the roles of student 
regulars and add ore taggers and throwers if needed. Make sure students are not getting tagged on 
purpose, if so no points awarded. If a students is not tagged through entire game (10 points) is awarded. 
Foam Balls (For Throwers)
Pool Noodles for taggers

point is scored for either student. Rule: Throwers cannot run with the ball. Mix up the roles of student
regulars and add ore taggers and throwers if needed. Make sure students are not getting tagged on
purpose, if so no points awarded. If a students is not tagged through entire game (10 points) is awarded.

fiches.title: Pinball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Pinball
Setup area as shown. This game is best played indoors. This game is a variation on dodgeball. Normal dodgeball rules apply. Players 
aim to throw the ball at the opposition, if they are hit they below the waist they are out and do not come back in until a 
teammate catches the ball or the teachers says ‘everyone back in’. if hit in the head students are not out unless they duck into 
an otherwise good throw. Students may also try and knock the balls of the oppositions ‘pins’ at the end of their zone. A ball that 
is knocked off cannot be placed back on. The winning team is the team that knocks down all balls of the oppositions pins. 
Students may protect the pins but if they are hit, and don’t catch the ball, they are out. Progression: Choose one ‘pinkeeper who 
is the only one allowed to block the pins (if they are hit they are not out), mark off this area and nobody else is allowed into the 
zone. 
Dodgeballs

sections.Setup:
Pinball
Setup area as shown. This game is best played indoors. This game is a variation on dodgeball. Normal dodgeball rules apply. Players
aim to throw the ball at the opposition, if they are hit they below the waist they are out and do not come back in until a
teammate catches the ball or the teachers says ‘everyone back in’. if hit in the head students are not out unless they duck into
an otherwise good throw. Students may also try and knock the balls of the oppositions ‘pins’ at the end of their zone. A ball that
is knocked off cannot be placed back on. The winning team is the team that knocks down all balls of the oppositions pins.
Students may protect the pins but if they are hit, and don’t catch the ball, they are out. Progression: Choose one ‘pinkeeper who
is the only one allowed to block the pins (if they are hit they are not out), mark off this area and nobody else is allowed into the
zone.
Dodgeballs

fiches.title: Battleships
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Battleships
Make a large playing area with a no go zone that is approximately 5-10 metres wide. In this game you can play it as 
a throwing game with dodgeballs or a kicking game with soccer balls. The aim of the game on the teachers call is 
to throw/kick balls into the opposing teams side (ship). Any balls that are kicked ‘overboard’ (out of bounds) or 
are caught between the two ships cannot be collected. The team that has the least amount of balls on their ship 
wins (teacher determines time limit). Students cannot cross over to the other teams ship.
NO GO ZONE
Dodgeballs / Foam Balls / Soccer Balls
Cones

are caught between the two ships cannot be collected. The team that has the least amount of balls on their ship
wins (teacher determines time limit). Students cannot cross over to the other teams ship.
NO GO ZONE

Cones

fiches.title: Target Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Target Ball
Setup area as shown with hula hoops scattered around the playing area. Students find a partner and stand behind a blue 
throwing cone. The object of this game is to collect as many tall Cones as possible. On teachers command one of the students 
from each pair races out and stands inside a hula hoop. Their partner aims to throw them a ball so they can successfully catch 
the ball with at least one foot inside the hoop. If successful, they bring the tall Cone inside that hoop back to their throwing area. 
If dropped or an inaccurate catch, they collect the ball and switch with their partner. The winning group is the one with the most 
tall Cones at the end of the game. Variation: Extend or shorten playing area, use larger or smaller balls, only two feet inside for a 
successful catch. 
Dodgeballs / Foam Balls / Tennis balls
Cones
Tall Cones
Hula Hoops

sections.Setup:
Target Ball
Setup area as shown with hula hoops scattered around the playing area. Students find a partner and stand behind a blue
throwing cone. The object of this game is to collect as many tall Cones as possible. On teachers command one of the students
from each pair races out and stands inside a hula hoop. Their partner aims to throw them a ball so they can successfully catch
the ball with at least one foot inside the hoop. If successful, they bring the tall Cone inside that hoop back to their throwing area.
If dropped or an inaccurate catch, they collect the ball and switch with their partner. The winning group is the one with the most
tall Cones at the end of the game. Variation: Extend or shorten playing area, use larger or smaller balls, only two feet inside for a
successful catch.
Dodgeballs / Foam Balls / Tennis balls
Cones
Tall Cones
Hula Hoops

fiches.title: Doctor Dodge
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Doctor Dodge
Setup dodgeball area with normal dodgeball rules. In this game there is one ‘doctor’ for each team. Split into two 
teams and have students huddle to nominate a ‘doctor’. If a student is hit below the shoulders they are ‘wounded’ 
and kneel/ They can still be involved in the game as a thrower and catcher but they cannot run around. If the 
doctor from their team tags them on the shoulder they are now ‘treated’ and are back in the game as per 
normal. If the Doctor is hit, they kneel and can no longer ‘treat’ others on their team. The winning team is the 
team that can get all of the other team ‘wounded’ and kneeling. 
Dodgeballs
Indicates the ‘Doctor’

Doctor Dodge

teams and have students huddle to nominate a ‘doctor’. If a student is hit below the shoulders they are ‘wounded’
and kneel/ They can still be involved in the game as a thrower and catcher but they cannot run around. If the
doctor from their team tags them on the shoulder they are now ‘treated’ and are back in the game as per
normal. If the Doctor is hit, they kneel and can no longer ‘treat’ others on their team. The winning team is the
team that can get all of the other team ‘wounded’ and kneeling.
Dodgeballs
Indicates the ‘Doctor’

fiches.title: Clear The decks
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Clear The decks
Setup large playing area with a large inner circle and a large outer area. The inner circle is called the ‘deck 
and the large outer circle is called the ‘water’. Choose 6 or so students to start on the ‘deck’. The aim of 
this game is for students on the deck to throw all dodgeballs / tennis balls / beanbags etc out into the 
water and clear their deck. The students in the water are called ‘sharks and collect the balls and race or roll 
them back to the deck. This is a continuous game, give everyone a chance to be on the deck. 
Dodgeballs/ Beanbags, Tennis balls
Large circle area

water and clear their deck. The students in the water are called ‘sharks and collect the balls and race or roll

fiches.title: Ball Tag
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Ball Tag
Setup
1.
This game can be played indoors or outdoors.  
2.
This is chasing and fleeing type game.  
3.
Choose two students to be taggers who start with a dodgebal and pinnies/bibs. The aim is for the taggers to 
‘tag’ (ball in hand) the dodgers. If a dodger is tagged they become part of the ‘tagging team’ and put on a 
pinnie/bib 
4.
Taggers cannot run with the ball (pivoting is allowed), they can only run without the ball.  
5.
Taggers cannot throw the ball at a dodger, they need to tag them anywhere below the shoulder with the ball 
6.
Progressions: Increase or decrease the playing area, add more balls, start with a 4-5 taggers. 
Dodgeball/s
Tagger
Tagger
Pinnies/Bibs

fiches.title: War Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
War Ball 
Setup
1.
This game is best played indoors with walls, setup as shown 
2.
Split group into 2 even teams.  
3.
The aim is to throw a ball at opposition team. If  a student is hit below the shoulders, or if their throw is caught, they go to ‘jail’ on 
the opposite side. Once in jail you can only escape and head back if you catch a ball thrown from your team mates. When heading 
back to the their team a player cannot be hit with a ball. All other dodgeball rules apply. To win: Send all opponents to jail at same 
time.  
Progression: Add 2 large mats on each side that each teams use a blocker, only team people can hold it up at a time, if they are hit they 
have 5  seconds to call someone else to hold it If at any time a mat hits the ground the other team wins (students have to 
communicate).  
Jail

Jail
4-6 x large mats or Cones

opposite side. Once in jail you can only escape and head back if you catch a ball thrown from your team mates or someone rescues you by 
running across and collecting you with a hula hoop. Once rescued you can head back unimpeded to your side.  
4.
Designate 1 or 2 people to be taggers per team who have a pool noodle each.. They can throw and also tag any opponents who try and cross 
over and perform a rescue. Once tagged, players must head back to their own side.  
5.
All other dodgeball rules apply. To win: Send all opponents to jail at same time.  
Progression: Add more mats as jails for both sides

fiches.title: Teacher Instructions
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
3
Teacher Instructions
Difficulty Level: This activity is appropriate for kids of all ages. Younger kids 
may need some help or hints from the teacher to solve the Brain Games. 
Learning Objectives
Students will improve their social-emotional skills, cognitive skills, teamwork, coordination, strength, 
agility and endurance. 
Equipment Needed
•	
You do not need any sports equipment to complete the fitness challenges. Some challenges use 
plastic Easter Eggs, so have plenty of extra on hand.
•	
Per the instructions below, you will need the printed game sheets for the students, an Easter Basket, 
and at least 12 plastic Easter Eggs.
Setup
1.	 Divide your class up into teams of 4-8 students. If you are working with a smaller group, you can 
make a single team. Or have students compete individually.
2.	 For each team, print out 1 copy of each Brain Game (5 sheets per team) and 1 Completion Certificate 
(on the final page).
3.	 Print and cut out the Fitness Challenges you want to use. Fold and place each one inside a plastic 
egg. Put all the plastic eggs into an Easter Basket or similar container.
In this Easter themed PE activity, students will be given an Escape Room scenario. An evil Easter 
Bunny has stolen all of their Easter Eggs! To get them back, they must complete a series of Brain 
Games and Fitness Challenges.
INTRODUCTION
THE GREAT EGG-SCAPE

(on the final page).
3.	 Print and cut out the Fitness Challenges you want to use. Fold and place each one inside a plastic
egg. Put all the plastic eggs into an Easter Basket or similar container.
In this Easter themed PE activity, students will be given an Escape Room scenario. An evil Easter
Bunny has stolen all of their Easter Eggs! To get them back, they must complete a series of Brain
Games and Fitness Challenges.

THE GREAT EGG-SCAPE

fiches.title: TENNIS BALL FACTS
fiches.body:
TENNIS BALL FACTS
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
How to Play
1.	 Call the students into the middle of the room and read out the Student Instructions below.
2.	 The object of the game is to complete a series of Brain Games and Fitness Challenges to recover the 
stolen Easter Eggs. Teams will compete to be the first ones to complete the challenges.
3.	 To start, each team will get a copy of Brain Game #1. Keep the other Brain Games with you for later.
4.	 Once they solve Brain Game #1, the students will bring the completed puzzle up to to the teacher. If 
their solution is correct, they get to pick one Easter Egg out of the Easter Basket. Open up the Easter 
Egg to reveal the Fitness Challenge.
5.	 All members of the team must complete this challenge. When the Fitness Challenge is complete, 
the students return the egg to the teacher to receive Brain Game #2. Put the egg back in the basket 
for other teams to use.
6.	 Again, they complete the puzzle, then trade it in to receive a new Fitness Challenge from the Easter 
Basket. This process continues until they complete all 5 Brain Games and 5 Fitness Challenges.
7.	 At this point, the team has found all the stolen Easter Eggs and won the game. Give them one of the 
printed Completion Certificates as their reward. 
Modifications
•	
To make the puzzles easier, you can partially fill in the solutions.
•	
If the kids are unable to solve the puzzles, allow them to approach you and receive clues. 
•	
For a longer game, you can have the teams pick 2 Fitness Challenges from the Easter Basket after 
each Brain Game is complete. All team members will complete both Fitness Challenges in order to 
proceed.
Student Instructions
Read these out loud to explain the game to your students.
•	
Oh no! An evil Easter Bunny has stolen all your Easter Eggs! To get them back, you’ll need to solve 
a series of Brain Games and Fitness Challenges. 
•	
Begin by solving Brain Game #1 and bringing the completed puzzle to your teacher. If it’s correct, 
you will get to pick one Fitness Challenge out of the Easter Basket.
•	
Every member of your team must complete this Fitness Challenge to advance to the next stage.
•	
Once it’s complete, return to your teacher to receive Brain Game #2. Solve that and bring the 
completed puzzle to your teacher. If it’s correct, you will get to pick a second Fitness Challenge 
out of the Easter Basket.
•	
Complete all 5 challenges to recover the stolen Easter Eggs and win the game!
Teacher Instructions
THE GREAT EGG-SCAPE

middle cone.

fiches.title: Bucket Brigade
fiches.body:
10
Instructions:
This is a team event with the objective of filling up a large barrel with water before the 
other team.  Each team must form a line between their large barrel filled with water and 
their empty barrel of the same size.  On ‘go’ the first kid in line near the barrel filled 
with water will dip the smaller bucket into the water to fill the bucket with water.  Once 
they do, they pass it to the next kid in line, who proceeds to pass it to the next kid until 
it gets passed all the way to the end.  The last kid in line must pour the bucket of water 
into the empty barrel.  After 2 minutes, whichever team filled their empty bucket with 
the most water wins.
Bucket Brigade
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Beach Ball Bounce
fiches.body:
12
Instructions:
At this event, your team is going to tap a beach ball into the air as many times as you 
can without hitting the ground.  No student may tap the ball 2 times in a row, nor can 
students create a ‘safe’ pattern to hit the ball.  The beach ball must be hit significantly 
into the air.  The team that can get the most successful taps in a row wins.
Beach Ball Bounce
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Water Balloon Toss
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
13
Instructions:
In this event, children will partner up and stand about 2 feet apart.  On ‘go’ one partner 
will toss a water balloon to their partner.  The partner will attempt to catch the balloon. 
If it drops and breaks, the group is eliminated.  If successful, the partners will now stand 
3 feet apart and continue the process.  With each successful catch, the partners will 
move back 1 foot until the last group left wins.
Water Balloon Toss

fiches.title: Hula Hoop Chain
fiches.body:
14
Instructions:
In this activity, each team will form a circle holding hands.  Each team should have 
an even amount of children.  Two students on each team will start with a hula hoop 
between their arms (as seen in image below).  On ‘go’ each team must move the hoop 
from the first student in the circle all the way around the circle until it gets back to the 
first student again.  Once the race starts, the circle of hand holding must never break, 
which means the teams must never let go of their hands of their neighboring partner 
or else they are disqualified.  To make the hoop move around the circle, children must 
step through it with their entire body without letting go of their partners hand.
Hula Hoop Chain
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
14

students will begin to turn and spin their hoops.  If it drops to the floor or if they use 
their hands to hold it up, they are eliminated.  If no winner after a few minutes, you can 
declare it a tie between all the kids left.

fiches.title: Fitness Bingo
fiches.body:
Fitness Bingo
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Welcome to Fitness Bingo! This resource provides you with a complete, printable kit 
you can use to inject some fun and excitement into your next PE class, recess, brain 
break or fitness activity.
What’s Included
Fitness Bingo includes the following printable components:
• Instructor Call Sheet with 30 different exercise tasks
• Medal Markers to use on Bingo Cards
• 30 different Fitness Bingo cards for students to use. Each Bingo Card includes 24 
exercise task squares and one Free Choice square. 
Instructions
Fitness Bingo can be used as an in-person activity or a remote learning activity.
Option 1: In-Person Activity
1. Print out the Instructor Call Sheet. Cut out the 30 individual exercise tasks. Fold 
them and place into a hat or bowl. 
2. Print and laminate the Fitness Bingo Cards, then distribute one to each student. 
3. Print out one page of Medal Markers for each student. Cut out the individual 
markers and distribute. You can also use poker chips, or have students mark the 
laminated sheets with dry erase marker.
4. Have your students spread out with enough space to stretch out their arms and 
legs.
5. Begin the game by pulling one exercise task from the hat or bowl. Call out the 
task, then demonstrate it for your students. Any student who has that square on 
their card will also complete the exercise (ie. “Run in place for 20 seconds!”) 
6. Once completed, the student marks that square as completed.
7. Continue pulling out new exercise tasks in this fashion. The game is over when one 
student completes a row, column or diagonal on their card. 
8. To begin a new game, have the students exchange cards, then start again.
(Continued…)

sections.Instructions:
Fitness Bingo can be used as an in-person activity or a remote learning activity.
Option 1: In-Person Activity
1. Print out the Instructor Call Sheet. Cut out the 30 individual exercise tasks. Fold
them and place into a hat or bowl.
2. Print and laminate the Fitness Bingo Cards, then distribute one to each student.
3. Print out one page of Medal Markers for each student. Cut out the individual
markers and distribute. You can also use poker chips, or have students mark the
laminated sheets with dry erase marker.
4. Have your students spread out with enough space to stretch out their arms and
legs.
5. Begin the game by pulling one exercise task from the hat or bowl. Call out the
task, then demonstrate it for your students. Any student who has that square on
their card will also complete the exercise (ie. “Run in place for 20 seconds!”)
6. Once completed, the student marks that square as completed.
7. Continue pulling out new exercise tasks in this fashion. The game is over when one
student completes a row, column or diagonal on their card.
8. To begin a new game, have the students exchange cards, then start again.
(Continued…)

fiches.title: Fitness Bingo
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Instructions (Continued)
Option 2: Remote Learning Activity
1. Digitally distribute this file to your class. 
Please note the file can be shared on a password protected site or emailed 
directly to your students, but cannot be posted publicly online.
2. Assign each student a specific Bingo Card and ask then to print it out before class. 
For example:
• Mason: Print Card 1
• Victoria: Print Card 2
• Liam: Print Card 3 
• etc.
3. Print out the Instructor Call Sheet. Cut out the 30 individual exercise prompts, fold 
them and place into a hat or bowl. 
4. Each student should be prepared with a Bingo Card, a pen or marker, 
comfortable clothing and space to exercise
5. Begin the game by pulling one exercise task from the hat or bowl. Call out the 
task, then demonstrate it for your students. Any student who has that square on 
their card will also complete the exercise (ie. “Run in place for 20 seconds!”) 
6. Once completed, the student marks that square as completed.
7. Continue pulling out new exercise tasks in this fashion. The game is over when one 
student completes a row, column or diagonal on his or her card. 
Tips
• There is one exercise task labeled “Free Choice.” This allows the students to perform 
any physical activity they want for 20 seconds. Anything goes!
• Try putting on some fun, up-beat music during the game. Let the winner of the 
game pick the next artist or song!
Fitness Bingo

fiches.title: Log Roll
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
1
Instructions:
Lie down flat on the mat.  Place your hands either at your side or 
above your head.  When ready, simply roll across the mat on your 
side.  Try to go straight so you do not roll off the mat.
Log Roll

fiches.title: Forward Roll
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
Instructions:
Start in a standing position with your hands out in front of you.  
Slowly crouch down into a baseball catcher position while placing 
your hands on the mat.  When ready, tuck your chin down to 
your chest and push forward.  Commit to rolling forward as you 
tuck your knees up.  As you propel yourself forward, make your 
body into a ball and roll forward.
Forward Roll

sections.Instructions:
Start in a standing position with your hands out in front of you.
Slowly crouch down into a baseball catcher position while placing
your hands on the mat.  When ready, tuck your chin down to
your chest and push forward.  Commit to rolling forward as you
tuck your knees up.  As you propel yourself forward, make your
body into a ball and roll forward.
Forward Roll

fiches.title: Log Roll
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
Instructions:
Lie down flat on the mat.  Place your hands either at your side or 
above your head.  When ready, simply roll across the mat on your 
side.  Try to go straight so you do not roll off the mat.
Log Roll

sections.Instructions:
Lie down flat on the mat.  Place your hands either at your side or
above your head.  When ready, simply roll across the mat on your
side.  Try to go straight so you do not roll off the mat.
Log Roll

fiches.title: Backward Roll
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
8
Instructions:
Start in a standing position with your hands out in front of you, 
facing backwards on the mat.  Slowly crouch down into a baseball 
catcher position, lifting your arms up so your palms are facing 
up.  Once ready, begin to sit as you make your body into a little 
ball (chin touching chest, knees bent and up).  Roll backwards, 
building up speed and momentum to propel your body over and 
back to the starting position. Have a friend help you if you get 
stuck.
Backward Roll

sections.Instructions:
Start in a standing position with your hands out in front of you,
facing backwards on the mat.  Slowly crouch down into a baseball
catcher position, lifting your arms up so your palms are facing
up.  Once ready, begin to sit as you make your body into a little
ball (chin touching chest, knees bent and up).  Roll backwards,
building up speed and momentum to propel your body over and
back to the starting position. Have a friend help you if you get
stuck.
Backward Roll

fiches.title: Headstand
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
9
Instructions:
Lie down flat on the mat.  Place your hands either at your side or 
above your head.  When ready, simply roll across the mat on your 
side.  Try to go straight so you do not roll off the mat.
Headstand

sections.Instructions:

above your head.  When ready, simply roll across the mat on your

fiches.title: Handstand
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
10
Instructions:
From a standing position, step forward as you bring your hands 
down to the mat.  Your arms and hands will eventually support 
your entire body upside down.  Once your hands touch the mat 
you will whip your feet upwards in a controlled movement.  From 
this point, try to balance on both hands. Do this against a wall, 
using the wall against your heels for support if you need help 
balancing.
Handstand

sections.Instructions:
From a standing position, step forward as you bring your hands
down to the mat.  Your arms and hands will eventually support
your entire body upside down.  Once your hands touch the mat
you will whip your feet upwards in a controlled movement.  From
this point, try to balance on both hands. Do this against a wall,
using the wall against your heels for support if you need help
balancing.
Handstand

fiches.title: Cartwheel
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
11
Instructions:
Standing tall, bend and place one hand on the floor followed by 
the other hand.  From there, you begin to roll over sideways in the 
shape of the letter X as your feet follow the 2 hands touching the 
ground.
Cartwheel

sections.Instructions:
Standing tall, bend and place one hand on the floor followed by
the other hand.  From there, you begin to roll over sideways in the
shape of the letter X as your feet follow the 2 hands touching the
ground.
Cartwheel

fiches.title: Roundoff
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
Instructions:
A roundoff is very similar to a cartwheel, but as your feet come 
over the top, they both come together as you turn at the hip.  
When the feet come down, they come to the mat together, 
instead of one after the other.
Roundoff

sections.Instructions:
A roundoff is very similar to a cartwheel, but as your feet come
over the top, they both come together as you turn at the hip.
When the feet come down, they come to the mat together,
instead of one after the other.

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
13
Instructions:
From a sitting position, bring your hands over your head onto the 
mat so your body is supported by your hands and feet.  Slowly 
and gently arch your back as shown in the image.
Bridge

sections.Instructions:
From a sitting position, bring your hands over your head onto the

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
14
Instructions:
Maintain your balance as you walk forward on the balance beam.  
Go slow at first until you get comfortable. Hold your arms out to 
your sides for balance.
Forward Walk Beam

Go slow at first until you get comfortable. Hold your arms out to

Walk slowly and focus as 
you go backwards on the 
balance beam.  Take small 
and slow steps until you 
feel comfortable going 
backwards. Hold your 
arms out to your sides for 
balance.

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
16
Instructions:
On a low balance beam, with one foot in front of the other, slowly 
squat down, with your arms out to either side for balance. Once 
comfortable, try to slowly extend your front leg further in front of 
you while maintaining your balance.
Forward Leg 
Squat Extension

18

Try to do a small jump on the beam and land safely while 
maintaining your balance.  See how many times you can do it in a

fiches.body:

19
Instructions:
With teacher supervision, try to support your body on the high 
bar as shown in the image below. You can also hang with your 
arms straight above your body. Carefully hold this position and 
then safely return to the mat.
High Bar Hang

fiches.title: Split
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
21
Instructions:
From a standing position, begin to slide your feet apart as your 
mid-region comes close to the floor as shown in the image.  Try 
not to go down too fast, and listen to your body.  This means do 
not force yourself all the way down if you feel any discomfort.
Split

With a running start, approach the horse and place both hands 
on handles for support.  Next, propel your body sideways as you 
leap over the horse.

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Large Group Games! 
In this resource, we’ve provided 24 fun games designed for groups of 12 or more. You can use 
these games for PE class, recess, field days, summer camps, or after school sports programs. Many 
of them can also be used with smaller groups with some minor modifications. 
Each game is explained with step by step instructions and rules for running the game. Along with 
a detailed diagram and equipment list showing you exactly how to set up the space. The 
activities are appropriate for Kindergarten through 8th grade students, and can be done indoors 
or outdoors. 
Let’s get started!

fiches.title: Rob the Nest
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Rob the Nest
Setup
x 1
x 4
Split group into 4 teams. Place beanbags in the middle circle. On the teachers whistle students 
race one at a time to collect ONE beanbag and bring it back to their groups ‘nest’ and tag the 
next student in line. After all beanbags have been taken from the middle, students may start 
‘stealing’ from other teams ‘nests’. The team with the most beanbags after a designated time 
are the winners. 
beanbags

sections.Setup:
x 1
x 4
Split group into 4 teams. Place beanbags in the middle circle. On the teachers whistle students
race one at a time to collect ONE beanbag and bring it back to their groups ‘nest’ and tag the
next student in line. After all beanbags have been taken from the middle, students may start
‘stealing’ from other teams ‘nests’. The team with the most beanbags after a designated time
are the winners.
beanbags

fiches.title: Dribble Knockout
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Dribble Knockout
1 Basketball for each student
In this game students students each have a basketball. The aim is to try and 
protect your basketball and knock others ball out bounds. No body contact allowed. 
Start with the whole basketball court, then reduce the playing area. 
Setup

fiches.title: Hoop IslandS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Hoop IslandS
Assorted Hula Hoops
Spread Hula Hoops in playing area. On teacher command student run around 
playing area. Teacher calls out a number and students must try and get into 
a hula hoops with that many students (each place one foot inside hoop). The 
first group to do so scores a point.
Setup

fiches.title: Rock, Paper, Scissors Snake
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Rock, Paper, Scissors Snake
Start
Finish
8 x Agility Poles
All students being at the start line. They play anyone in a game of rock, paper, scissors. If they 
win, they move to the next pole, if they lose they turn and face someone and play another 
game. The aim is to get to the last pole and the race back to the start. Count how many laps 
you can get in. If a person is at a pole by themselves at the back with nobody to play, they do 
5 star jumps and run to the next pole. 
Setup

fiches.title: Team Tic Tac Toe
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Team Tic Tac Toe
Setup as shown with groups of 3-4 students. The game begins with the first person in line racing out and placing 
their shirt/Cone inside a hula hoop, they then race back and tag the next person in line who does the same. The 
game continues until one team gets 3 in a row. Variations. Change locomotor, add more hula hoops and make it a 
connect 4 type game, add a skill component students have to perform before they put a shirt in such as make 
basketball shot, throw a ball into a bucket etc
Shirts or Cones to designate each team
9 x Hula Hoops

sections.Setup:
Team Tic Tac Toe
Setup as shown with groups of 3-4 students. The game begins with the first person in line racing out and placing
their shirt/Cone inside a hula hoop, they then race back and tag the next person in line who does the same. The
game continues until one team gets 3 in a row. Variations. Change locomotor, add more hula hoops and make it a
connect 4 type game, add a skill component students have to perform before they put a shirt in such as make
basketball shot, throw a ball into a bucket etc
Shirts or Cones to designate each team
9 x Hula Hoops

fiches.title: Peg Relay
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Peg Relay
Setup on tennis courts or with portable tennis nets. Students partner up and line up along the baseline on either 
side. On teachers command the first student races out and places their teams peg on the bottom section of the 
net, they then run back and tag their partner. The partner runs out and moves the peg up one section of the net. 
This continues until their peg has reached the top section of the net. The team that does this first are the 
winners. 
Setup
Net
Pegs
Cones

sections.Setup:
Net
Pegs
Cones

fiches.title: Clumps
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Clumps
Scatter hula hoops across the playing area. When music start or on teachers command students begin 
travelling around the area without going inside the hula hoops. When the music stops/teachers whistle the 
teacher calls out a number e.g “3”. Students must try and group with 3 students with one foot inside a hula 
hoop. Students cannot hover together or hang near hoops. The team that does it first score a point each. 
The winners are the students with the highest points after a designated time period. Variations: Change 
locomotor, have different colored hoops so you call a number plus a color.
Hula Hoops

sections.Setup:
Clumps
Scatter hula hoops across the playing area. When music start or on teachers command students begin
travelling around the area without going inside the hula hoops. When the music stops/teachers whistle the
teacher calls out a number e.g “3”. Students must try and group with 3 students with one foot inside a hula
hoop. Students cannot hover together or hang near hoops. The team that does it first score a point each.
The winners are the students with the highest points after a designated time period. Variations: Change
locomotor, have different colored hoops so you call a number plus a color.
Hula Hoops

fiches.title: Relay MAdness
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Relay MAdness
In this game split class into teams of approx 3-4. Students simply race around the agility Cone and back 
and tag their team mates. The winning team is the team that has everyone go twice. Variations: Mix up the 
locomotor, have students perform a skill or fitness exercise when they get to the agility pole e.g 10 
starjumps or throw a ball to a target like a frisbee catcher etc.
Agility Poles

sections.Setup:
Relay MAdness
In this game split class into teams of approx 3-4. Students simply race around the agility Cone and back
and tag their team mates. The winning team is the team that has everyone go twice. Variations: Mix up the
locomotor, have students perform a skill or fitness exercise when they get to the agility pole e.g 10
starjumps or throw a ball to a target like a frisbee catcher etc.
Agility Poles

fiches.title: Zone Catches
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Zone Catches
Split the class into teams of approximately 4 students. The aim of this game is to successfully catch a pass from 
your team mate. Students can choose which zone they want to receive from. If they catch a pass in that zone 
they add that many points to their teams total. If a dropped catch or bad throw, no points are scored. The 
team with the highest total are the winners. Variations: increase or decrease distance, add more zones, change 
size of ball, add a ‘interceptor for each tea, who tries to intercept other teams throws, change type of catch e.g 
one handed or allow one bounce. 
Tennis balls, beanbags or similar
1 Point
2 Points
3 Points
4 Points

team with the highest total are the winners. Variations: increase or decrease distance, add more zones, change
size of ball, add a ‘interceptor for each tea, who tries to intercept other teams throws, change type of catch e.g
one handed or allow one bounce.

fiches.title: Rapid Fire
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Rapid Fire 
Setup
1.
Mark out playing area as shown. 
2.
Split class in to 2 teams, batters and fielders 
3.
Have 3 batters from the batting team hit a ball out in to the field at the same time. The rest of the batting team 
is lined up at each end of the wickets. Once the batters have hit the balls, they try and make as many runs back 
and forth between the wickets before the fielders place the balls back on the tee. Runners must tag their team 
mates before they can run 
4.
Switch over once everyone has had a bat.  
5.
Progression: Fielding team can score points for their team by catching a ball on the full. Use baseball bats and tees.

fiches.title: Corner Soccer
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Corner Soccer 
Setup
1.
Mark out playing area as shown. 
2.
Split class in to 4 even teams, each student get a number from 1-5 etc. When a players number is called they play 
the game against players from opposing teams with the same numbers. . Teacher can call multiple numbers.  
3.
No bumping or body contact is allowed, goalkeeping is permitted (feet only) 
4.
The aim of the game is to score a goal against any of the four opposing goals (benches). If a player scores a goal 
they receive a point for their team and all players return to their team behind the goals.  
5.
Progression: Call more numbers, add goalkeepers.. 
Bench x 4
Soccer Ball

fiches.title: Bench Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Bench Ball 
Setup
1.
Mark out playing area as shown. 
2.
Split class in to 2 even teams or 4 teams or more if you want less players per game 
3.
The aim is to get all of your team members standing on the bench before the other team 
4.
Have on person per team stand on each bench, this is their ‘goal’. Teams have to try and get the ball to their ‘catcher’ on the bench who 
successfully catches it. If a successful catch is made, the passer joins the ‘catcher’ on the bench to make it two people. The ‘catcher tries to 
then pass back in to the field of play to a team mate who again tries to get it back to anyone on the bench.  
5.
No dribbling, 3 passes must be made before throwing to a person on the bench (except if a pass is coming from a ‘catcher e.g you can 
immediately pass back and join the bench.  
6.
Players can intercept but not steal from opponents hands. Ball on ground = possession change.  
7.
No defenders allowed to guard the goals, mark off an area if needed. 
Bench x 2
Basketball (or similar)

fiches.title: End Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
End Ball 
Setup
1.
This game is best played indoors with walls, setup as shown 
2.
The aim of the game is to make as may runs as possible. Players score a run by running to the ‘end base’ and making it back to ‘home base’. 
3.
Play begins with the pitcher rolling the ball to the kicker. The kicker attempts to kick the ball out into the field of play and run the the ‘end 
base’.  Fielders collect the ball and try to throw it at the kicker who is running to the ‘end base’. If hit or tagged with the ball below the waist 
the kicker is out. They are also out if the ball is caught. If the kicker makes it safely to the ‘ end base’ they can either stay there or attempt 
to run to the ‘home base’ . Each time a player gets to ‘home base’ they score a run. Play either 3 out and side away or everyone gets a kick 
before rotating. 
4.
Progressions: Catches off the wall are out, maximum of 3 players at the ‘end base’ if there is 3 on they all must run. Have 2-3 students kick 
and run at the same time.  
Pitcher
Kicker
End Base
Home Base
2 x Mats

fiches.title: Crazy Baseball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Crazy Baseball
Setup
S
S
S
Cones / Polyspots (2 colors)
1.
This is a modified version of baseball/teeball/softball. Split your class into two teams. One team is the fielding team (who are each standing on a 
red polyspot above). The other is the striking team who use the pink polyspots. 
2.
The aim of the batting team is to hit the ball out into the field of play and run around (touching each pink base with their foot) as many bases 
as possible. The fielding team aim to stop the ball and throw it to the closest “stop’ base (indicated by an “S”). When a student at a stop base 
catches the ball the fielders yell ‘stop’. The runner must stop and head back to the last base they touched before the fielders yelled stop.. 
3.
The next batter takes their turn and all batting team students that are on a base run if a successful hit has occurred. Once the fielders get 
the ball to a ‘stop’ base and yell freeze all runners must stop and go back to the last base they touched. Overtaking is not allowed.  
4.
Fielders rotate clockwise to a different polyspot / Cone after every hit. 
5.
Any catches = out and a point for the fielding team .  
6.
Batters cannot hit a ball behind the ‘foul line’.  
7.
Every students gets a bat before rotating teams. 
Foul Line

fiches.title: Long Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Long Ball
Setup
Teeball stand and ball
1.
This is a modified version of baseball/teeball/softball. Split your class into two teams. One team is the fielding team. The other is the striking 
team. 
2.
The aim of the batting team is to hit the ball in to the field of play and successfully reach their ‘end base’ (marked off with Cones or a mat) 
without being tagged by the fielding team.  
3.
The fielding team aim to tag the runners either by tagging them with the ball in hand, or by using an underarm throw and hitting the runner 
below the knees (use a soft ball if incorporating this rule). If the runner reaches the crossing line of the ‘end base’ area they are safe.  
4.
To score a run the batters must make it back to their striking area without being tagged. Only 1 batter can be in the ‘end base’ area at a time 
5.
Fielders can tag runners on their way back.  
6.
If caught the batter is out and anyone on the ‘end base’ must go back. and wait for the next batter. .  
7.
Play either 3 out and side away, or let the whole team bat through regardless of outs. 
Foul Line
Crossing Line
Cones
End base

fiches.title: 4  way capture the flag
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
4 way capture the flag
Setup
1.
Set out playing area as shown and group students into 4 teams. The larger area, the more students can be used per team.  
2.
The object of the game is to collect all 6 of your teams color beanbags / flags from the opposing teams ‘bases’ which are ‘safe 
zones’ You cannot be tagged in these bases / safe zones, even if it is not your teams ‘base’.. The first team that can successfully 
collect all 6 are the winners.  
3.
Rules: Players can only take one beanbag at a time, players that are tagged in an opposing teams square sit down where they are 
tagged and can only be picked up and taken back to their teams side (cannot be tagged while heading back with two people) by a 
team mate (link arms). If the rescue person is tagged, they also sit down. A player that reaches the safe zone / opposing base can 
wait as long as possible to head back. If they get tagged when they head back, they return the beanbag and sit down where tagged. 
Teams cannot tag other players in squares other their their own. Teams cannot enter their own ‘base’.  
Pink Base
Blue Base
Orange Base
White Base
4 diﬀerent colored sets of beanbags / ﬂags (6 for each color)
Cones

fiches.title: Crazy Kickball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Crazy Kickball
Setup
1.
This game can be played outside or inside with walls.  
2.
Split the class in to two teams - kickers and fielders 
3.
Two Kickers at a time kick the ball out into the field of play and aim to make it to the end base without being tagged by a 
ball. If they reach the end base they are safe. There can only be a maximum of 4 kicking team players in the base at one 
time. If there is more the first people that were there must head back for home on the next kick.  
4.
Kickers are out if their ball is caught 
5.
Fielders can throw the ball at the kickers as they run but they cannot run with the ball. Passing between fielders is allowed. 
Any kickers that are hit below the neck with the ball are out. 
6.
Rotate after everyone has had a kick

fiches.title: CRoss The River
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
CRoss The River
Setup
1.
Split group into approximately groups of 6-8. Make sure every group has the same number of people if 
possible.  
2.
Each group receives 2 large gym mats, 2 scooter boards, 2 poly spots and a large tug of war rope. 
3.
The object of the game is for groups to use communication, strategy and getting along to get all of the 
people in the group  (and each piece of equipment) over to the other side of the ‘river' 
4.
The first team to do so are winners.  
5.
Allow students to talk strategy at the start and emphasis teamwork and good communication.  
Large mats
scooters 
boards
polyspots
large 
ropes

fiches.title: Color Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Color Ball
Setup
Throwing Line
1.
Split group into two teams and setup as shown. One team are the throwers and one team are the fielders 
2.
Give a ball to each student, doesn’t matter what color they get. 
3.
On go, the throwers all throw out into the field of play. Once they have done that, the first person in line 
attempt to run around the bases (hoops) and ten tag the next person in line who does the same. 
4.
The fielders aim to return all the balls back to their corresponding colored hula hoops. Filders can throw 
them back to a person near the hoop who can place it in or run it back. Fielders yell freeze when all balls are 
returned. have 5 throws each or rotate.  
4 diﬀerent colored hula hoops
Diﬀerent colored balls/ beanbags that 
match color of hoops

fiches.title: Flip the Cone
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Flip the Cone
Setup
1.
Divide each group into teams. Each group chooses 3-4 taggers who stay on their side of the playing area and 
attempt to tag opposition. On teachers signal teams attempt to cross over to the other teams area and ‘flip’ a disc 
/ Cone.  
2. Once a player has one foot inside a hula hoop, they cannot be tagged. They flip the Cone and get a free pass back to 
the start of the area 
3.
Taggers must stay out of hula hoops. If tagged on the way over, players must immediately go back to the start of 
their playing area 
4.
The team that flips them all over first are the winners
Hula Hoops and Cones

fiches.title: Invaders
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Invaders
Setup
•
Split group into two even teams. Each player wears a ‘sash’/pinnie and tucks it into the back of their pants with 
approx 2/3rds showing.  
•
The aim of this game is to invade the other teams country and steal ‘treasure’ (Cones) from their hula hoop. If you 
successfully steal a treasure then you can head back to your side with ‘immunity’ e.g cannot be tagged 
•
If you are tagged (sash taken from back of pants and thrown on the ground), then you pick up your sash and head 
to ‘jail’ on the opposing side. 
•
You can be released from jail by one of your team mates ‘rescuing you’. If you are rescued you and your partner 
link arms and can head back to your side (country) with immunity 
•
Winning team is the team that has the most ‘treasure’ at the end of a specified time limit
Hula Hoops and Cones
Pinnie, Sash, Scarf or Similar Item

fiches.title: Castles
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Castles
1 x Tall Cone each
Setup area as show. Student each have a Cone ‘Castle’ they need to protect. The object is to knock 
other students Cones over with a soccer pass. Students can protect their Cone with the feet or 
body only, no hands. The last standing 3 castles are the winners. To keep students involved, once they 
are out they find a partner and perform soccer passing skills at a different station. Progressions: To 
make it harder have less balls, lengthen the area or allow hands. To make it easier shorten the area, 
add kore balls or allow rolling. 
1 x soccer ball for every 3 people in the game

make it harder have less balls, lengthen the area or allow hands. To make it easier shorten the area,
add kore balls or allow rolling.

fiches.title: Baby Dragons
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Baby Dragons
Cones & Hula Hoops
• Setup playing area with targets (nest) and Cones spread out as shown 
• Randomly place beanbags (dragon eggs) underneath Cones. The more Cones the better so students have to search 
• When a student finds a beanbag (dragon egg), they pick it up and try to throw through the nearest target (nests) 
• If a students is successful they place that beanbag in their teams nest (bucket) 
• If unsuccessful, try from a closer distance.  
• Play for a specified time period or until all being shave been successfully returned. 
Disc Cones
Beanbags

fiches.title: Pinball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Pinball
Setup area as shown. This game is best played indoors. This game is a variation on dodgeball. Normal dodgeball rules apply. Players 
aim to throw the ball at the opposition, if they are hit they below the waist they are out and do not come back in until a 
teammate catches the ball or the teachers says ‘everyone back in’. if hit in the head students are not out unless they duck into 
an otherwise good throw. Students may also try and knock the balls of the oppositions ‘pins’ at the end of their zone. A ball that 
is knocked off cannot be placed back on. The winning team is the team that knocks down all balls of the oppositions pins. 
Students may protect the pins but if they are hit, and don’t catch the ball, they are out. Progression: Choose one ‘pinkeeper who 
is the only one allowed to block the pins (if they are hit they are not out), mark off this area and nobody else is allowed into the 
zone. 
Dodgeballs

an otherwise good throw. Students may also try and knock the balls of the oppositions ‘pins’ at the end of their zone. A ball that
is knocked off cannot be placed back on. The winning team is the team that knocks down all balls of the oppositions pins.
Students may protect the pins but if they are hit, and don’t catch the ball, they are out. Progression: Choose one ‘pinkeeper who
is the only one allowed to block the pins (if they are hit they are not out), mark off this area and nobody else is allowed into the
zone.

fiches.title: Protect the Castle
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Protect the Castle
• Setup playing area as shown 
• Throwers lined up outside if the large circle (marked by cones) 
• The throwing teams object is to throws balls at the targets in the middle (tall Cones and pins) 
• Choose two students in the middle who try and block or catch throws 
• Any blocked throws can be picked up, and taken to the outside of the large circle. If a broker catches the ball, they 
simply roll it back out 
• Switch over when all pins have been hit or knocked over
Cones & Bowling Pins
Dodgeballs
