fiches.title: LEG DAY
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 20
20
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This lower body workout focuses on building strength and stability in the legs and glutes. Participants 
will target major muscle groups through controlled movements that enhance balance, endurance, 
and functional power. This workout is perfect for improving athletic performance and supporting 
everyday lower-body activities.
Remind participants to keep their knees aligned with their toes during squats and lunges to protect 
their joints.
Encourage slow, controlled movements for maximum muscle engagement, especially during 
single-leg exercises.
Highlight the importance of maintaining an upright posture and engaging the core for stability.
COACHING TIPS
GLUTE BRIDGES: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet 
flat on the ground. Press into your heels to lift your hips towards the 
ceiling, then lower back down.
EASIER: Perform the exercise with a smaller range of motion.
HARDER: Hold the lifted position for a longer duration or add weight 
to your hips.
SINGLE-LEG ROMANIAN DEADLIFT: Stand on one leg with a slight 
bend in your knee. Hinge at the hips, lowering your torso while 
extending the opposite leg backward. Return to the starting 
position.
EASIER: Use a wall or chair for balance.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
SUMO SQUATS: Stand with your feet wider than shoulder-width 
apart and your toes turned out. Lower into a squat, keeping your 
chest up and knees tracking over your toes, then return to the 
starting position.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the squat.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
STATIC LUNGE HOLD: Stand with your feet together. Step one foot 
forward and lower into a lunge, holding the position.
EASIER: Shorten the duration of the hold.
HARDER: Hold weights in each hand.
EXERCISES
LEG DAY

© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

LEG DAY

fiches.title: POWER SURGE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 22
22
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This workout focuses on building explosive power and strength using high-intensity movements. The 
exercises are designed to challenge the muscles and improve your ability to generate force quickly. 
Focus on strong, controlled landings during jumps to protect joints and maintain balance.
Encourage participants to use maximum effort during explosive movements, but remind them to 
reset between reps if needed.
Demonstrate both easier and harder versions to ensure everyone can participate at their own level.
COACHING TIPS
JUMP SQUATS: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Lower 
into a squat, then explosively jump up, reaching your arms 
overhead.
EASIER: Perform regular squats without jumping.
HARDER: Increase the depth of the squat or add weights.
EXERCISES
BROAD JUMPS: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Bend 
your knees and swing your arms backward. Jump forward as far as 
you can, landing softly on your feet.
EASIER: Perform a smaller jump.
HARDER: Increase the distance of your jumps or add a squat at the 
landing.
PLYO PUSH-UPS: Start in a standard push-up position. Lower your 
body to the ground, then explosively push up, lifting your hands off 
the ground.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees.
HARDER: Clap your hands together at the top of each push-up.
TOUCH AND HOP: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Lower 
into a squat and reach down to touch the ground with one hand. As 
you rise, hop on the opposite leg, lifting your other knee up towards 
your chest. Repeat on the other side.
EASIER: Perform the movement without the hop, just standing up 
instead.
HARDER: Increase the speed and height of the hop.
LATERAL HOPS: Stand with your feet together. Jump to the side, 
landing softly on both feet, then jump back to the starting position.
EASIER: Step side to side instead of jumping.
HARDER: Increase the distance of each jump.
BURPEES: Begin standing with your feet shoulder-width apart. Lower 
into a squat, place your hands on the ground, and jump your feet back 
into a plank position. Perform a push-up, then jump your feet back 
towards your hands, and explosively jump up, reaching your arms 
overhead.
EASIER: Skip the push-up.
HARDER: Add a tuck jump at the end.
POWER SURGE

fiches.title: S.A.Q. MASTERY
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 24
24
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This workout is designed to improve speed, agility and quickness. By incorporating lateral and 
forward movements, participants will train their ability to change direction quickly and maintain 
control during high-intensity activities. It’s perfect for enhancing athletic performance and overall 
functional fitness.
Encourage quick, controlled movements during lateral and directional changes to maximize agility 
gains.
Focus on staying light on the feet and landing softly to reduce strain on the knees and ankles.
Demonstrate proper form and balance during each movement to prevent injuries and improve 
effectiveness.
COACHING TIPS
LATERAL SHUFFLES: Stand with your feet hip-width apart and your 
knees slightly bent. Shuffle quickly to the side, keeping your body 
low.­
EASIER: Slow down the pace.
HARDER: Add a touch to the ground with each step.
EXERCISES
FORWARD AND BACKWARD HOPS: Stand with your feet hip-width 
apart. Jump forward a small distance, then jump backward to the 
starting position.
EASIER: Perform small hops.
HARDER: Increase the distance of each hop.
SKATER HOPS: Stand on one leg with your knee slightly bent. Jump 
laterally to the opposite leg, landing softly and balancing before 
jumping back.
EASIER: Step side to side instead of jumping.
HARDER: Increase the distance of each jump.
LATERAL HIGH KNEES: Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Run in 
place, bringing your knees up towards your chest while moving laterally.
EASIER: March in place with high knees.
HARDER: Increase the speed and distance of lateral movement.
TOUCH AND HOP: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Lower 
into a squat and reach down to touch the ground with one hand. As 
you rise, hop on the opposite leg, lifting your other knee up towards 
your chest. Repeat on the other side.
EASIER: Perform the movement without the hop, just standing up 
instead.
HARDER: Increase the speed and height of the hop.
FAST FEET: Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Quickly alternate 
lifting your feet off the ground, as if running in place but with minimal 
elevation.
EASIER: Slow down the pace.
HARDER: Increase the speed.
S.A.Q. MASTERY

fiches.title: CLIMB AND CONQUER
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 26
26
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This HIIT Pyramid workout alternates between increasing and decreasing effort levels to build 
endurance and power. The intensity rises as you add more time, then decreases, allowing for 
maximum effort followed by recovery. It’s designed to push limits while keeping the session dynamic 
and fun.
Encourage participants to start strong but save enough energy to maintain intensity as the pyramid 
builds.
Focus on controlled form during each exercise, especially as fatigue sets in at the peak.
Motivate participants to push harder during the decreasing time intervals to finish strong.
COACHING TIPS
JUMPING JACKS: Stand with your feet together and your arms at 
your sides. Jump your feet out to the sides while raising your arms 
overhead, then jump back to the starting position.
EASIER: Step one foot out at a time instead of jumping.  
HARDER: Increase the speed or add a squat when your feet are out 
wide.
MOUNTAIN CLIMBERS: Start in a plank position with your hands 
under your shoulders. Quickly alternate bringing one knee towards 
your chest, then the other, as if running in place.
EASIER: Slow down the pace.
HARDER: Add a twist by bringing your knee towards the opposite 
elbow.
LUNGES: Stand with your feet together. Step one foot forward and 
lower your hips until both knees are bent at about 90 degrees. 
Return to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Perform smaller steps.
HARDER: Add a jump to switch legs.
EXERCISES
BURPEES: Begin standing with your feet shoulder-width apart. 
Lower into a squat, place your hands on the ground, and jump your 
feet back into a plank position. Perform a push-up, then jump your 
feet back towards your hands, and explosively jump up, reaching 
your arms overhead.
EASIER: Skip the push-up.
HARDER: Add a tuck jump at the end.
SQUAT PULSE: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Lower 
into a squat and perform small, pulsing movements at the bottom of 
the squat.  
EASIER: Perform regular squats. 
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
CLIMB AND CONQUER

fiches.title: COOL - DOWN CIRCUIT
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 28
28
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This recovery circuit is designed to help the body transition from intense exercise to rest. It focuses 
on improving flexibility, reducing muscle tension, and promoting relaxation. These low-impact 
movements are perfect for cooling down and resetting after a workout.
Encourage participants to focus on slow, deep breathing to promote relaxation and recovery.
Emphasize holding each stretch gently, avoiding any bouncing or forced movements.
Remind participants to focus on areas of tightness and adjust their range of motion as needed.
COACHING TIPS
CHILD'S POSE: Kneel on the floor with your big toes touching and 
knees spread apart. Sit back on your heels and extend your arms 
forward, lowering your torso between your thighs.
EASIER: Keep your knees together.  
HARDER: Reach your arms further forward for a deeper stretch.
COBRA POSE: Lie face-down on the floor with your hands under 
your shoulders and your elbows close to your body. Press into your 
hands to lift your chest off the floor, keeping your hips grounded.
EASIER: Lift only to a comfortable height.
HARDER: Extend your arms fully to increase the stretch.
FIGURE FOUR STRETCH: Sit or lie on the floor with your legs 
extended. Cross one ankle over the opposite knee, forming a figure 
four shape. Lean forward slightly to increase the stretch.
EASIER: Keep your back straight.
HARDER: Pull your crossed leg closer to your body.
NECK ROTATIONS: Stand or sit comfortably with your back straight. 
Slowly rotate your head in a circular motion, first clockwise, then 
counterclockwise.
EASIER: Reduce the range of motion.
HARDER: Increase the duration of each rotation.
EXERCISES
COOL-DOWN CIRCUIT

The techniques, ideas, and suggestions in this document are not intended as a substitute for proper 
medical advice! Consult your physician or health care professional before performing any exercise 
or exercise technique. Any application of the techniques, ideas, and suggestions in this document 
is at the reader’s sole discretion and risk. 

© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
3
Welcome to No Equipment PE Games! This resource is designed to be your go-to guide for fun, engaging 
physical education activities that require no equipment at all. It’s packed with 20 kid-tested games that 
are suitable for all ages and fitness levels.
Whether you’re looking for something that will get your heart racing or something that’s a little more laid 
back, you’re sure to find an activity that fits the bill.
Each game includes the following information:
1.	 Focus: The components of fitness or skill areas that the activity focuses on: 
2.	 Group Size: The minimum number of participants required for the lesson.
3.	 Difficulty:  A rating from 1 (easiest) to 5 (hardest). Harder games require more skill, more setup or more 
complex rules. 
4.	 Equipment: Required sports equipment or instructor materials.
5.	 Instructions:  How to play the game, step by step.
6.	 Variations: Modifications to make the game easier or harder. Or just a different way to play.
We know that physical education can sometimes be a challenging subject to teach, especially if you don’t 
have access to a lot of equipment or space. 
But with No Equipment PE Games we hope to make your job a little easier and a lot more fun. So grab a 
copy of this book, round up some students, and get ready to have a blast!
TEAMWORK

GROSS MOTOR
FINE MOTOR
STRENGTH
ENDURANCE

fiches.title: Relay Rumble Relay Rumble
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Use other locomotor skills other than running in each race such as skipping, galloping, carioca, side 
stepping, hopping, crab walking, and bear crawls.
Relay Rumble
Relay Rumble
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Put the students in traditional relay race lines.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first kid must run from the start line to the opposite end line, touch the line and then 
come back to the start line.
2.	 Once back at the start line, the next child will follow the same pattern.
3.	 The first team to have each student on their team complete the task wins the round.

sections.Variation:
Use other locomotor skills other than running in each race such as skipping, galloping, carioca, side
stepping, hopping, crab walking, and bear crawls.
Relay Rumble
Relay Rumble
DIFFICULTY

•
None
sections.Setup:
•
Put the students in traditional relay race lines.

come back to the start line.
2.	 Once back at the start line, the next child will follow the same pattern.
3.	 The first team to have each student on their team complete the task wins the round.

fiches.title: Back-2-Back Back-2-Back
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To make it more challenging, group the kids into trios instead of pairs.
Back-2-Back
Back-2-Back
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.  Each child will be assigned one partner to pair up with 
for the activity.  The teacher should have music available to play.
Instructions
1.	 Once the children are assigned a partner, the teacher will begin to play music.
2.	 While the music is playing, the children will be told to use some type of locomotor skill (walking, 
jogging, galloping, skipping, grapevining…etc.) to move about the room.
3.	 When they move about the room, they will not be travelling with their partner , but moving about 
independently
4.	 As soon as the teacher turns off the music, the kids must stop their locomotion movement and 
find their partner and stand back-to-back with them.
5.	 Then, the teacher will put on music again and the process starts all over again.
6.	 After a few minutes, switch up the students so they have a new partner.

sections.Variation:
To make it more challenging, group the kids into trios instead of pairs.
Back-2-Back
Back-2-Back
DIFFICULTY

•
None

fiches.title: Tag Team Relay Tag Team Relay
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
You can make the event into a competition where the children have to count how many laps their 
group gets in the specific time period allotted for the activity.  Whichever team has the most laps 
wins the round.
Tag Team Relay
Tag Team Relay
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Place the students in pairs along the outside of your room or play area using the corners of the 
rooms as a start area.
Instructions
1.	 This is not a race; it’s an endurance activity.
2.	 On ‘go’ one child in the group will begin to run a lap around your designated area.
3.	 Once they get back to the start line, they tag their partner’s hand who now runs a lap.
4.	 The partners will continue to follow this pattern for the time frame of the activity.
5.	 Children need to be careful and considerate towards the other groups running and space out 
properly.

sections.Variation:
You can make the event into a competition where the children have to count how many laps their
group gets in the specific time period allotted for the activity.  Whichever team has the most laps
wins the round.
Tag Team Relay
Tag Team Relay

None

5.	 Children need to be careful and considerate towards the other groups running and space out

fiches.title: Mirror, Mirror Mirror, Mirror
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
8
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
After each round, have the children rotate to a new partner.  Every time they move to a new 
partner, they should play rock, paper, scissor to determine the new leader.
Mirror, Mirror
Mirror, Mirror
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room in pairs.
Instructions
1.	 Place students in pairs, or groups of three as needed.
2.	 Within each group, pick one child to be the leader.
3.	 On ‘go’ the leader will begin performing some stationary exercise or movement in their spot. For 
example, freestyle dance, jumping jacks, push-ups, sit-ups, running in place, etc.
4.	 The other children in the group must ‘mirror’ all their movements.
5.	 After a few minutes, have the children switch roles.

sections.Variation:
After each round, have the children rotate to a new partner.  Every time they move to a new
partner, they should play rock, paper, scissor to determine the new leader.
Mirror, Mirror
Mirror, Mirror

None

4.	 The other children in the group must ‘mirror’ all their movements.
5.	 After a few minutes, have the children switch roles.

fiches.title: Locomotion Commotion Locomotion Commotion
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
9
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
You can allow the kids to change directions when they run on the tracks.  This may make the game 
extend for a much longer period of time.
Locomotion Commotion
Locomotion Commotion
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.  Pick one child to be the chaser.  All the kids must stand 
on a gym floor line.
Instructions
1.	 Pick one child to be the chaser who starts off in the middle of the room.
2.	 During the game, all the children can only run forward on the lines on the floor as if they were a 
train on a railroad track.
3.	 They cannot change direction to go backwards while they travel around the room.
4.	 On ‘go’ the chaser will begin to run forward on the lines to chase and tag kids who are also 
running on the established lines on the floor.
5.	 If a child gets tagged, they must sit in their spot, and now become a track blocker.  The chaser 
is the only student who can run around a track blocker.  Other student who approach a track 
blocker are now trapped on the railroad track and much easier to catch.
6.	 The last student left is the winner and the next chaser.
Chaser

sections.Variation:
You can allow the kids to change directions when they run on the tracks.  This may make the game
extend for a much longer period of time.
Locomotion Commotion
Locomotion Commotion
DIFFICULTY

•
None
sections.Setup:
•
Spread the students out around the room.  Pick one child to be the chaser.  All the kids must stand
on a gym floor line.
sections.Instructions:
1.	 Pick one child to be the chaser who starts off in the middle of the room.
2.	 During the game, all the children can only run forward on the lines on the floor as if they were a
train on a railroad track.
3.	 They cannot change direction to go backwards while they travel around the room.
4.	 On ‘go’ the chaser will begin to run forward on the lines to chase and tag kids who are also
running on the established lines on the floor.
5.	 If a child gets tagged, they must sit in their spot, and now become a track blocker.  The chaser
is the only student who can run around a track blocker.  Other student who approach a track
blocker are now trapped on the railroad track and much easier to catch.
6.	 The last student left is the winner and the next chaser.
Chaser

fiches.title: Calisthenics Corner Calisthenics Corner
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
10
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of clapping, the teacher can use music to have the children move about the room.
Calisthenics Corner
Calisthenics Corner
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.  
Instructions
1.	 Designate each corner of the room as an exercise station.  For example, one corner could be 
push-ups, and the other corners can be jogging in place, crab walking, and jumping jacks.
2.	 When the teacher starts clapping their hands creating a rhythm, the students will begin to walk or 
jog quietly around the room.
3.	 As soon as the teacher stops clapping their hands, the students must pick a corner of the room to 
go to and perform the specific exercise designated at the corner.
4.	 Next, the teacher will begin to clap their hands again, and the kids will now follow the same 
pattern as they did previously.
5.	 The kids should not go to the same corner two times in a row when the clapping stops.

sections.Variation:
Instead of clapping, the teacher can use music to have the children move about the room.
Calisthenics Corner
Calisthenics Corner
DIFFICULTY

•
None

push-ups, and the other corners can be jogging in place, crab walking, and jumping jacks.

3.	 As soon as the teacher stops clapping their hands, the students must pick a corner of the room to
go to and perform the specific exercise designated at the corner.
4.	 Next, the teacher will begin to clap their hands again, and the kids will now follow the same
pattern as they did previously.
5.	 The kids should not go to the same corner two times in a row when the clapping stops.

fiches.title: Shadow Tag Shadow Tag
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
11
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
You can try to play the game with three students per square to create more action in the game.
Shadow Tag
Shadow Tag
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room and put them in pairs.  The pairs of student are going 
to be challenging each other in speed, agility, and cognitive skills.  If you do not have existing 
lines on the ground, you can use floor tape or chalk to draw a small rectangles around each 
group.
Instructions
1.	 In this activity, the children will pair off against a partner.  Both students must stay within their 
rectangle.
2.	 On ‘go’ the students will attempt to touch with their hands their opposing player’s feet.  Each 
time they can touch a foot, they get a point.
3.	 Children will move about their square playing offense (touching a foot) and defense (avoid 
having their feet touched).
4.	 If a student moves outside their square, a point is also awarded to the other player.
5.	 Children may not dive on the ground in an effort to touch their competition’s feet.
6.	 Children must also be aware and careful not to bump heads with their challenger.

sections.Variation:
You can try to play the game with three students per square to create more action in the game.
Shadow Tag
Shadow Tag
DIFFICULTY

•
None

to be challenging each other in speed, agility, and cognitive skills.  If you do not have existing

group.

fiches.title: Rock, Paper, Scissor  Rock, Paper, Scissor  Jumping Jacks Jumping Jacks
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
You can have the children keep track of who wins more times with their partners.  The child who 
loses to their partner is eliminated when they switch partners.  Keep following this process with 
each round until you have 1 child left.
Rock, Paper, Scissor 
Rock, Paper, Scissor 
Jumping Jacks
Jumping Jacks
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room in pairs.  Teach them how to play the classic game of 
rock, paper, scissor.
Instructions
1.	 Students play a simple game of rock, paper, scissor with a partner.
2.	 Each time a student loses, they must do 5 jumping jacks.
3.	 The children can play as many rounds as they can within a one minute period.
4.	 After one minute, have the children switch partners and then begin the process again.

sections.Variation:
You can have the children keep track of who wins more times with their partners.  The child who
loses to their partner is eliminated when they switch partners.  Keep following this process with
each round until you have 1 child left.

fiches.title: Creature Crawl Creature Crawl
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
13
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Partner up the kids so they can perform the animal with a friend and move about the room 
together.
Creature Crawl
Creature Crawl
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.
Instructions
1.	 During this activity, the children will have to use their imagination.
2.	 The teacher will call out an animal, and the students will now have to act like this animal.  For 
example, if the teacher says a ‘bird’ the children will have to flap their arms and move about the 
room like a bird.
3.	 If the teacher says a ‘kangaroo’ the children will have to hop around the room.
4.	 Every 30 seconds, the teacher should switch to a new animal.

sections.Variation:
Partner up the kids so they can perform the animal with a friend and move about the room
together.
Creature Crawl
Creature Crawl
DIFFICULTY
sections.Equipment:
•
None
sections.Setup:
•
Spread the students out around the room.
sections.Instructions:
1.	 During this activity, the children will have to use their imagination.
2.	 The teacher will call out an animal, and the students will now have to act like this animal.  For
example, if the teacher says a ‘bird’ the children will have to flap their arms and move about the
room like a bird.
3.	 If the teacher says a ‘kangaroo’ the children will have to hop around the room.
4.	 Every 30 seconds, the teacher should switch to a new animal.

fiches.title: Escape and Evade Escape and Evade
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
14
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To increase the action and make it more challenging, choose multiple kids to be the chaser at one 
time.
Escape and Evade
Escape and Evade
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.  Pick one child to be the chaser.  
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the  children will run around the room.
2.	 The chaser will attempt to tag other children.
3.	 If a child gets tagged by the chaser, they must freeze in their spot with their hands above their 
head.
4.	 A child who is not frozen can now crawl between the legs of a child who is caught to free them 
and allow them to come back into the game.  For safety reasons, kids can only crawl between 
legs from back to front.
5.	 After a few minutes, switch to a new chaser.

sections.Variation:
To increase the action and make it more challenging, choose multiple kids to be the chaser at one
time.
Escape and Evade
Escape and Evade
DIFFICULTY
sections.Equipment:
•
None

•
Spread the students out around the room.  Pick one child to be the chaser.
sections.Instructions:
1.	 On ‘go’ the  children will run around the room.
2.	 The chaser will attempt to tag other children.
3.	 If a child gets tagged by the chaser, they must freeze in their spot with their hands above their
head.
4.	 A child who is not frozen can now crawl between the legs of a child who is caught to free them
and allow them to come back into the game.  For safety reasons, kids can only crawl between
legs from back to front.
5.	 After a few minutes, switch to a new chaser.

fiches.title: Fishy Fiasco Fishy Fiasco
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
15
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Start with 2 or 3 fishermen to play a faster game. You can also play in a larger area such as a soccer 
field with a very large group of kids.
Fishy Fiasco
Fishy Fiasco
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Place all the children (the fish) on one end line.  Pick one child to be the fisherman (the chaser) 
who starts off in the middle of the room.
Instructions
1.	 When the fisherman says, “Come Fishes Come” all the fish try to run carefully from one end line 
to the other end line. The fisherman will chase after them, attempting to tag them.
2.	 If a fish gets tagged, they now become seaweed, frozen in their spot and waving their hands 
from side to side.  All the kids who made it to the other end line are safe.
3.	 Then, the fisherman will say again, “Come Fishes Come” as the untagged students must run 
back to the opposite end line.  This time, they must avoid the fisherman and the seaweed (who 
can move their arms and tag the fish running across).
4.	 The pattern continues until the last fish is left who is the winner.
Fisherman

sections.Variation:
Start with 2 or 3 fishermen to play a faster game. You can also play in a larger area such as a soccer
field with a very large group of kids.
Fishy Fiasco
Fishy Fiasco
DIFFICULTY

•
None

2.	 If a fish gets tagged, they now become seaweed, frozen in their spot and waving their hands

3.	 Then, the fisherman will say again, “Come Fishes Come” as the untagged students must run

can move their arms and tag the fish running across).
4.	 The pattern continues until the last fish is left who is the winner.
Fisherman

fiches.title: Boogie Breakout Boogie Breakout
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
16
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To make it more challenging, the teacher can pick the locomotor movement each child should do.  
For example, each child needs to crabwalk when the music comes on.
Boogie Breakout
Boogie Breakout
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
Music
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.  Have some fun music to play.
Instructions
1.	 This is a simple activity that will work on locomotor skills.
2.	 When the teacher starts to play music, the children will begin to freestyle dance around the 
room or perform some type of locomotor skill (skip, gallop, hop…etc.).
3.	 As soon as the music stops, the children must freeze in their spot.
4.	 When the music begins again, the children will again start to move around the room.

sections.Variation:
To make it more challenging, the teacher can pick the locomotor movement each child should do.
For example, each child needs to crabwalk when the music comes on.

3.	 As soon as the music stops, the children must freeze in their spot.
4.	 When the music begins again, the children will again start to move around the room.

fiches.title: Cardio Freeze Cardio Freeze
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
17
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of using a whistle, play music. Turn the music on and off to stop and start movement each 
time.
Cardio Freeze
Cardio Freeze
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.  The teacher will use their whistle so the children know 
when it is time to start and stop. 
Instructions
1.	 Blow your whistle and ask the kids to slowly jog around the room.
2.	 After 30-60 seconds, blow the whistle again. Ask the children to freeze where they are and 
measure their heart rate by feeling their neck, wrist or chest.
3.	 Wait 10-20 seconds, then call out a new locomotor such as skipping, galloping, running or crab 
walking. Move for 30-60 seconds, blow the whistle again and measure heart rates.
4.	 Ask the kids which movements made their heart rates speed up the most!

sections.Variation:
Instead of using a whistle, play music. Turn the music on and off to stop and start movement each
time.
Cardio Freeze
Cardio Freeze
DIFFICULTY
sections.Equipment:
•
None

•

when it is time to start and stop.

1.	 Blow your whistle and ask the kids to slowly jog around the room.
2.	 After 30-60 seconds, blow the whistle again. Ask the children to freeze where they are and
measure their heart rate by feeling their neck, wrist or chest.
3.	 Wait 10-20 seconds, then call out a new locomotor such as skipping, galloping, running or crab
walking. Move for 30-60 seconds, blow the whistle again and measure heart rates.
4.	 Ask the kids which movements made their heart rates speed up the most!

fiches.title: No-Rope, Jump  No-Rope, Jump
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
18
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Try introducing real jump ropes or hula hoops and repeating the skills.
No-Rope, Jump 
No-Rope, Jump 
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.
Instructions
1.	 In this activity, the teacher will call out various skills for the kids to perform to simulate jumping 
rope, but without the ropes.
2.	 The children will have to move their arms in the direction needed as well as jump with each make 
believe pass of the rope.
3.	 Children can practice jumping forwards, backwards, one jump with each pass of the rope or two 
jumps with each pass of the rope. 
4.	 Skills can vary depending on the age of the class.

sections.Variation:
Try introducing real jump ropes or hula hoops and repeating the skills.

No-Rope, Jump

toe touches.
The Zen Zone

None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room so they have enough space.
Instructions
1.	 For this activity, the teacher can play some soft, relaxing music in the background.
2.	 The teacher will call out and demonstrate various yoga poses for the kids to perform. 
3.	 For example, boat pose, child’s pose, downward dog, happy baby, cat/cow or triangle pose.
4.	 The yoga poses should be simple movements that do not require any mat beneath them.
5.	 After about 30 seconds, the teacher will call out another yoga pose to perform and switch up 
the poses every 30 seconds.

fiches.title: Wheelbarrow Warriors Wheelbarrow Warriors
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
20
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
When the kids partner up, change the format.  As the first pair gets to the end line, they switch 
roles and then come back to the start line.
Wheelbarrow Warriors
Wheelbarrow Warriors
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Place the kids in traditional relay race lines.  The first child in line will lay on the floor in a push-up 
position as the next child in line will lift up their feet and hold them by their side.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first two kids in line will wheelbarrow from the start line to the end line, then back to 
the start line.
2.	 When they return to start, the child who was just on the ground  must go to the end of the line 
as the kid who held their feet now gets into the push-up position as the third child in line will 
now lift up their feet to their waist area.
3.	 This pattern will continue until the first child in line gets a chance to hold another child’s feet by 
their waist area.  This way, all the children got to do each skill for the race.
4.	 The first team to finish wins the round.

sections.Variation:

roles and then come back to the start line.

fiches.title: Birthday Brigade Birthday Brigade
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
21
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Depending on the age of the children, give them a time limit to finish their line.   This will add a little 
‘spark’ to the activity.
Birthday Brigade
Birthday Brigade
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Have the children sit on the floor to listen to directions. Use your phone, a clock, or a stopwatch 
to time them.
Instructions
1.	 The objective in this activity is to have the children form a straight line in birthday order with 
January birthdays at the front of the line, and December birthdays at the end of the line.
2.	 The key challenge is they are not allowed to communicate verbally.
3.	 The line must be sequential.  This means if two kids have a birthday in March, the child with the 
earlier birthday should be ahead of the other.
4.	 On the command of ‘go’ they must begin to form their line as quickly as they can.
5.	 When completed, have the first kid in line announce out loud their birthday, followed by the 
next child….and so on.  The children will see if they did it properly.

sections.Variation:
Depending on the age of the children, give them a time limit to finish their line.   This will add a little
‘spark’ to the activity.

fiches.title: Crab Taps Crab Taps
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
22
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of the teacher calling out right, left, or freestyle, one child in each group can take on that 
role.  
Crab Taps
Crab Taps
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Partner children into pairs and spread them out around the room.  Each pair should be facing 
each other in a crab position.
Instructions
1.	 This is a great upper body strength activity along with balance and teamwork.
2.	 Partners will face each other in a crab position, supporting their bodies.
3.	 When the teacher calls out ‘right’ the partners will lift their right feet and tap them together.
4.	 When the teacher calls out ‘left’ the partners will lift their left feet and tap them together.
5.	 When the teacher yells out ‘free style’ the partners will tap whichever foot they want.

sections.Variation:

role.

fiches.title: Scuttlebutts Scuttlebutts
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
23
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of crab walking, you can allow the kids to scoot instead. They can sit on the floor and pull 
themselves along while allowing their bottoms to slide on the ground. You can also bear crawl 
instead of crab walk.
Scuttlebutts
Scuttlebutts
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
None
Setup
•	
Spread the students out around the room.  Pick one child to be the chaser.  All the kids should be 
in the crab walk position.
Instructions
1.	 In this activity, the students may only travel around the room using a crab walk or a crab scoot.
2.	 On ‘go’ the children will crab walk or crab scoot around the room.  The chaser will attempt to tag 
another child with their hand.
3.	 If a child gets tagged, they must stand up and perform 10 jumping jacks, then they may re-enter 
the game.
4.	 After a few minutes, give the kids a rest period, and pick a new chaser.
Chaser

Put the kids into two groups and have them race against one another to see who can create the 
number as a group first. You can also have them create letters or symbols.

None

•	
Spread the students out around the room.

1.	 This is a group activity where the children will need to communicate and be creative.
2.	 The teacher will call out a number.
3.	 Once the number is called, the entire class must attempt to form that number using all their 
bodies.
4.	 Once completed, the teacher will call out a new number for the children to create as a group.

fiches.title: INTRODUCTION
fiches.body:
3
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
•
Exercise descriptions with modifications for different fitness levels
•
Coaching tips to help ensure proper form and technique
•
Suggested sets, reps, and rest times for structured training
As a special bonus, we’ve also included a set of printable workout journals you can 
distribute to students to help them track their progress, reflect on their performance, 
and learn more about strength training concepts.
These circuits are perfect for PE classes, team training, or general fitness sessions. 
Whether you’re leading a group or training independently, these workouts are designed 
to build strength in a way that is accessible, progressive, and engaging—all without the 
need for expensive equipment.
Let’s get started on building strength, confidence, and fitness—anytime, anywhere! 
INTRODUCTION
Welcome to No Equipment Strength Circuits! This resource is designed to help you lead 
fun, effective, and accessible strength training workouts that require zero equipment. 
Whether you’re working with students, athletes, or general fitness participants, these 
circuits provide a structured and engaging way to build strength using only bodyweight 
exercises.
The Importance of Strength Training
Strength training is any form of exercise that improves muscle strength, endurance, 
and stability. It helps build stronger muscles, bones, and joints, which are essential for 
overall health and injury prevention. Strength training isn’t just for athletes—it’s beneficial 
for people of all ages, from kids to adults, because it improves movement efficiency, 
balance, and posture while boosting confidence and physical performance.
Unlike heavy weightlifting, bodyweight strength training is a safe and effective way for 
kids and teens to build strength without the risk of lifting weights incorrectly. Exercises like 
squats, push-ups, lunges, and planks use natural movement patterns that help develop 
coordination, endurance, and overall fitness in a way that is both safe and functional.
What’s Included?
Inside this resource, you’ll find 13 step-by-step strength circuits, each designed to 
target different areas of the body while building total-body strength. Each workout 
comes with an instructor guide that includes:

fiches.title: WORKOUTS
fiches.body:
5
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
WORKOUTS

fiches.title: STRONG & STEADY
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
6
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This workout focuses on slow, controlled movements to maximize strength and muscle activation. By 
reducing momentum and emphasizing time under tension, participants will build stability, endurance, 
and total-body strength.
Slow it down—each rep should be controlled, with a focus on steady movements rather than speed.
Encourage participants to pause at the hardest part of each exercise (e.g., bottom of a squat or 
push-up) to increase muscle engagement.
Emphasize alignment—knees should track over toes during squats and lunges, and the core should 
stay engaged during plank exercises.
COACHING TIPS
STATIC SQUAT HOLD: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. 
Lower into a squat and hold the position, keeping your chest up and 
thighs parallel to the floor.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the squat. 
HARDER: Hold a weight in each hand.
CLOSE-GRIP PUSH-UPS: Start in a push-up position, but place your 
hands closer together, directly under your shoulders. Lower your 
body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back 
up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees. 
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a step or bench.
SINGLE-LEG GLUTE BRIDGE HOLD: Lie on your back with your 
knees bent and feet flat on the ground. Lift one leg off the ground 
and extend it straight, then push through your other foot to lift your 
hips and hold.
EASIER: Perform a regular glute bridge with both feet on the 
ground.
HARDER: Add pulses at the top of the hold.
PLANK WITH LEG LIFTS: Start in a plank position with your hands 
under your shoulders. Lift one leg off the ground, hold for a 
moment, then switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Lift both legs simultaneously or add a push-up between 
lifts.
EXERCISES
STRONG & STEADY

© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
STRONG & STEADY

fiches.title: CORE ACTIVATOR
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
8
8
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This workout is designed to develop core strength, stability, and endurance using bodyweight 
movements that target the abs, obliques, and lower back. By combining static holds with controlled 
core exercises, participants will improve posture, balance, and overall functional strength.
Focus on core engagement—encourage participants to tighten their abs and avoid overarching their 
lower back.
Control the movement—slow and steady reps will build more strength than rushing through the 
exercises.
Encourage proper breathing—remind participants to exhale during effort (e.g., lifting in a crunch) 
and inhale during the release.
COACHING TIPS
PLANK: Start in a forearm plank position with your elbows under 
your shoulders and your body in a straight line. Hold the position, 
engaging your core. 
EASIER: Perform the plank on your knees. 
HARDER: Lift one leg or one arm off the ground.
HOLLOW BODY HOLD: Lie on your back with your arms extended 
overhead and your legs straight. Lift your arms, shoulders, and legs 
off the ground, creating a hollow shape with your body.
EASIER: Keep your knees bent.  
HARDER: Lower your body closer to the ground without touching it.
BIRD DOGS: Start on all fours with your hands directly under your 
shoulders and your knees under your hips. Extend your right arm 
forward and your left leg backward simultaneously. Hold for a 
moment, then return to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise without extending your arm.
HARDER: Add a crunch under your body.
SIDE PLANK: Lie on your side with your elbow under your shoulder 
and your legs stacked. Lift your hips off the ground, forming a 
straight line from your head to your feet.
EASIER: Perform the plank on your knees.
HARDER: Lift your top leg off the ground.
EXERCISES
CORE ACTIVATOR

fiches.title: BALANCE & CONTROL
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 10
10
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This workout focuses on improving stability, coordination, and body control through strength-based 
balance exercises. By challenging core engagement and single-leg movements, participants will build 
better posture, injury prevention, and overall body awareness.
Encourage slow and controlled movements—rushing through balance exercises reduces their 
effectiveness.
Keep the core engaged at all times—a strong core helps stabilize the body and prevent unnecessary 
wobbling.
Use a focal point—having participants focus on a stationary object can help improve balance and 
reduce shakiness.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
SINGLE-LEG BALANCE: Stand on one leg, lifting the other foot off the 
ground. Hold the position, engaging your core to maintain balance. 
EASIER: Use a wall or chair for support. 
HARDER: Close your eyes or move your lifted leg in different 
directions.
REVERSE PLANK: Sit on the floor with your legs extended and your 
hands placed behind you, fingers pointing towards your feet. Lift 
your hips off the ground, forming a straight line from your head to 
your heels.
EASIER: Bend your knees and keep your feet flat on the ground. 
HARDER: Lift one leg off the ground.
LATERAL LUNGE HOLD: Stand with your feet together. Step one 
foot to the side, lowering your hips into a lunge while keeping your 
other leg straight. Hold the position.
EASIER: Reduce the duration of the hold.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
BURPEES: Stand on one leg with your other foot placed against 
your inner thigh or calf. Bring your hands together at your chest or 
overhead, holding the position.
EASIER: Use a wall for support.
HARDER: a wall for support. HARDER: Close your eyes or reach your 
arms higher.
SINGLE-LEG ROMANIAN DEADLIFT: Stand on one leg with a slight 
bend in your knee. Hinge at the hips, lowering your torso while 
extending the opposite leg backward. Return to the starting position.
EASIER: Use a wall or chair for balance. 
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
BALANCE & CONTROL

fiches.title: STRENGTH STACK
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
12
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This progressive full-body workout builds strength by layering the intensity - increase the number of 
reps with every round. Each exercise focuses on control and endurance, helping participants develop 
muscle stability and stamina over multiple sets.
Encourage participants to focus on consistent pacing—don’t rush through the reps, but also avoid 
unnecessary rest between movements.
Emphasize proper depth on squats and lunges to ensure full muscle activation and avoid 
overloading the knees.
For push-ups and plank variations, remind participants to engage their core to prevent sagging hips 
and lower back strain.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
SUMO SQUATS: Stand with your feet wider than shoulder-width apart 
and your toes turned out. Lower into a squat, keeping your chest up 
and knees tracking over your toes, then return to the starting position.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the squat.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
GLUTE BRIDGE HOLD: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet 
flat on the ground. Lift your hips towards the ceiling and hold the 
position, squeezing your glutes.
EASIER: Perform a regular glute bridge with controlled up-and-down 
movement.
HARDER: Add pulses at the top of the hold.
PLANK WITH ARM AND LEG LIFT: Start in a plank position with your 
hands under your shoulders. Lift one arm and the opposite leg 
simultaneously, hold for a moment, then switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Hold the lifted position longer or add a push-up between 
lifts.
LATERAL LUNGES: Stand with your feet together. Step one foot to 
the side, lowering your hips into a lunge while keeping your other 
leg straight. Return to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the lunge.
HARDER: Add a small jump between lunges.
DIAMOND PUSH-UPS: Start in a standard push-up position, but place 
your hands close together, forming a diamond shape with your 
thumbs and index fingers. Lower your body until your chest nearly 
touches your hands, then push back up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees.
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a bench or step.
STRENGTH STACK

fiches.title: PUSH - UP PROGRESSION
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 14
14
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This upper body strength workout focuses on different push-up variations to build chest, shoulder, 
and triceps strength. By progressing through different angles and hand positions, participants will 
improve overall endurance and control.
Encourage full range of motion, ensuring the chest comes close to the ground on each push-up.
Remind participants to engage their core to maintain a straight body line and avoid sagging hips.
For those struggling with push-ups, suggest starting on knees or an elevated surface before 
progressing to standard variations.
COACHING TIPS
WIDE GRIP PUSH-UPS: Start in a push-up position, but place your 
hands wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your 
chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees. 
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a step or bench.
DIAMOND PUSH-UPS: Start in a standard push-up position, but 
place your hands close together, forming a diamond shape with 
your thumbs and index fingers. Lower your body until your chest 
nearly touches your hands, then push back up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees.
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a bench or step.
ISOMETRIC PUSH-UP HOLD: Start in a standard push-up position. 
Lower your body halfway down and hold the position.
EASIER: Perform the hold on your knees.
HARDER: Hold for a longer duration or add pulses.
PLANK WITH SHOULDER TAPS: Start in a plank position with your 
hands under your shoulders. Tap one shoulder with the opposite 
hand, alternating sides while keeping your body stable.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Increase the speed or add a push-up between taps.
EXERCISES
PUSH-UP PROGRESSION

fiches.title: UPPER BODY ARMOR
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 16
16
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This upper body workout strengthens the arms, shoulders, chest, and back using a variety of 
bodyweight movements. By combining different push-up angles, isometric holds, and 
triceps-focused exercises, participants will build endurance and stability in key upper body muscles.
Encourage proper elbow positioning—elbows should stay close to the body during triceps dips and 
close-grip push-ups to target the arms effectively.
Remind participants to control their descent on push-ups and dips to build strength rather than 
relying on momentum.
Emphasize a tight core and strong shoulder positioning during planks and holds to prevent 
unnecessary strain on the lower back.
COACHING TIPS
TRICEPS DIPS: Sit on the edge of a chair or bench with your hands 
gripping the edge. Slide your hips off the edge and lower your 
body by bending your elbows, then push back up.
EASIER: Keep your feet flat on the ground.
HARDER: Extend your legs straight out in front of you.
PIKE PUSH-UPS: Start in a downward dog position with your hips 
raised and your hands shoulder-width apart. Bend your elbows to 
lower your head towards the ground, then push back up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups with your knees slightly bent.
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a bench or step.
PLANK WITH ARM AND LEG LIFT: Start in a plank position with your 
hands under your shoulders. Lift one arm and the opposite leg 
simultaneously, hold for a moment, then switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Hold the lifted position longer or add a push-up between 
lifts.
CLOSE-GRIP PUSH-UPS: Start in a push-up position, but place your 
hands closer together, directly under your shoulders. Lower your 
body until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back 
up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees.
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a step or bench.
EXERCISES
UPPER BODY ARMOR

fiches.title: LEG DAY ALL DAY
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
18
18
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This lower body strength workout focuses on building leg power, endurance, and stability using only 
bodyweight movements. By incorporating a mix of squats, lunges, and isometric holds, participants 
will develop stronger quads, hamstrings, and glutes.
Emphasize knee alignment—knees should stay in line with the toes during squats and lunges to 
prevent strain.
Encourage full range of motion—squats should go to at least a 90-degree bend, and lunges should 
engage both legs equally.
Remind participants to engage their core to maintain balance and control during single-leg 
exercises.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
SUMO SQUATS: Stand with your feet wider than shoulder-width apart 
and your toes turned out. Lower into a squat, keeping your chest up 
and knees tracking over your toes, then return to the starting position.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the squat.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
GLUTE BRIDGE HOLD: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet 
flat on the ground. Lift your hips towards the ceiling and hold the 
position, squeezing your glutes.
EASIER: Perform the exercise with a smaller range of motion.
HARDER: Hold the lifted position for a longer duration or add weight 
to your hips.
SINGLE-LEG ROMANIAN DEADLIFT: Stand on one leg with a slight 
bend in your knee. Hinge at the hips, lowering your torso while 
extending the opposite leg backward. Return to the starting 
position.
EASIER: Use a wall or chair for balance.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
STATIC SQUAT HOLD: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. 
Lower into a squat and hold the position, keeping your chest up and 
thighs parallel to the floor.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the squat.
HARDER: Hold a weight in each hand.
LUNGES: : Stand with your feet together. Step one foot forward and 
lower your hips until both knees are bent at about 90 degrees. Return 
to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Perform smaller steps.
HARDER: Hold a weight in each hand.
LEG DAY ALL DAY

fiches.title: GLUTES & GRIT
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 20
20
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This lower body workout is focused on strengthening the glutes, hamstrings, and hip muscles to 
improve stability and power. With a mix of isometric holds and dynamic movements, this routine will 
help develop lower body endurance and control.
Encourage full glute activation by pressing through the heels during bridges and maintaining 
control during each rep.
Focus on knee stability during single-leg movements to avoid excess wobbling and improve 
balance.
Emphasize slow and controlled motion rather than speed to maximize muscle engagement and 
avoid using momentum.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
GLUTE BRIDGES: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat 
on the ground. Press into your heels to lift your hips towards the 
ceiling, then lower back down.
EASIER: Perform the exercise with a smaller range of motion.
HARDER: Hold the lifted position for a longer duration or add weight 
to your hips.
FIRE HYDRANT: Start on all fours with your hands under your 
shoulders and knees under your hips. Keeping your knee bent, lift one 
leg out to the side, then lower it back down.
EASIER: Reduce the height of the lift.
HARDER: Add a resistance band around your thighs.
SINGLE-LEG GLUTE BRIDGE: Lie on your back with one foot planted 
and the other leg extended. Press through your grounded foot to lift 
your hips, then lower back down.
EASIER: Perform a regular glute bridge with both feet on the ground.
HARDER: Hold the top position longer or add pulses.
LATERAL LUNGES: Stand with your feet together. Step one foot to 
the side, lowering your hips into a lunge while keeping your other 
leg straight. Return to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the lunge.
HARDER: Add a small jump between lunges.
SINGLE-LEG SQUAT:  Stand on one leg with your other leg extended 
in front of you. Lower your body into a squat while keeping your 
extended leg off the ground, then return to the starting position.
EASIER: Hold onto a wall or chair for balance.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
GLUTES & GRIT

fiches.title: UNLEASHED
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 22
22
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This full-body workout is designed to build functional strength by targeting major muscle groups with 
bodyweight exercises. By combining upper body, lower body, and core-focused movements, 
participants will develop a well-rounded foundation of strength and endurance.
Encourage full range of motion—ensure squats go to at least a 90-degree bend and push-ups go as 
low as possible while maintaining form.
Maintain core engagement throughout—this will help stabilize the body, especially during plank and 
leg exercises.
Emphasize controlled movement over speed—moving with intention will maximize muscle 
engagement and prevent injuries.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
SUMO SQUATS: Stand with your feet wider than shoulder-width apart 
and your toes turned out. Lower into a squat, keeping your chest up 
and knees tracking over your toes, then return to the starting position.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the squat.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
GLUTE BRIDGES: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat 
on the ground. Press into your heels to lift your hips towards the 
ceiling, then lower back down.
EASIER: Perform the exercise with a smaller range of motion.
HARDER: Hold the lifted position for a longer duration or add weight 
to your hips.
WARRIOR POSE (WARRIOR I): Stand with your feet wide apart. Turn 
one foot out and bend your knee to form a 90-degree angle. Extend 
your arms overhead, keeping your hips squared towards the front. 
Hold the position, engaging your core and focusing on your balance.
EASIER: Shorten your stance.
HARDER: Deepen the bend in your front knee.
LUNGES: Stand with your feet together. Step one foot forward and 
lower your hips until both knees are bent at about 90 degrees. 
Return to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Perform smaller steps.
HARDER: Hold a weight in each hand.
PUSH-UPS:  Start in a high plank position with your hands slightly 
wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower your body until your chest 
nearly touches the ground, then push back up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees.
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a step or bench.
UNLEASHED

fiches.title: TOTAL CORE
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 24
24
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This workout challenges the entire core, including the abs, obliques, and lower back, using a mix of 
isometric holds and dynamic movements. By incorporating anti-rotational and stabilization exercises, 
participants will develop better control, posture, and functional strength.
Encourage full-body tension—engaging the glutes, shoulders, and legs during core exercises will 
improve stability.
Maintain proper spinal alignment—remind participants to keep their neck and lower back in a neutral 
position, avoiding excessive arching or rounding.
Focus on controlled breathing—exhaling during effort (e.g., lifting in a crunch) and inhaling during 
relaxation will improve endurance.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
PLANK WITH LEG LIFTS: Start in a plank position with your hands or 
elbows under your shoulders. Lift one leg off the ground, hold for a 
moment, then switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Lift both legs simultaneously or add a push-up between lifts.
SIDE PLANK WITH LEG LIFT: Lie on your side with your elbow under 
your shoulder and your legs stacked. Lift your hips off the ground and 
raise your top leg, keeping your body aligned.
EASIER: Perform a regular side plank without the leg lift.
HARDER: Hold the lifted leg higher or extend your arm overhead.
HOLLOW BODY HOLD: Lie on your back with your arms extended 
overhead and your legs straight. Lift your arms, shoulders, and legs off 
the ground, creating a hollow shape with your body.
EASIER: Keep your knees bent.
HARDER: Lower your body closer to the ground without touching it.
PLANK WITH ARM AND LEG LIFT: Start in a plank position with your 
hands under your shoulders. Lift one arm and the opposite leg 
simultaneously, hold for a moment, then switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
FIGURE FOUR STRETCH (ACTIVE CORE VARIATION):  Sit or lie on the 
floor with your legs extended. Cross one ankle over the opposite 
knee, forming a figure four shape. Engage your core and lift your 
upper body slightly to add resistance.
EASIER: Keep your back straight and perform a smaller crunch.
HARDER: Hold a crunch position longer or add a leg extension.
TOTAL CORE

fiches.title: THE GAUNTLET
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 26
26
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This challenging workout targets full-body strength with a mix of pushing, pulling, and 
core-stabilizing movements. By incorporating a variety of bodyweight exercises, participants will build 
endurance, coordination, and muscular control without the need for equipment.
Encourage proper push-up form—elbows should be at a 45-degree angle, and the core should 
remain engaged to prevent sagging hips.
Focus on slow and controlled movements for leg and core exercises to maximize muscle activation.
Remind participants to breathe steadily—inhale during the lowering phase and exhale during the 
push or contraction phase.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
WIDE GRIP PUSH-UPS: Start in a standard push-up position, but 
place your hands wider than shoulder-width apart. Lower your body 
until your chest nearly touches the ground, then push back up.
EASIER: Perform the push-ups on your knees.
HARDER: Elevate your feet on a step or bench.
SINGLE-LEG ROMANIAN DEADLIFT: Stand on one leg with a slight 
bend in your knee. Hinge at the hips, lowering your torso while 
extending the opposite leg backward. Return to the starting position.
EASIER: Use a wall or chair for balance.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
PLANK WITH LEG LIFTS: Start in a plank position with your hands or 
elbows under your shoulders. Lift one leg off the ground, hold for a 
moment, then switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Lift both legs simultaneously or add a push-up between lifts.
LATERAL SHUFFLES: Stand with your feet hip-width apart and your 
knees slightly bent. Shuffle quickly to the side, keeping your body 
low.
EASIER: Slow down the pace.
HARDER: Add a touch to the ground with each step.
WALL SIT: Stand with your back against a wall and your feet 
shoulder-width apart. Slide down until your knees are bent at a 
90-degree angle and hold the position.
EASIER: Reduce the duration of the hold.
HARDER: Hold a weight in your hands.
BURPEES: Begin standing with your feet shoulder-width apart. Lower 
into a squat, place your hands on the ground, and jump your feet back 
into a plank position. Perform a push-up, then jump your feet back 
towards your hands, and explosively jump up, reaching your arms 
overhead.
EASIER: Skip the push-up.
HARDER: Add a tuck jump at the end.
THE GAUNTLET

fiches.title: LOWER BODY IGNITION
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 28
28
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This workout is designed to build explosive lower body strength using bodyweight movements that 
develop power, speed, and endurance. By incorporating dynamic and controlled exercises, 
participants will improve muscle activation and stability in their legs and glutes.
Focus on explosive movement during jump exercises while maintaining soft landings to protect the 
knees.
Encourage deep engagement of the glutes and hamstrings in bridge and squat movements to 
maximize power output.
Remind participants to use controlled movements for single-leg exercises to build stability and 
balance.
COACHING TIPS
EXERCISES
JUMP SQUATS: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Lower into 
a squat, then explosively jump up, reaching your arms overhead. 
EASIER: Perform regular squats without jumping. 
HARDER: Increase the depth of the squat or add weights. 
SINGLE-LEG GLUTE BRIDGE: Lie on your back with your knees bent 
and feet flat on the ground. Lift one leg off the ground and extend it 
straight, then push through your other foot to lift your hips. 
EASIER: Perform a regular glute bridge with both feet on the ground. 
HARDER: Add pulses at the top of the hold. 
FORWARD AND BACKWARD HOPS: Stand with your feet hip-width 
apart. Jump forward a small distance, then jump backward to the 
starting position. 
EASIER: Perform small hops. 
HARDER: Increase the distance of each hop. 
WALL SIT: Stand with your back against a wall and your feet 
shoulder-width apart. Slide down until your knees are bent at a 
90-degree angle and hold the position.
EASIER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
HARDER: Perform the exercise on your knees.
LATERAL LUNGES:  Stand with your feet together. Step one foot to 
the side, lowering your hips into a lunge while keeping your other leg 
straight. Return to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Reduce the depth of the lunge. 
HARDER: Add a small jump between lunges. 
LOWER BODY IGNITION

fiches.title: BODYWEIGHT BLAST
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 30
30
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This recovery circuit is designed to help the body transition from intense exercise to rest. It focuses 
on improving flexibility, reducing muscle tension, and promoting relaxation. These low-impact 
movements are perfect for cooling down and resetting after a workout.
Encourage participants to focus on slow, deep breathing to promote relaxation and recovery.
Emphasize holding each stretch gently, avoiding any bouncing or forced movements.
Remind participants to focus on areas of tightness and adjust their range of motion as needed.
COACHING TIPS
CHILD'S POSE: Kneel on the floor with your big toes touching and 
knees spread apart. Sit back on your heels and extend your arms 
forward, lowering your torso between your thighs.
EASIER: Keep your knees together.  
HARDER: Reach your arms further forward for a deeper stretch.
COBRA POSE: Lie face-down on the floor with your hands under 
your shoulders and your elbows close to your body. Press into your 
hands to lift your chest off the floor, keeping your hips grounded.
EASIER: Lift only to a comfortable height.
HARDER: Extend your arms fully to increase the stretch.
FIGURE FOUR STRETCH: Sit or lie on the floor with your legs 
extended. Cross one ankle over the opposite knee, forming a figure 
four shape. Lean forward slightly to increase the stretch.
EASIER: Keep your back straight.
HARDER: Pull your crossed leg closer to your body.
NECK ROTATIONS: Stand or sit comfortably with your back straight. 
Slowly rotate your head in a circular motion, first clockwise, then 
counterclockwise.
EASIER: Reduce the range of motion.
HARDER: Increase the duration of each rotation.
EXERCISES
BODYWEIGHT BLAST

fiches.title: WORKOUT  JOURNALS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 32
32
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
WORKOUT 
JOURNALS

fiches.title: STRONG & STEADY
fiches.body:
33
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
STRONG & STEADY
WORKOUT JOURNAL
Did you notice a difference in how your muscles felt when performing slow, controlled reps vs. moving quickly? 
Describe the difference.  
Which muscle group do you feel was worked the hardest today? What daily activities or sports might benefit from 
strengthening these muscles?  
How does proper form and technique affect injury prevention in strength training? Research common mistakes 
people make in bodyweight exercises and explain how they can lead to injuries.

fiches.title: CORE ACTIVATOR
fiches.body:
34
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 34
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
CORE ACTIVATOR
WORKOUT JOURNAL
Which core exercise in this workout required the most stability? What made it challenging?  
Why is core strength important for more than just ab definition? Explain how a strong core benefits movement, 
posture, and injury prevention.  
Research the difference between anti-flexion, anti-extension, and anti-rotation core exercises. How do these dif­
ferent movements contribute to full core strength?

fiches.title: BALANCE & CONTROL
fiches.body:
35
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
BALANCE & CONTROL
WORKOUT JOURNAL
Which exercise required the most balance today? How did you adjust to maintain stability?  
How does core engagement affect balance training? Why is a strong core essential for single-leg and control-
based exercises?  
Research proprioception and its role in strength and balance training. How can training balance improve reaction 
time and injury prevention?

fiches.title: STRENGTH STACK
fiches.body:
36
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com 36
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
STRENGTH STACK
WORKOUT JOURNAL
Which exercise in this workout required the most control and focus? Why?  
If you had to modify one exercise today, what change did you make? How did it affect your performance?  
Explain the concept of time under tension (TUT) and how it impacts muscle growth. How can you use this concept 
to make bodyweight exercises more effective?

fiches.title: PUSH - UP PROGRESSION
fiches.body:
37
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
PUSH-UP PROGRESSION 
WORKOUT JOURNAL
Which push-up variation felt the most natural for you? Which one do you need to work on the most?  
Push-ups engage more than just the arms. List three other muscle groups they work and explain how these 
muscles contribute to overall strength.  
Research the role of core strength in push-ups. Why is a strong core important in maintaining good push-up form?

fiches.title: INTRODUCTION
fiches.body:
3
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Welcome to No Equipment Tabata Circuits! This resource is designed to help you lead 
fun, high-energy, and effective workouts that require zero equipment. Whether you’re 
working with students, athletes, or general fitness participants, these circuits provide a 
structured and engaging way to improve cardio, endurance, strength, and agility using just 
bodyweight movements.
The Power of Tabata Training
Tabata training is a form of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) that alternates 20 seconds 
of work with 10 seconds of rest, typically repeated for multiple rounds. This method is 
an efficient and adaptable way to boost cardiovascular fitness, muscular endurance, and 
overall athletic performance.
One of the biggest benefits of no-equipment Tabata workouts is their versatility. They can 
be done anywhere—in a gym, classroom, outdoor space, or even at home—making them 
ideal for PE classes, sports conditioning, and general fitness. The short bursts of effort 
combined with brief recovery periods keep participants engaged while improving both 
aerobic and anaerobic fitness.
What’s Included?
Inside this resource, you’ll find 12 ready-to-use Tabata circuits, each with a unique focus, 
from strength and cardio to agility and mobility. Each workout comes with an instructor 
guide that includes:
•	
Exercise descriptions with modifications for different fitness levels
•	
Coaching tips to ensure proper form, technique, and engagement
•	
Clear work/rest instructions for structured and effective training
As a special bonus, we’ve also included a printable workout tracker to help participants 
monitor progress, set fitness goals, and stay motivated throughout their training.
These circuits are perfect for PE classes, sports teams, group fitness, or solo training. 
Whether you’re leading a session or working out independently, these workouts are 
accessible, challenging, and fun—all without the need for expensive equipment.
Let’s get moving and experience the intensity, efficiency, and excitement of Tabata 
training—anytime, anywhere!
INTRODUCTION

fiches.title: WORKOUTS
fiches.body:
5
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
WORKOUTS

fiches.title: TABATA 101
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
6
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
This Tabata workout features gentle, beginner-friendly movements designed to keep kids and 
newcomers active while building confidence. With simple, low-impact exercises, participants can 
improve coordination and endurance without high-intensity jumps or complex moves!
Encourage students to engage their core and calves when balancing on their toes. If needed, they 
can lightly touch a wall for support instead of grabbing it.
In the Bird Dog exercise, movement should be slow and controlled, focusing on balance and core 
stability rather than speed.
During Glute Bridges, remind students to actively squeeze their glutes at the top and avoid 
over-arching the lower back.
COACHING TIPS
BALANCE ON TOES: Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Raise 
your heels off the ground and balance on your toes. Hold the 
position, engaging your core to maintain stability.
EASIER: Hold onto a wall or chair for support.  
HARDER: Close your eyes or perform calf raises.
BIRD DOGS: Start on all fours with your hands directly under your 
shoulders and your knees under your hips. Extend your right arm 
forward and your left leg backward simultaneously. Hold for a 
moment, then return to the starting position and switch sides.
EASIER: Perform the exercise without extending your arm. 
HARDER: Add a crunch under your body.
GLUTE BRIDGES: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet 
flat on the ground. Press into your heels to lift your hips towards the 
ceiling, then lower back down.
EASIER: Perform the exercise with a smaller range of motion.
HARDER: Hold the lifted position for a longer duration or add weight 
to your hips.
TOE TAPS: Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Quickly tap your 
toes on an elevated surface, alternating feet.
EASIER: Tap your toes on the ground instead.
HARDER: Increase the speed or use a higher surface.
EXERCISES
TABATA 101

fiches.title: TABATA 101
fiches.body:
DIFFICULTY
BALANCE ON TOES 
20 secs work > 10 secs rest
2-4 rounds then rotate
BIRD DOGS

2-4 rounds then rotate

TOE TAPS 
20 secs work > 10 secs rest
2-4 rounds then rotate
EMPHASIS
INSTRUCTIONS
Low-impact, 
Coordination
For each exercise, do 20 seconds of work followed 
by 10 seconds of rest. Complete 2-4 rounds of one 
exercise before moving to the next.
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
TABATA 101
