fiches.title: ODD ONE OUT ODD ONE OUT
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
1
ODD ONE OUT
ODD ONE OUT
HOW TO PLAY
1.	 Divide your students into 2-3 teams and place them in traditional relay race lines.
2.	 Set up 11 cards in a grid at the opposite end of the play area. There should be 10 
matching cards (2 sets of 5) plus one card with no match (the odd one out).
3.	 On the ‘go’ command, the first child in each line must run down to the grid of 
plates and turn them over to reveal two exercise cards.
4.	 If the cards do not match, the child leaves them face up in the same position and 
returns to the back of the line.
5.	 If the cards do match, they bring the cards back and perform the exercise 
challenge. 
6.	 Once the challenge is completed, the next child in line will run to the grid of plates 
and flip over two more, looking for a pair.
7.	 The goal of the game is to figure out which of the 11 cards is the odd one out. The 
first team to figure it out wins.
Odd One Out Setup

fiches.title: MYSTERY CARD MYSTERY CARD
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
4
MYSTERY CARD
MYSTERY CARD
HOW TO PLAY
1.	 Divide your students into 2-3 teams and place them in traditional relay race lines.
2.	 Set up a grid of exercise cards at the opposite end of the play area. 
3.	 The teacher will announce one card as the mystery card. For example, the 
“Burpees” exercise can be your mystery card.
4.	 On the ‘go’ command, the first child in each line must run down to the grid of 
plates and turn them over to reveal two exercise cards.
5.	 If they do not find the mystery card, the child must place the cards back in the 
same location they found them (face down) and return to the back of their line. 
6.	 If they do find the mystery card, the child picks up the plate and runs it back to 
their team.
7.	 The entire team must then perform that exercise challenge. When the exercise is 
complete, that team wins one point.
8.	 Re-set the game by placing the card back in its original location. Choose a 
different mystery card this time and play again.
9.	 Play for a set amount of time, or until one team reaches 5 points.
Mystery Card Setup
Grid Of Plates

sections.How To Play:
1.	 Divide your students into 2-3 teams and place them in traditional relay race lines.
2.	 Set up a grid of exercise cards at the opposite end of the play area.
3.	 The teacher will announce one card as the mystery card. For example, the
“Burpees” exercise can be your mystery card.
4.	 On the ‘go’ command, the first child in each line must run down to the grid of
plates and turn them over to reveal two exercise cards.
5.	 If they do not find the mystery card, the child must place the cards back in the
same location they found them (face down) and return to the back of their line.
6.	 If they do find the mystery card, the child picks up the plate and runs it back to
their team.
7.	 The entire team must then perform that exercise challenge. When the exercise is
complete, that team wins one point.
8.	 Re-set the game by placing the card back in its original location. Choose a
different mystery card this time and play again.
9.	 Play for a set amount of time, or until one team reaches 5 points.
Mystery Card Setup
Grid Of Plates

fiches.title: BRAIN TWISTER BRAIN TWISTER
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
BRAIN TWISTER
BRAIN TWISTER
HOW TO PLAY
1.	 Divide your students into 2 teams
2.	 For each team, set up 10 exercise cards on the floor in a specific layout with the 
exercises facing up. Each team will have the same, exact layout with identical 
cards. 
3.	 Take a picture of the layout for your own reference.
4.	 Give each team 20 seconds to study the layout of the cards. 
5.	 Then, pick up the cards and mix up them up into a pile for each team. 
6.	 On ‘go’ both teams will attempt to set the cards back into the exact same layout 
they started with.
7.	 The first team to set the cards up properly wins. 
Brain Twister Setup

6.	 On ‘go’ both teams will attempt to set the cards back into the exact same layout
they started with.

fiches.title: EXERCISE BINGO EXERCISE BINGO
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
EXERCISE BINGO
EXERCISE BINGO
HOW TO PLAY
1.	 Divide your students into 2-4 teams.
2.	 For each team, set up a 3x3 grid of exercise cards. All cards should be unique (no 
matching pairs) and facing up.
3.	 The teacher will have the remaining cards in a large pile.  
4.	 From your pile, flip over one of the cards and announce it out loud
5.	 If a team has the matching card, they all perform that exercise challenge, then flip 
over the card to show it has been completed.
6.	 The first team to get all 9 cards flipped over wins.
Exercise Bingo Setup

sections.How To Play:
1.	 Divide your students into 2-4 teams.
2.	 For each team, set up a 3x3 grid of exercise cards. All cards should be unique (no
matching pairs) and facing up.
3.	 The teacher will have the remaining cards in a large pile.
4.	 From your pile, flip over one of the cards and announce it out loud
5.	 If a team has the matching card, they all perform that exercise challenge, then flip
over the card to show it has been completed.
6.	 The first team to get all 9 cards flipped over wins.
Exercise Bingo Setup

fiches.title: EXERCISE STATIONS EXERCISE STATIONS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
EXERCISE STATIONS
EXERCISE STATIONS
HOW TO PLAY
1.	 You will need one pair of exercise cards for each student – so if you have 20 
students you will need 20 pairs of exercise cards
2.	 Take one card from each pair and place it in the middle of the room.
3.	 Take the matching card from that pair and create a “station” by taping it to the 
wall or to a tall cone around the outside of the room.
4.	 On “go” the students run to the middle of the room and pick up an exercise card. 
They will then run around the outside of the room looking for the matching station. 
When they find the station, they complete the exercise challenge.
5.	 They will then return their card to the middle of the room and find another one.
6.	 The goal is for each student to complete as many exercise challenges as possible 
in the time allowed. 
Exercise Stations Setup

fiches.title: TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
3
TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS
TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS
LEARNING OBJECTIVES
This activity will help students work on their strength, endurance, coordination, 
flexibility,balance, cognitive skills and teamwork.
EQUIPMENT & MATERIALS
The lesson kit includes 48 matching exercise cards that you will need to print out. We 
recommend that you attach each card to a paper plate or laminate it. No equipment is 
required for the exercise challenges.
SETUP
1.	 Divide your students into 2-3 teams and place them in traditional relay race lines.
2.	 Print out all the exercise card pages in the lesson kit.
3.	 Each page contains two matching exercise cards with a simple exercise challenge and 
illustration. Cut each page in half so you have a matching pair.
4.	 Staple, tape or glue each exercise card to the bottom of a paper plate.
5.	 Once completed, place the plates face down into a 6x8 grid at one end of your play area. 
The exercise cards should be hidden from view.
PE Memory Match Setup
Grid Of Plates

4.	 Staple, tape or glue each exercise card to the bottom of a paper plate.
5.	 Once completed, place the plates face down into a 6x8 grid at one end of your play area.
The exercise cards should be hidden from view.

fiches.title: TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
4
TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS
TEACHER INSTRUCTIONS
HOW TO PLAY
1.	 Divide your students into 2-3 teams and place them in a traditional relay race line.
2.	 On the ‘go’ command, the first child in each line must run down to the grid of plates 
and turn them over to reveal two exercise cards.
3.	 If the exercise cards do not match, the child must place them back in the same 
location they found them (face down) and return to the back of their line. 
4.	 If the exercise cards do match, the child picks up the plates and runs them back to 
their team.
5.	 When the student brings back the matching exercise cards, the entire team must 
perform that exercise challenge.  For example, if the cards say “do 5 push-ups,” every 
child on the team must perform the 5 push-ups.
6.	 Once the challenge is completed, the next child in line will run to the grid of plates 
and flip over two more, following the same process as the previous child.
7.	 The game is over when all the exercise cards have been removed from the grid.
8.	 Which ever team collected the most exercise cards at the end of the game wins the 
race.

sections.How To Play:
1.	 Divide your students into 2-3 teams and place them in a traditional relay race line.
2.	 On the ‘go’ command, the first child in each line must run down to the grid of plates
and turn them over to reveal two exercise cards.
3.	 If the exercise cards do not match, the child must place them back in the same
location they found them (face down) and return to the back of their line.
4.	 If the exercise cards do match, the child picks up the plates and runs them back to
their team.
5.	 When the student brings back the matching exercise cards, the entire team must
perform that exercise challenge.  For example, if the cards say “do 5 push-ups,” every
child on the team must perform the 5 push-ups.
6.	 Once the challenge is completed, the next child in line will run to the grid of plates
and flip over two more, following the same process as the previous child.
7.	 The game is over when all the exercise cards have been removed from the grid.
8.	 Which ever team collected the most exercise cards at the end of the game wins the
race.

Welcome to PE Relay Race Games! Inside this resource you’ll find 15 fun and exciting games 
that use a traditional relay race format. The games are designed to engage students of all 
ages and abilities while also promoting fitness, coordination, and teamwork. You can use them 
for PE class, recess, after school programs, or any environment where you want to get kids up 
and moving!
Each game includes the following information:
1. Focus: The components of fitness or skill areas that the activity focuses on:
2. Group Size: The minimum number of participants required for the lesson.
3. Difficulty:  A rating from 1 (easiest) to 5 (hardest). Harder games require more skill, more setup
or more complex rules.
4. Equipment: Required sports equipment or instructor materials.
5.
Instructions:  How to play the game, step by step.
6. Variations: Modifications to make the game easier or harder. Or just a different way to play.

fiches.title: Hula Skating Hula Skating
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Allow the kids to move with one foot in the hoop and one foot outside of the hoop to make it 
easier. Or use two hoops (one foot in each hoop) to make it harder.
Hula Skating
Hula Skating
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 hula hoops
•	
Cones
Setup
•	
Put children in traditional relay race lines.  The first kid 
in line will stand in a hula hoop at the start line.  Place a 
cone at the other end of the room for each team.
Instructions
1.	 This is a relay race where the kids will “skate” across the floor with their hula hoops.
2.	 Both feet must stay inside the hula hoops. They will move by sliding their feet across the ground 
to push the hula hoop forward. This movement looks a lot like skating!
3.	 The first kid in line will skate across the floor, around their cone and back to their line, then tag 
the next kid in line.
4.	 Continue this pattern until all the kids on one team have finished.

sections.Variation:
Allow the kids to move with one foot in the hoop and one foot outside of the hoop to make it
easier. Or use two hoops (one foot in each hoop) to make it harder.
Hula Skating

3.	 The first kid in line will skate across the floor, around their cone and back to their line, then tag

4.	 Continue this pattern until all the kids on one team have finished.

fiches.title: Basket Blitz Basket Blitz
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
6
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
For older kids, they must make their layup before returning to the relay line. For younger kids, lower 
the height of the basket or play with a smaller ball.
Basket Blitz
Basket Blitz
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 basketballs
Setup
•	
Place the students in 2 traditional relay race lines.  Give 
the first child in line a basketball.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first kid in line must dribble their basketball towards the other end of the room and 
try to make a basket.
2.	 If they make a basket, they must dribble the ball back to their line and hand off the ball to the 
next student on their team.
3.	 If they miss their shot, they get two more attempts to make a basket.  If they miss all three times, 
they must run back to the start line and hand the ball off to the next student.
4.	 The first team to finish the race wins the round.  Mix up the teams and start a new round.

sections.Variation:
For older kids, they must make their layup before returning to the relay line. For younger kids, lower
the height of the basket or play with a smaller ball.
Basket Blitz

next student on their team.
3.	 If they miss their shot, they get two more attempts to make a basket.  If they miss all three times,
they must run back to the start line and hand the ball off to the next student.
4.	 The first team to finish the race wins the round.  Mix up the teams and start a new round.

fiches.title: Bean Bag Bowling Bean Bag Bowling
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
7
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Use water bottles if you don’t have bowling pins. Adjust the throwing distance to make it easier or 
harder.
Bean Bag Bowling
Bean Bag Bowling
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
20 bowling pins
•	
Bean bags
Setup
•	
Set up the kids in traditional relay race lines.  Place 
10 pins for each team on the opposite end line of the 
room.  Give the first kid in each line a bean bag.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first student in line must slide the bean bag across the room towards to bowling pins, 
attempting to knock over pins.
2.	 Once they make their toss, they must run after their bean bag, pick it up, and return it to the 
next student in line.  
3.	 The next student will follow the same pattern.
4.	 Once a pin gets knocked down, the students should leave it knocked over.
5.	 The first team to knock down all their pins wins that round.
6.	 Once completed, the kids will pick up the pins, stand them up, and start a new round.
7.	 If a team accidentally knocks over the other team’s pins, they still count as being knocked over.

sections.Variation:
Use water bottles if you don’t have bowling pins. Adjust the throwing distance to make it easier or
harder.
Bean Bag Bowling
Bean Bag Bowling
DIFFICULTY

fiches.title: Rolling Rumble Rolling Rumble
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
8
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Kids can sit on the scooters instead of lying flat. If you don’t have scooters you can do this as a 
wheelbarrow race instead.
Rolling Rumble
Rolling Rumble
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 scooters
•	
2 cones
Setup
•	
Place the students in traditional relay race lines.  Give 
each team one scooter and place cones at the end of 
the room as shown.
Instructions
1.	 To start the race, the first child in line is the “rider” – they lie flat on top of the scooter (at the 
start line) and place their hands in a safe position, keeping them out of the path of the wheels.
2.	 The second child in line (the “pusher”) will lift up the rider’s legs and safely and carefully push 
them across the floor, around the cone and back to the line.
3.	 From there, the pusher becomes the rider, and the next child in line becomes the pusher.
4.	 Continue in this pattern until every kid on one team has been the pusher and the rider once.
5.	 The first team to finish wins the race.

sections.Variation:
Kids can sit on the scooters instead of lying flat. If you don’t have scooters you can do this as a
wheelbarrow race instead.
Rolling Rumble
Rolling Rumble
DIFFICULTY

2.	 The second child in line (the “pusher”) will lift up the rider’s legs and safely and carefully push
them across the floor, around the cone and back to the line.
3.	 From there, the pusher becomes the rider, and the next child in line becomes the pusher.
4.	 Continue in this pattern until every kid on one team has been the pusher and the rider once.
5.	 The first team to finish wins the race.

fiches.title: Ready, Set, Hike Ready, Set, Hike
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
9
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
If a team ever drops the ball to the ground, the entire group must run back to the start line and 
begin again. 
Ready, Set, Hike
Ready, Set, Hike
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
A football for each group
•	
Cones  
Setup
•	
Put students in groups of 4 or 5.  Each group must form 
a line at the start line.  Place a cone in each corner of 
the room to designate the start and finish lines.  Give 
the first student in line a football. All kids have their 
backs toward the finish line.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first student in line must pass the football over their head to the person behind them 
who takes the ball.
2.	 After they hand the ball off to the person behind them, they run to the end of the line and wait 
for their turn again.
3.	 The next student who gets the ball must football ‘hike’ it between their legs (facing backward) to 
the student behind them in line.  Again, once the ball is passed they must run to the end of the 
line to await their turn again.
4.	 When a student has the ball in their hands, they are not allowed to take steps with the ball at any 
time.
5.	 The first team that gets the ball to the end line wins the race.

sections.Variation:
If a team ever drops the ball to the ground, the entire group must run back to the start line and
begin again.
Ready, Set, Hike
Ready, Set, Hike
DIFFICULTY

•

•

a line at the start line.  Place a cone in each corner of
the room to designate the start and finish lines.  Give
the first student in line a football. All kids have their
backs toward the finish line.

1.	 On ‘go’ the first student in line must pass the football over their head to the person behind them
who takes the ball.
2.	 After they hand the ball off to the person behind them, they run to the end of the line and wait
for their turn again.
3.	 The next student who gets the ball must football ‘hike’ it between their legs (facing backward) to
the student behind them in line.  Again, once the ball is passed they must run to the end of the
line to await their turn again.
4.	 When a student has the ball in their hands, they are not allowed to take steps with the ball at any
time.
5.	 The first team that gets the ball to the end line wins the race.

fiches.title: Spaghetti and Meatballs Spaghetti and Meatballs
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
10
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To make it harder, use a smaller ball like a volleyball or tennis ball.
Spaghetti and Meatballs
Spaghetti and Meatballs
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 large beach balls
•	
2 pool noodles 
•	
2 cones
Setup
•	
Put the kids in traditional relay race lines.  Give the first 
kid in each line a pool noodle (spaghetti) and beach  
ball (meatball).  Set 2 cones up an the other end of the 
gym for the kids to move around.
Instructions
1.	 The object of the race is to use your ”spaghetti” to push the “meatball” all the way around the 
cone and back.
2.	 On “go” the first kid in line will use their “spaghetti” to push their “meatball” all the way around 
the cone and back to the front of the line.
3.	 They will then hand off the pool noodle to the next kid in line and repeat.
4.	 The first team to finish the race wins the round.

sections.Variation:
To make it harder, use a smaller ball like a volleyball or tennis ball.
Spaghetti and Meatballs
Spaghetti and Meatballs
DIFFICULTY
sections.Equipment:
•
2 large beach balls
•
2 pool noodles
•
2 cones
sections.Setup:
•
Put the kids in traditional relay race lines.  Give the first
kid in each line a pool noodle (spaghetti) and beach
ball (meatball).  Set 2 cones up an the other end of the
gym for the kids to move around.
sections.Instructions:
1.	 The object of the race is to use your ”spaghetti” to push the “meatball” all the way around the
cone and back.
2.	 On “go” the first kid in line will use their “spaghetti” to push their “meatball” all the way around
the cone and back to the front of the line.
3.	 They will then hand off the pool noodle to the next kid in line and repeat.
4.	 The first team to finish the race wins the round.

fiches.title: Bean Bag Burglars Bean Bag Burglars
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
11
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Change the locomotor – have the kids hop, crawl, skip or crabwalk to retrieve the bean bags.
Bean Bag Burglars
Bean Bag Burglars
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
50 bean bags
•	
Milk crate
Setup
•	
Divide the class into 2 teams and place each team on 
one half of the room.  Kids should form a line on the 
sideline. Put an equal amount of bean bags on both end 
lines of the room. Place a milk crate or similar container 
between the two teams.
Instructions
1.	 On “go,” the first kid from each team will run across the room and take one bean bag from the 
other team’s stash.
2.	 Once they take the bean bag, they run back to the sideline as fast as they can, drop it in the milk 
crate, tag the next person, then go to the back of the line.
3.	 The next kid in line repeats this.
4.	 Each kid will get multiple turns going, but the first team to steal all of the other team’s bean bags 
wins.

sections.Variation:
Change the locomotor – have the kids hop, crawl, skip or crabwalk to retrieve the bean bags.

fiches.title: Basketball Boulevard Basketball Boulevard
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
To make it harder, if they lose control of the ball while dribbling, they have to start again at 
the beginning of the zig-zag course.  
Basketball Boulevard
Basketball Boulevard
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 basketballs
•	
6-10 cones
•	
2 bowling pins
Setup
•	
Place the kids in 2 traditional relay race lines.  Use cones 
to set up a zig-zag course to dribble through. Place a 
bowling pin for each team at the other end of the room.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first kid in each line dribbles through the zig-zag course from one end to the other.
2.	 When they reach the last cone, they pick up their dribble and try to knock over the pin using a 
chest pass. If they miss, they must retrieve their ball, run back to the cone and try again.
3.	 Continue until you knock over the pin. Then speed dribble back to your line and hand the ball off 
to the next kid in line.
4.	 The teacher will re-set the pins when they get knocked down.
5.	 The first team to finish the course wins.

fiches.title: Agility Olympic Agility Olympic
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
13
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
If you don’t have the equipment you can use chalk to mark the different obstacles on 
the blacktop. You can also introduce any other equipment you have to create additional 
challenges. 
Agility Olympic
Agility Olympic
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
8 cones
•	
2 speed ladders
•	
8 hula hoops
Setup
•	
Place the students in traditional relay race lines.  In front 
of each line place 3 cones, followed by an exercise 
ladder, followed by 4 hula hoops as shown in the image 
below.  Place a cone at each team’s end line.
Instructions
1.	 The 2 teams will race to complete the agility course first.
2.	 Begin by weaving back and forth through the cones as fast as you can.
3.	 Next, run through the speed ladder, stepping twice in each square (one left foot, one right foot).
4.	 Finally, hop through the hula hoops one by one.
5.	 At the end of the course, turn around, sprint back and tag the next kid in line.
6.	 The first team that completes the course wins.

fiches.title: Noodle Jump Relay Noodle Jump Relay
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
14
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
For an extra challenge, try having 2 or 3 pool noodle holders for each team!
Noodle Jump Relay
Noodle Jump Relay
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 pool noodles 
•	
2 cones
Setup
•	
Put the kids in traditional relay race lines.  Each team will 
get a pool noodle. Set up a cone for each team at the 
far end of the room.
Instructions
1.	 One student on each team will be designated as the pool noodle holder for their team. This 
person will stand about halfway between the start line and cone.
2.	 On go, the first kid in line will run towards their cone. The pool noodle holder will hold the noodle 
a few inches off the ground. The runner will jump over the pool noodle, then circle around the 
cone.
3.	 The holder will then raise the pool noodle to shoulder height. 
4.	 On the way back, the runner will limbo underneath the pool noodle, then run back to tag the 
next kid in line. 
5.	 The first team to finish the race wins the round.

sections.Variation:
For an extra challenge, try having 2 or 3 pool noodle holders for each team!

fiches.title: Bucket Ball Bucket Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
15
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Set up multiple buckets for each team, some closer to the shooters, some farther away.  
The farther buckets can be worth 2 or 3 points.
Bucket Ball
Bucket Ball
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 large buckets
•	
2 utility balls
Setup
•	
Place the kids in 2 traditional relay race lines.  Place a 
bucket or basket about 15 feet away from the start line, 
and give the first kid in each line a utility ball.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first student in line tries to bounce their ball into the bucket.
2.	 Whether they make it or miss it, they must retrieve the ball and hand it off to the next child in 
line.
3.	 Each time a basket is made, their team receives a point.
4.	 If the bucket tips over or moves, it is the shooter’s responsibility to place it back into the correct 
location.
5.	 Play for 5 minutes, or until one team makes 5 points.

sections.Variation:

The farther buckets can be worth 2 or 3 points.

fiches.title: Temple of Doom Temple of Doom
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
16
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of rolling the yoga ball, have the kids dribble a soccer ball or basketball through 
the course.
Temple of Doom
Temple of Doom
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
20-30 cones
•	
1 large yoga ball
•	
Stopwatch or timer
Setup
•	
Place the children on equal size teams.  Use as many 
cones as you can to set up a ‘rolling track’ for the kids 
to move the ball through.  Have one large yoga ball at 
the start line with the first student in line.  Be as creative 
as you want with your ‘rolling track.’
Instructions
1.	 The teacher will use a stopwatch to time each team to see which group has the fastest time.
2.	 On ‘go’ the first team will begin the race.  The first child must use their hands to roll the big ball 
through the cones.
3.	 Once they get to the end, they give the ball to the next child who follows the same pattern.
4.	 If a cone gets knocked over at any time, 1 second will be added to the team’s score.
5.	 The students must move the ball through the designated area.
6.	 Once completed, the teacher will give them their time, and then the next group will begin.

sections.Variation:
Instead of rolling the yoga ball, have the kids dribble a soccer ball or basketball through
the course.

fiches.title: Racket Rendezvous Racket Rendezvous
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
17
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Instead of a push-up, they have to go back to their line and start over.
Racket Rendezvous
Racket Rendezvous
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 tennis rackets
•	
2 scarves
•	
2 cones
Setup
•	
Place the children in 2 traditional relay race lines.  Give 
the first student in line a tennis racket and a scarf.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first student in line must lay the entire scarf on top of their tennis racket and move 
towards their cone.
2.	 Once they get to their cone, they must go around it and come back to the start line.
3.	 The children may not use their hands to balance the scarf on top of the racket.
4.	 The children may move as fast as they would like, but if the scarf falls to the ground, they must 
stop, do 1 push-up, pick up the scarf and balance it back on top of the racket before they start 
moving again.
5.	 When they get back to the start line, they must hand off the racket and scarf to the next child 
who follows the same pattern.
6.	 The first team to finish wins the round.

fiches.title: Wind Warriors Wind Warriors
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
18
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Try using larger or smaller pieces of cardboard
Wind Warriors
Wind Warriors
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 large pieces of cardboard
•	
2 scarves 
•	
2 cones
Setup
•	
Place the children in 2 traditional relay race lines.  Give 
the first student in each line a large piece of cardboard 
and a scarf.
Instructions
1.	 The first kid in line must place the scarf on the ground in front of them.
2.	 On ‘go’ the first student in line will begin flapping their piece of cardboard at their scarf, trying 
to create a ‘blowing wind’ that will push the scarf forward.
3.	 They must blow the scarf towards their cone, go around it, and come back to the start line as 
they hand off the cardboard to the next child.
4.	 The next child must follow the same pattern.
5.	 The first team to finish wins the round.

fiches.title: Beach Ball Buddies Beach Ball Buddies
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
19
FOCUS
GROUP SIZE
VARIATION
Kids can stand belly to belly or side to side instead of back to back.
Beach Ball Buddies
Beach Ball Buddies
DIFFICULTY
Equipment
•	
2 large beach balls
•	
2 cones
Setup
•	
Place the kids in 2 traditional relay race lines.  Give each 
team a beach ball or large exercise ball.
Instructions
1.	 On ‘go’ the first two kids in each line must balance a beach ball between them, standing back to 
back.
2.	 Without using their hands, they must walk with the beach ball balanced between them to their 
team’s cone, go around the cone, then come back to start.
3.	 If the ball drops to the ground, they must stop, balance it again, then continue on their path.
4.	 When they get back to the start line, they hand the ball off to the next 2 kids who follow the 
same pattern.
5.	 The first team to finish the race wins the round.

fiches.title: TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
5
TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
What You Need
•	
At least one basketball hoop
•	
5-10 basketballs (one per child is best but they can be shared)
•	
Pens or pencils
•	
1 printed copy of each Basketball Station Card
•	
1 printed Basketball Scavenger Hunt Worksheet per student or team
How to Play
1.	 Print out one copy of the Basketball Station Cards on the following pages. Each of 
these stations contains an important event in basketball history AND a basketball 
skills challenge that your students must complete. 
2.	 Laminate the station cards and place them in different locations around your 
gym or basketball court. You can simply tape them to the wall, or make it more 
challenging by hiding them behind equipment, under desks, or behind doors. Make 
sure the stations are spread out evenly in your space. You do NOT want all the kids 
congregating together in one spot. Remember – this is a HUNT!
3.	 Students can perform this activity in small teams or individually. Each team or 
individual will need one copy of the Basketball Scavenger Hunt Worksheet. This 
worksheet includes a trivia question and a skills challenge for each station.
4.	 Set up any required equipment in one area of the gym where students can retrieve it 
as needed.
5.	 The object of the game is to find all the stations, complete the skills challenges, and 
answer the trivia questions on the worksheet.
Way to Add Intrigue…
Make it a silent hunt! Before students begin, announce that no one is allowed to speak. 
Any communicating between partners or among teams must be in writing or with hand 
motions and lip reading.

fiches.title: TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
19
TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
What You Need
•	
1 or more soccer goals (you can set up cones or use a real goal)
•	
5-10 soccer balls (one per child is best but they can share)
•	
Hula hoops
•	
Bowling pins or similar objects
•	
Pens or pencils
•	
1 printed copy of each Soccer Station Card
•	
1 printed Soccer Scavenger Hunt Worksheet per student
How to Play
1.	 Print out one copy of each of the Soccer Station Cards on the following pages. Each 
one contains an important event in soccer history AND a soccer skills challenge that 
your students must complete. 
2.	 Laminate the Soccer Station Cards and place them in different locations around 
your gym or field. You can simply tape them to the wall, or make it more challenging 
by hiding them behind equipment, under desks, behind doors etc. Make sure 
the stations are spread out evenly in your space. You do NOT want all the kids 
congregating together in one spot. Remember – this is a HUNT!
3.	 Students can perform this activity in small teams or individually. Each team or 
individual will need one copy of the Soccer Scavenger Hunt Worksheet on the next 
page. This worksheet includes a trivia question and a skills challenge for each station.
4.	 Set up any required equipment in one area of the gym where students can retrieve it 
as needed.
5.	 The object of the game is to find all the stations, complete the skills challenges, and 
answer the trivia questions on the worksheet.
Way to Add Intrigue…
Make it a silent hunt! Before students begin, announce that no one is allowed to speak. 
Any communicating between partners or among teams must be in writing or with hand 
motions and lip reading.

fiches.body:

22
TSU CHU WALL BALL
INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a ball 
and stand 10 feet away from the 
wall. Using the inside of your 
foot, pass the ball against the 
wall. Trap the rebound, then 
repeat. Do it 10 times without 
losing control to complete the 
challenge!
2500 BC
A kicking game called Tsu Chu was 
developed in Ancient China and used to 
train soldiers. The word Tsu means “to 
kick the ball with feet,” and the word Chu 
means “a stuffed ball made of leather.”

The Women’s World Cup Final in Los 
Angeles marked the largest ever 
attendance for a women’s sporting event 
in history. Led by Mia Hamm, Brandi 
Chastain, and Julie Foudy, the United 
States defeated China on penalty kicks in 
front of 90,185 screaming fans.

Try five times. How many did you score?

fiches.title: TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
33
TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
What You Need
•	
5-10 tennis balls 
•	
5-10 volleyballs (one per child is best but they can share)
•	
2-3 beach balls or similar
•	
Pens or pencils
•	
1 printed copy of each Volleyball Station Card
•	
1 printed Volleyball Scavenger Hunt Worksheet per student
How to Play
1.	 Print out one copy of each of the Volleyball Station Cards on the following pages. 
Each one contains an important event in volleyball history AND a volleyball skills 
challenge that your students must complete. 
2.	 Laminate the Volleyball Station Cards and place them in different locations around 
your gym or court. You can simply tape them to the wall, or make it more challenging 
by hiding them behind equipment, under desks, behind doors etc. Make sure 
the stations are spread out evenly in your space. You do NOT want all the kids 
congregating together in one spot. Remember – this is a HUNT!
3.	 Set up any required equipment in one area of the gym where students can retrieve it 
as needed.
4.	 Students can perform this activity in small teams or individually. Each team or 
individual will need a pencil/pen and one copy of the Volleyball Scavenger Hunt 
Worksheet. This worksheet includes a trivia question and a skills challenge for each 
station.
5.	 The object of the game is to find all the stations, complete the skills challenges, and 
answer the trivia questions on the worksheet.
Way to Add Intrigue…
Make it a silent hunt! Before students begin, announce that no one is allowed to speak. 
Any communicating between partners or among teams must be in writing or with hand 
motions and lip reading.

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
36
MINTONETTE MADNESS
INSTRUCTIONS: Let’s test your hand-eye coordination! Find a partner 
and two tennis balls. Stand about 10 feet apart. On “go” both of you 
throw your tennis balls to each other and try to catch the incoming 
one (it’s hard!) Keep trying until you get 5 successful catches.
1895
William G. Morgan invented the game of 
volleyball while working as PE director 
for the YMCA in Holyoke Massachusetts. 
He combined features of handball, 
basketball and tennis, and called the sport 
“Mintonette.”
1895

BEACH VOLLEY
INSTRUCTIONS: Find a partner and a beach ball. Tap the ball to your 
partner, back and forth using any part of your body. Complete 10 taps 
in a row to complete this challenge!
1915
The first beach volleyball games took 
place at the Outrigger Beach and Canoe 
Club in Waikiki Beach, Hawaii.

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
38
BOMBERINO
INSTRUCTIONS: Find a partner and a volleyball. Stand about 10 feet 
apart. Have your partner lob the ball high and soft, towards your 
head. Reach up and spike the ball back to them! Do it 10 times to 
complete the challenge!
1916
In the Philippines, players introduced the 
“set” and “spike” - passing the ball high 
to a teammate, who strikes it over the 
net to score a point. They called this the 
“bomba” or kill, and called the hitter a 
“bomberino.”
1916
SPIKE

BUMP & CLAP
INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a 
volleyball. Throw it lightly in 
the air, then bump it as high 
as you can. See how many 
times you can clap before 
the ball lands again! Try 10 
times and see what your 
personal record is.
1919
More than 16,000 volleyballs were sent to 
US soldiers serving in Europe and around 
the world. Volleyball continued to spread 
and grow in popularity.

BUMP, SET, SPIKE!
INSTRUCTIONS: Find two friends and a volleyball and form a triangle. 
You will be passing the ball around the triangle, first with a bump (or 
underhand pass), then set (or overhand pass), then a spike! Finish this 
sequence twice (without losing control) to complete this challenge!
1922
A new rule was established, limiting 
teams to three hits per side.
1922

41
WALL BLOCK
INSTRUCTIONS: Being a 
good blocker requires great 
timing and jumping ability. 
Stand in front of the wall with 
your knees and elbows bent. 
Crouch down slightly, then 
jump as high as you can. How 
far up the wall can you touch? 
Do 10 jumps to complete this 
challenge!
1938
Players in Czechoslovakia perfected the 
technique of blocking at the net, which 
became an official part of the game 
rules.
1938

42
BUMP TRIANGLE
INSTRUCTIONS: Find two friends and a volleyball. Set up in a 
triangle. Using your forearms, bump the ball around the triangle 
from person to person. Get it around three times without 
dropping the ball to complete this challenge!
1940
The forearm pass (or “bump”) was 
introduced to the game. Until then, most 
balls were played with an overhand pass.
1940

43
UNDERHAND SERVE
INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a ball 
and stand about 20 feet from 
the wall. Hold the ball, palm-
up, in front of your body at 
waist height. Reach back with 
your right arm, then swing 
through and hit the ball out 
of your hand. How high on 
the wall can you hit it? Repeat 
10 times to complete this 
challenge!
1964
Volleyball was introduced to the Tokyo 
Olympic Games. The USSR took the 
men’s gold medal and Japan took the 
women’s.
1964

OVERHAND SERVE

and stand about 20 feet from 
the wall. Hold the ball, palm-
up, in front of your body at 
shoulder height. Toss the ball 
up slightly, reach back with 
your right arm, then swing 
through and hit the ball out of 
the air. Can you hit a target on 
the wall? Repeat 10 times to 
complete this challenge!
1996
Beach volleyball was introduced at the 
Atlanta Olympics. Karch Kiraly won the 
gold medal, becoming the first athlete to 
win gold in indoor AND beach volleyball.
1996

WALL SPIKE
INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a volleyball and stand around 6-8 feet 
away from the wall. Toss the ball up then spike it into the ground 
so it bounces off the wall and back to you. Can you spike it several 
times in a row? Complete 25 spikes to finish this challenge!
2012
The beach volleyball duo of Misty-May 
Treanor and Kerri Walsh-Jennings won 
their 3rd consecutive gold medal at the 
London Olympic Games.

What You Need
•	
5-10 pool noodles
•	
5-10 jump ropes
•	
5-10 basketballs and 1 basketball hoop
•	
1 chair that can be easily moved around
•	
Pens or pencils
•	
1 printed copy of each Olympics Station Card
•	
1 printed Olympics Scavenger Hunt Worksheet per student or team
How to Play
1.	 Print out one copy of each of the Olympics Station Cards on the following pages. 
Each one contains an important event in Olympics history AND an Olympics skills 
challenge that your students must complete. 
2.	 Laminate the Olympics Station Cards and place them in different locations around 
your gym or field. You can simply tape them to the wall, or make it more challenging 
by hiding them behind equipment, under desks, behind doors etc. Make sure 
the stations are spread out evenly in your space. You do NOT want all the kids 
congregating together in one spot. Remember – this is a HUNT!
3.	 Set up any required equipment in one area of the gym or field where students can 
retrieve it as needed.
4.	 Students can perform this activity in small teams or individually. Each team or 
individual will need a pencil/pen and one copy of the Olympics Scavenger Hunt 
Worksheet. This worksheet includes a trivia question and a skills challenge for each 
station.
5.	 The object of the game is to find all the stations, complete the skills challenges, and 
answer the trivia questions on the worksheet.
Way to Add Intrigue…
Make it a silent hunt! Before students begin, announce that no one is allowed to speak. 
Any communicating between partners or among teams must be in writing or with hand 
motions and lip reading.

192 STRIDES
INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a friend and get ready to race! Pretend to 
be Coroebus and run for 192 strides. Count out each stride as you 
run! How many laps can you complete?
776 BC
Greece
The first Ancient Olympic Games were held 
during a religious festival honoring Zeus. A 
cook named Coroebus won the only event 
— a 192-meter footrace called the “stade” — 
to become the first Olympic champion.
776 BC
Greece

The Olympic Games were broadcast 
on television for the first time. African-
American sprinter Jesse Owens won four 
gold medals, embarrassing Adolf Hitler and 
undermining his racist agenda.

INSTRUCTIONS: Can you run fast like Jesse Owens? Find an 
empty lane where you won’t crash into anyone. Get down into a 
sprinter’s crouch and have a friend say “Ready, Set, Go!” Take off 
and sprint as fast as you can to the other side of the gym!

The first Paralympic Games were held. 
Four hundred athletes - all in wheelchairs 
- competed in athletics, wheelchair 
basketball, swimming, table tennis, archery, 
snooker, dartchery (a combination of darts 
and archery) and wheelchair fencing.

INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a chair 
and a basketball and sit down in 
front of basketball hoop. 
Can you make a basket 
while sitting down (just like a 
wheelchair basketball player)? 
It takes a lot of upper 
body strength! Take 5 
shots and see how many 
you can make.

American swimmer Michael Phelps won 
his 28th medal, an all-time record. Phelps 
won 23 golds, 3 silvers and 2 bronzes

POOL FLOAT
INSTRUCTIONS: Lie down flat on your belly with your arms by 
your sides. Bring your chest up off the ground and hold it in place!

swimming in a pool. Go for 1 minute to complete this challenge!

fiches.title: TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
61
TEACHER’S INSTRUCTIONS
What You Need
•	
10-20 tennis balls
•	
5-10 large cones
•	
3-5 soft baseball bats
•	
Baseball gloves (not required – students can play bare-handed if needed)
•	
1 bucket or chair
•	
Pens or pencils
•	
1 printed copy of each Baseball & Softball Station Cards
•	
1 printed Baseball & Softball Scavenger Hunt Worksheet per student or team
How to Play
1.	 Print out one copy of each of the Baseball & Softball Station Cards on the following 
pages. Each one contains an important event in Baseball & Softball history AND a 
Baseball & Softball skills challenge that your students must complete. 
2.	 Laminate the Baseball & Softball Station Cards and place them in different locations 
around your gym or field. You can simply tape them to the wall, or make it more 
challenging by hiding them behind equipment, under desks, behind doors etc. Make 
sure the stations are spread out evenly in your space. You do NOT want all the kids 
congregating together in one spot. Remember – this is a HUNT!
3.	 Set up any required equipment in one area of the gym or field where students can 
retrieve it as needed.
4.	 Students can perform this activity in small teams or individually. Each team or 
individual will need a pencil/pen and one copy of the Baseball & Softball Scavenger 
Hunt Worksheet. This worksheet includes a trivia question and a skills challenge for 
each station.
5.	 The object of the game is to find all the stations, complete the skills challenges, and 
answer the trivia questions on the worksheet.
Way to Add Intrigue…
Make it a silent hunt! Before students begin, announce that no one is allowed to speak. 
Any communicating between partners or among teams must be in writing or with hand 
motions and lip reading.

5.	 The object of the game is to find all the stations, complete the skills challenges, and

Way to Add Intrigue…

Any communicating between partners or among teams must be in writing or with hand
motions and lip reading.

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
64
1845
 
A group of New York City men created 
the Knickerbocker Base Ball Club and 
establish the first set of modern rules. They 
introduced a diamond-shaped infield, foul 
lines, and three-strike rule. They also got 
rid of the dangerous practice of tagging 
runners by throwing balls at them!
1845
HOME RUN SPRINT
INSTRUCTIONS: Using four cones, set up a baseball diamond with 
home plate, first base, second base, and third base. Pretend to hit 
a ball, then sprint as fast as you can around the bases!

sections.Instructions:
home plate, first base, second base, and third base. Pretend to hit
a ball, then sprint as fast as you can around the bases!

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
65
1941
New York Yankees center fielder Joe 
DiMaggio went on a 56-game hitting streak, 
getting at least one base hit every game 
from May 15th to July 17th. Many people 
think this record will never be broken!
1941
BASE HIT
INSTRUCTIONS: Find a 
soft bat, a tennis ball, and 
some space for you to safely 
hit. Use a batting tee or 
have a friend pitch to you 
underhand. Take 5 swings 
and see how far you can hit 
the ball!

sections.Instructions:
soft bat, a tennis ball, and

hit. Use a batting tee or

and see how far you can hit

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
66
1947
Jackie Robinson broke the color barrier, 
becoming the first African-American to 
play Major League Baseball. Despite being 
treated harshly, Robinson became a star 
player for the Brooklyn Dodgers and won 
the MLB Rookie of the Year Award.
1947
CLAP AND CATCH
INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a 
tennis ball and (if you have 
one) a baseball glove. Throw 
the ball up into the air, clap 
once, then catch it. 
Try again! This time, throw the 
ball higher and see how many 
times you can clap. 
Make 10 attempts. How many 
claps can you make and still 
catch the ball?

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
67
1965
The first ever Women’s Softball World 
Championship was held in Melbourne, 
Australia. Australia took the gold medal 
with the United States taking the silver.
1965
WALL BALL
INSTRUCTIONS: Grab a tennis ball and (if you have one) a 
baseball glove. Stand about 10 steps away from a wall. Throw the 
ball against the wall and try to catch it. Try high ones and low, 
bouncing ones! Throw and catch the ball 10 times to complete 
this challenge!

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
68
1972
The Title 9 law passed, ensuring women 
have the same opportunities to participate 
in sports as men. Colleges and high schools 
began to add fastpitch softball programs, 
allowing millions of girls to get involved 
in sports, and thousands to earn college 
scholarships.
1972
FIVE IN A ROW
INSTRUCTIONS: Find a partner and a tennis ball. Stand about 10 
steps away from each other and play catch. Make five throws in a 
row (without dropping the ball) to complete this challenge!

sections.Instructions:
steps away from each other and play catch. Make five throws in a
row (without dropping the ball) to complete this challenge!

fiches.body:
1977
New York Yankees slugger Reggie Jackson 
blasted three home runs in a single World 
Series games. The Yankees went on to win 
the game 8-4 and win the World Series. 
Jackson was named MVP and given the 
nickname “Mr. October.”
1977
GROUND BALL CHALLENGE
INSTRUCTIONS: Find a partner 
and a tennis ball. Stand about 
10 steps away from each other. 
Roll the ball along the ground to 
your partner. They will field the 
ground ball using both hands, 
then throw it back to you. Do 5 
ground balls in each direction to 
complete this challenge!
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
69

sections.Instructions:

10 steps away from each other.
Roll the ball along the ground to

ground ball using both hands,
then throw it back to you. Do 5
ground balls in each direction to
complete this challenge!
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
69

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
70
1982
The NCAA held the first ever Women’s 
College World Series for fastpitch 
softball. UCLA beat Fresno State in the 
championship game, 2-0.
1982
DIVING CATCH
INSTRUCTIONS: Find a partner and a tennis ball. Stand about 10 
steps away from each other. Have your partner kneel down while 
you stay standing up. Throw the ball just outside their reach, so 
they have to make a diving catch! Do 5 diving catches each way 
to complete this challenge!

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
71
1995
Cal “The Iron Man” Ripken broke Lou 
Gehrig’s record of 2,130 consecutive games 
played. He received a 22-minute standing 
ovation from the crowd, the longest ever 
given to an athlete.
1995
IRON MAN TRAINING CIRCUIT
INSTRUCTIONS: Cal 
Ripken trained hard 
to stay strong, avoid 
injury and play in so 
many games! Can you 
complete this baseball 
training circuit?
10 Pushups
10 Sit ups
30 Second Plank

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
72
2004
All-American softball pitcher Jennie Finch 
was invited to face off against MLB stars at 
the 2004 Pepsi All-Star Softball Game. She 
struck out Albert Pujols, Mike Piazza, and 
Brian Giles, back-to-back!
2004
TARGET PRACTICE
INSTRUCTIONS: Place a large cone on top of a bucket or chair. 
Grab a tennis ball and stand around 10 steps away. Pretend you are 
a professional pitcher! Wind up, then throw the ball at the cone and 
try to knock it over. You can throw overhand like a baseball pitcher, 
or use a windmill motion like a softball pitcher. Take 5 throws and 
see how many times you can knock over the cone!

sections.Instructions:
Grab a tennis ball and stand around 10 steps away. Pretend you are
a professional pitcher! Wind up, then throw the ball at the cone and
try to knock it over. You can throw overhand like a baseball pitcher,
or use a windmill motion like a softball pitcher. Take 5 throws and
see how many times you can knock over the cone!

other and try to catch them. Catch 10 pop flies to complete this 
challenge!

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
4
Welcome to PE Worksheets, a collection of engaging, fun, and purposeful activities designed to inspire 
Welcome to PE Worksheets, a collection of engaging, fun, and purposeful activities designed to inspire 
movement, promote healthy habits, and build essential life skills. This resource is perfect for educators, 
movement, promote healthy habits, and build essential life skills. This resource is perfect for educators, 
program leaders, and families looking to encourage kids to stay active, reflect on their goals, and develop 
program leaders, and families looking to encourage kids to stay active, reflect on their goals, and develop 
teamwork and sportsmanship in a meaningful way.
teamwork and sportsmanship in a meaningful way.
Inside, you’ll find a variety of worksheets, challenges, and interactive activities that focus on fitness, 
Inside, you’ll find a variety of worksheets, challenges, and interactive activities that focus on fitness, 
personal growth, and positive behavior. Whether you’re supporting students in PE class, at home, or in 
personal growth, and positive behavior. Whether you’re supporting students in PE class, at home, or in 
after-school programs, these tools will help kids build confidence, set goals, and enjoy being active every

day.
How to Use This Resource
How to Use This Resource
For PE Teachers:
•	•	
Use the worksheets as quick warm-up or cool-down activities to reinforce key fitness and teamwork 
Use the worksheets as quick warm-up or cool-down activities to reinforce key fitness and teamwork 
concepts.
concepts.
•	•	
Incorporate challenges as monthly or weekly goals to keep students motivated and accountable for 
Incorporate challenges as monthly or weekly goals to keep students motivated and accountable for 
their progress.
their progress.
•	•	
Assign reflection activities to encourage students to set goals and track their achievements over time.
Assign reflection activities to encourage students to set goals and track their achievements over time.
For Classroom Teachers:
•	•	
Use the activities as active brain breaks to help students refocus while promoting movement and 
Use the activities as active brain breaks to help students refocus while promoting movement and 
energy.
energy.
•	•	
Incorporate worksheets into health or SEL lessons to connect physical activity to personal growth and 
Incorporate worksheets into health or SEL lessons to connect physical activity to personal growth and 
well-being.
well-being.
•	•	
Create a fitness challenge board in the classroom where students can track their progress together.
Create a fitness challenge board in the classroom where students can track their progress together.
For After-School Programs:
•	•	
Organize friendly competitions or challenges to promote teamwork and sportsmanship in a fun, low-
Organize friendly competitions or challenges to promote teamwork and sportsmanship in a fun, low-
pressure environment.
pressure environment.
•	•	
Use the worksheets for structured activity time, helping kids stay active while reinforcing healthy 
Use the worksheets for structured activity time, helping kids stay active while reinforcing healthy 
habits.
habits.
•	•	
Reward participation with “fitness achievements” to recognize effort and encourage continued 
Reward participation with “fitness achievements” to recognize effort and encourage continued 
engagement.
engagement.
For Parents:
•	•	
Use the activities to create a family fitness routine, working together on challenges and tracking 
Use the activities to create a family fitness routine, working together on challenges and tracking 
progress.
progress.
•	•	
Encourage kids to set personal fitness goals and celebrate small achievements as they build healthy 
Encourage kids to set personal fitness goals and celebrate small achievements as they build healthy 
habits.
habits.
•	•	
Provide structure for screen-free time with fun, self-paced activities that promote movement and 
Provide structure for screen-free time with fun, self-paced activities that promote movement and 
positivity.
positivity.

fiches.title: COMPONENTS OF FITNESS
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
12
COMPONENTS OF FITNESS 
The components of fitness are the building blocks that help us stay healthy, strong, and active. 
They include things like strength, endurance, flexibility, and balance, which all work together to keep 
our bodies and minds in shape.
In this word search, you will find important fitness words related to these components. As you 
search, think about how each word connects to your health and physical activity.
WORD SEARCH
Cardiovascular
Endurance 
Strength  
Flexibility  
Power    
Balance  
Speed  
Coordination  
Reaction  
Muscle
Anaerobic  
Heart  
Stamina  
Fitness  
Energy  
Agility
Aerobic  
Resistance
Core  
Mobility 
  
Once you finish, choose 2 words and write a quick sentence about why they 
are important for fitness!

fiches.title: COMPONENTS OF FITNESS
fiches.body:

© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
COMPONENTS OF FITNESS 
The components of fitness are the building blocks that help us stay healthy, strong, and active. 
They include things like strength, endurance, flexibility, and balance, which all work together to keep 
our bodies and minds in shape.
In this word search, you will find important fitness words related to these components. As you 
search, think about how each word connects to your health and physical activity.

are important for fitness!

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Racket Games! 
Inside this resource, you’ll get 13 fun activities you can use with Kindergarten through 8th Grade. Many of the games are 
demonstrated using tennis courts and equipment, but they can be easily adapted for badminton, pickleball or other 
racket sports. Each activity is explained with simple instructions, equipment lists, diagrams and modifications. 
Skill Focus 
Each activity will have a skill focus, based on one of the fundamental skills of racket sports. 
Group Size 
All of these activities are designed to work with smaller or larger groups. A group size is provided, but this is just the 
minimum number of students you will need for the game to work. For larger groups, you can increase the number of 
players in the game, or create multiple stations with smaller groups. 
Complexity 
Each activity is rated on a Complexity scale indicating how difficult the skills are to perform. 
  Green - recommended for Kindergarten and up (or with modifications for older children) 
  Yellow - recommended for 3rd grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
  Red - recommended for 5th grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
Modifications 
We’ve also included modifications to make the games easier or harder, for different age levels. 
Ask Students 
Some games also include review questions you can ask your students - allowing you to reinforce key coaching points, 
rules or skills.

fiches.body:
Drop, Hit & Catch
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment

Ask STudents
Forehand 
Balls
Rackets
• Students partner up with a ball per pair. One hitter and one catcher 
• The hitter positions themselves near the baseline, the catcher a few 
steps inside the service box 
• The hitter performs a drop serve/forehand towards their partner 
• The catcher attempts to catch the ball after one bounce or on the full 
• Switch after 5 hits/catches

stroke back

the catcher

fiches.title: Peg Battle
fiches.body:
Peg Battle
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Forehand 
• Students partner up with a ball and a peg: One student is the thrower/catcher 
and one is the hitter 
• The thrower/catcher starts by underarm tossing the ball over the net on the 
forehand side of the hitter 
• The hitter allows the ball to bounce once before hitting the ball over the net 
with a forehand strike 
• The thrower/catcher aims to catch the ball after one bounce with two hands 
• For each successful catch the catcher moves a peg up a rung, starting from 
the bottom rung of the net. Switch after 5 throws/strikes
What are some key points 
of the forehand ground 
stroke?
Hitter can send the ball over 
from both forehand and 
backhand side 
Use underarm throws instead 
of a racket 
Catch on the full only 
2
Rackets
Balls
Pegs

• Students partner up to form a group of two as shown below 
• Split the court into 4 zones separated by either 2 nets or Cones 
• The game starts with the team in zone 1 performing a serve into any 
other zone 
• Teams can hit the shuttle into any zone 
• If the ball hits the ground in a teams zone, they move to zone 4.  
• Everybody else moves up a zone.

fiches.title: Squad Up
fiches.body:
Squad Up
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Court Position
How can you make it more 
difficult for your opponent 
to return the ball? 
How can you prevent your 
opponent from scoring a 
point?
6-8
• Form teams (squads) and send each to a court to face another team 
• The front person for each ‘squad’ starts the game and plays a game 
of singles using tennis/badminton/pickleball/volleyball rules against 
the front person from the other ‘squad’. 
• Winner stays on, loser heads to the back of their squads line 
• If a players wins 3 points in a row, they cycle off and head to the back 
of their line. However the winning squad still serves. 
• Squads earn a point every time they win a rally. Play to the best of 11 
or to a certain time limit. 
Use different striking 
instruments e.g hand, 
different racket etc 
Use different shaped balls 
e.g tennis balls, bouncy 
balls, shuttles 
Ball can bounce more 
than once 
Play in the service boxes 
only (not full court)
Rackets
Balls
Defending Space
Creating an attack
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Small Group Games! 
In this resource, we’ve provided 22 fun games designed for groups of 2 to 12. You can use these 
games for PE class, recess, field days, summer camps, or after school sports programs. Many of 
them can also be used with larger groups with some minor modifications. 
Each game is explained with step by step instructions and rules for running the game. Along with 
a detailed diagram and equipment list showing you exactly how to set up the space. The 
activities are appropriate for Kindergarten through 8th grade students, and can be done indoors 
or outdoors. 
Let’s get started!

fiches.title: Volcanoes and Tornadoes
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Volcanoes and Tornadoes
Markers
Split students into two groups (Volcanoes & Tornadoes) and disperse amongst playing area. On 
teacher whistle students run around the playing area trying to flip markers to their teams 
side. Winning team is the team with the most at the end of designated time. Extension: Use with 
dribbling, non dribbling, dribble with opposite hand, no basketball and change the locomotor e.g 
skipping, defensive slide etc
Setup

fiches.title: Fruit Salad
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Fruit Salad
Setup
markers
hoops
Line up students along a lined set of markers (or lines on courts). Have students name 5 fruits 
and then assign each student one of those 5. there should be an even amount of each ‘fruits’. 
When a teacher calls a fruit, those students must travel around the end marker and back. The 
last students to get back is in the ‘fruit salad bowl’. When the teacher calls out ‘fruit salad’, 
everyone in the bowl is back in the game. Mix up the locomotor often and adjust students 
starting positions adjust for skill levels.

fiches.title: Heads, Shoulders, Knees and Cones
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Heads, Shoulders, Knees and Cones
Setup
markers - 4 diﬀerent colours. Each group should 
have one of each
Students partner up. Each group should have the same 4 coloured markers. The teacher calls 
out commands such as ‘heads, shoulders, knees, toes etc. Students copy. When the teacher calls 
a colour, students must try and pick that coloured marker up before their opponent. Mix up call 
commands and starting position often.

fiches.title: Octopus
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Octopus
Setup
Tagger
Tagger
Cones to design playing area
Students (Fish) line up on one end. Choose 3-4 people to start as ‘taggers’ (Octopus). The aim is to 
reach the other side without being tagged by one of the ‘taggers’. if you are you turn into 
‘seaweed’ where you were tagged and go to your knees. Seaweeds can tag fish, but cannot run. 
Last student left is the winner.

Setup
In this game the teacher is the captain and students spread out around the playing 
area. The captain calls out numerous actions that students have to perform. Here 
are a few ideas on the following page:

fiches.title: Corners
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Corners
Setup
4 diﬀerent coloured large markers
Students all start in the middle of the playing area. On teachers command (“5,4,3,2,1 - Find a 
corner”) students must run and stay at a cone. Teacher spins a spare cone, the group it points 
to are out ad must grab a tennis ball and perform 20 catches with partner / wall before they 
can come back in. You can choose any skill you wish. The game is continuous with no winners. 
\

fiches.title: Rock, Paper, Scissors War
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Rock, Paper, Scissors War
Setup
Home Base
Home Base
markers
Setup area as shown. Split class into 4-6 even groups. The aim is to race out and challenge the opposing 
team in a game of rock, paper, scissors. The winning students continues racing towards the opponents 
‘home base’ line. The loser races back to the end of their teams line. If a student makes it to the 
other teams ‘home base’ line and wins a game, their team wins. Tip: The next student in line must be 
ready to go. Emphasise paying attention. To extend the game, make the playing area larger.

fiches.title: Tic-Tac Throw
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Group students into teams of 4-5. Teams battle against each to knock off their coloured cones 
from a bench/table/wall. If successful the student places that cone into one of the hoops at 
the back of the throwing area. The first team to get 3 in a row (like Tic-Tac-Toe) wins. 
Tic-Tac Throw
Two sets of 5 markers each
2 xThrowing markers
9 x hula hoops

fiches.title: Volcanoes and Islands
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Volcanoes and Islands
Two sets of markers each
2 xThrowing markers
Hula hoops
Group students into teams of 3-4. Teams battle against each other to claim the most 
‘islands’ (hoops). Students throw a beanbag out and try to land it in an ‘island’, if so that team 
claims that island and places their coloured marker (Volcano) inside. Any other beanbag that 
lands inside that ‘island’ does not count. Missed throws are picked up and returned to the next 
thrower in the team. The winning team is the team that claims the most ‘islands’

sections.Setup:

2 xThrowing markers

thrower in the team. The winning team is the team that claims the most ‘islands’

fiches.title: Castles
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Castles
1 x Tall marker each
Setup area as show. Student each have a marker ‘Castle’ they need to protect. The object is to knock 
other students markers over with a soccer pass. Students can protect their marker with the feet 
or body only, no hands. The last standing 3 castles are the winners. To keep students involved, once 
they are out they find a partner and perform soccer passing skills at a different station. 
Progressions: To make it harder have less balls, lengthen the area or allow hands. To make it easier 
shorten the area, add kore balls or allow rolling. 
1 x soccer ball for every 3 people in the game

sections.Setup:

1 x Tall marker each
Setup area as show. Student each have a marker ‘Castle’ they need to protect. The object is to knock
other students markers over with a soccer pass. Students can protect their marker with the feet
or body only, no hands. The last standing 3 castles are the winners. To keep students involved, once
they are out they find a partner and perform soccer passing skills at a different station.
Progressions: To make it harder have less balls, lengthen the area or allow hands. To make it easier
shorten the area, add kore balls or allow rolling.
1 x soccer ball for every 3 people in the game

fiches.title: Lions Den
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Lions Den
Setup as shown with all students on the ‘start’ line and one ‘Lion’ inside the Lions Den. For the first few 
games with younger students have the teacher as the ‘Lion’. The game starts with the ‘Lion’ facing 
towards the students, once the lion turns around the students race out, when the lion turns around they 
must ‘freeze’. If any students are moving (or the last to freeze), the lion calls their name and sends them 
back to the start line. Students who make it to the Lions Den grab a beanbag and run it back down the 
sideline and place it at the start line. The person who collects the most beanbags wins. 
Start
Lions Den
Beanbags

fiches.title: Space Invaders
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Space Invaders
Setup as shown but with throws all around the playing area. Students can play as individuals or as teams of two. 
The aim is to throw missiles ‘balls’ at the ‘planets’ (balls on markers). If successful the students returns that 
‘planet’ to their own ‘solar system’ (throwing spot). The student with the most ‘planets is the winner. Progressions: 
Increase the distance, have smaller missiles and planets, have less planets. For an added fitness level, give students 
only 1 ball each and they must chase their ball and return it rather than getting a track of balls to throw (make 
sure they are soft so if they get hit it doesn’t hurt, foam dodgeballs are best).

markers with balls on top

sections.Setup:
Space Invaders
Setup as shown but with throws all around the playing area. Students can play as individuals or as teams of two.
The aim is to throw missiles ‘balls’ at the ‘planets’ (balls on markers). If successful the students returns that
‘planet’ to their own ‘solar system’ (throwing spot). The student with the most ‘planets is the winner. Progressions:
Increase the distance, have smaller missiles and planets, have less planets. For an added fitness level, give students
only 1 ball each and they must chase their ball and return it rather than getting a track of balls to throw (make
sure they are soft so if they get hit it doesn’t hurt, foam dodgeballs are best).

fiches.title: Hula Spinnercise
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Hula Spinnercise
Starjumps
In this quick warm up, students partner up and have one hula hoop between them. One person starts as the 
caller, one person starts as the spinner. The caller says an exercise and the spinner spins the hula hoop. The spinner 
must continue to perform that exercise until the hula hoop has come to a complete stop. Switch roles after 
every spin. To extend this warm up: give each student a hula hoop and they perform the exercise the teacher calls 
out. Alternatively have a list displayed on a projector / board that students can choose from. 
Hula Hoops

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Speedball
Goals
Goals
Goals
Goals
Use a normal indoor basketball court with 4 soccer / hockey goals that are placed in either corner as shown. Each team 
has a basketball net and 2 soccer / hockey goals at each end. Teams pass the ball in the air like basketball (but no 
dribbling is permitted). If the ball falls to the ground it is now played as soccer with indoor soccer rules. A player may kick 
the ball into his team mates hands, but picking up from the ground with hands is not permitted. Basketball goals are 
worth 2 points, soccer goals are worth 1 point. 
Goals
4 x goals

fiches.title: Golden Child
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Golden Child
Split students into two team (if you have access to kore courts, split into smaller teams so there is less waiting). Have one shooting team 
and one running team. On teachers command, the first person in the running team runs around the court from the star marker to the 
‘home base’ If a student reaches this point without the shooting team making a basket, they score a run and join the end of the line. If the 
shooting team make a basket they yell ‘freeze’ and the runner needs to sit down where they are up to. The last person in line is the ‘golden 
child’. When they run they tap the head of the students that are ‘frozen’ and they get up and continue to try and get to home base along 
with the ‘golden child’. If they make it home before the shooting team makes a basket they all score a run, if the shooting team  saes a 
basket they all freeze and the teachers counts the runs. Switch shooting team and running team. Winning team is the team that has the 
most runs. 
Home Base
Start
Running Team
Shooting Team
Markers
1 xBasketball

fiches.title: Bucket Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Bucket Ball
Setup
1.
This game is best played indoors or on a basket ball court 
2.
The aim of the game is for offensive players to pass their ball to their ‘catcher’ to score a goal. The catcher can only use 
the bucket and not their hands to catch a pass. Offensive players must make 3 passes before passing to the ‘catcher’ 
3.
Players can only ‘pivot’ with the ball, no dribbling or running with the ball permitted. Players move without the ball to get 
open. Offensive players have 3 seconds to dispose of the ball. If not a turnover occurs.  
4.
Defenders can ‘intercept’ a ball but cannot steal a ball out of the opponents hands.  
5.
A ball that is on the ground (missed pass) = any team can collect. If both players grab the ball and tussle over it = jump ball 
(like in basketball). Another way you can do it is to alternate the possession arrow each time.  
6.
Only the catcher are permitted into the circle area. at each end.  
x 2 buckets
x 2 chairs (or similar)

fiches.title: Head of the Court
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Head of the Court
Setup
Net
1.
Replicate this setup on multiple smaller tennis, pickleball or badminton courts as shown. e.g you should create 
enough courts so every student in your class isn’t waiting too long for a turn 
2.
Split your class into groups of 8. Within this group each students should have a partners to play doubles with.  
3.
Normal doubles tennis rules apply (you can adjust rules to suit your classes skill level) 
4.
The winners stay on and are ‘head of the court’ whilst the losers head to the back and wait their turn. Next 
group takes their place 
5.
Keep games short e.g best of 5 and rotate service every point.  
6.
Head of the court get to serve first

fiches.title: Connect 4 Tennis
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Connect 4 Tennis
Setup
Net
Net
Net
Net
Connect 4
Waiting Bay
Dice
Polyspots
Small or large connect 4 
game/s
Tennis rackets and 
1 ball per court
1.
Setup multiple tennis, pickleball or badminton courts, making them smaller for space reasons is fine 
2.
Split group in to two teams and have give them a number each. Each team finds the opposition with the same number (this will be 
their first opponent and lines up along the polyspots.  
3.
Players roll the dice (1-6), the number they land on is their starting score in a best of 7 game. As soon as players have both roll the 
dice they head to a court and play the first to 7 points (using normal or modified tennis rules). If a player wins they head to the 
connect 4 game and pop their teams coloured counter into an open spot. The loser heads to the waiting bay and waits for an 
opponent (not the same one twice in a row), the winner also does the same after popping the counter into the connect 4 game. 
4.
The first team to get a connect 4 wins. 
5.
Restart the game with a new starting opponent.

fiches.title: Rock, Paper, Scissors Twist
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Rock, Paper, Scissors Twist
Setup
1.
Split students into two teams (blue and red)  
2.
Have 4 dice stations with a blue and red marker at each. Students start the game out lining up behind one of their teams markers 
(spread out so evenly spaced numbers) 
3.
On go the first players in each line roll a dice. The number they roll is their starting number. Once both have rolled both players move 
to the side and play a game of rock , paper, scissors (first to seven). They start the game from the number they rolled. e,g 1 player 
rolls a 6 and the other rolls a 1. The person on 6 only has to win one game to get to 7.  
4.
Once a student has got 7, they run and place a piece in the connect 4 board / Hoops then find another marker to challenge someone. 
The loser heads to another line and looks to challenge someone else. Encourage looking g for shorter lines 
5.
The first team to fill half of the connect 4 board wins.  
4 x large dice
1 x large connect 4 / or setup with hula hoops on ground

fiches.title: Hit the Target
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Hit the Target
Setup
• Dive students into teams of 2-3. You can run multiple games simultaneously by marking off playing areas 
• The aim is for students to use passing and moving skills to knock down the opposing teams marker/pin/ bottle 
etc 
• Students can only maintain possession of the ball for 3-5 seconds before passing or throwing at the target 
• All players must stay out of the hula hoop zone at both ends 
• If a pin is successfully knocked down, offensive team scores a point and the pin is reset 
• Defenders can only ‘intercept’ no ‘stealing’ allowed
Cones
Dodgeballs
Hula Hoops

fiches.title: Multi Ball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Multi Ball
Setup
1.
This game can be played indoors or outdoors 
2.
Split group into two teams. One team are fielders, the other is the running team.  
3.
One by one the running team each have a turn at throwing, kicking, hitting (depends on the ball) all 5 balls into the field of 
play. Once all balls have been played, the fielding team aims to collect them (fielders must start behind the ‘fielding line’) and 
return them to each of the 5 poly spots. Meanwhile the first player in line for the running team runs around the end 
marker and back and tags the next player in line. When the fielders return all the balls, they yell ‘freeze’ and the running 
team stops.  
4.
Switch over when everyone has had a go. The team with the most runs wins.  
Progression: catches = +3 runs to the fielding team.  
Fielding Team
Running team
Fielding Line
Markers
Polyspots
Various sporting equipment

fiches.title: CRoss The River
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
CRoss The River
Setup
1.
Split group into approximately groups of 6-8. Make sure every group has the same number of people if 
possible.  
2.
Each group receives 2 large gym mats, 2 scooter boards, 2 poly spots and a large tug of war rope. 
3.
The object of the game is for groups to use communication, strategy and getting along to get all of the 
people in the group  (and each piece of equipment) over to the other side of the ‘river' 
4.
The first team to do so are winners.  
5.
Allow students to talk strategy at the start and emphasis teamwork and good communication.  
Large mats
scooters 
boards
polyspots
large 
ropes
