fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Soccer Games! 
Inside this resource, you’ll get 28 fun activities you can use with Kindergarten through 8th Grade. Each activity is explained 
with simple instructions, equipment lists, diagrams and modifications. 
Skill Focus 
Each activity will have a skill focus, based on one of the fundamental skills of soccer. 
Group Size 
All of these activities are designed to work with smaller or larger groups. A group size is provided, but this is just the 
minimum number of students you will need for the game to work. For larger groups, you can increase the number of 
players in the game, or create multiple stations with smaller groups. 
Complexity 
Each activity is rated on a Complexity scale indicating how difficult the skills are to perform. 
  Green - recommended for Kindergarten and up (or with modifications for older children) 
  Yellow - recommended for 3rd grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
  Red - recommended for 5th grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
Modifications 
We’ve also included modifications to make the games easier or harder, for different age levels. 
Ask Students 
Some games also include review questions you can ask your students - allowing you to reinforce key coaching points, 
rules or skills.

Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment

Ask STudents

Trapping 
Passing

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Keepy Off
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Cones
Balls
Bibs/Pinnies
Defender
-
Create groups of 4 students: 3 offensive players and 1 defensive player.  
-
Each group has 1 soccer ball.  
-
The offensive players will try to keep the ball away from the 1 defender. Use both dribbling 
and passing skills.  
-
If the defender steals the ball, return the ball to the group of 3 and continue play.  
-
Stop play every 1 to 2 minutes to change defenders.
Play with no defenders, students 
try to pass and move to 
complete set amount of passes 
Place Cones / poly spots on the 
ground. These will identify places 
the students could run to to 
create space
How can you make sure the 
defender doesn’t intercept the 
ball? 
If you do not have the ball as an 
attacker what can you do?

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Free Agent
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Cones
Balls
Bibs/Pinnies
-
Create groups of 7 students (or any odd amount) 
-
Team play 3 vs 3 small sided games of soccer, basketball hockey etc 
-
The player that is left over is called the ‘free agent’. This player plays for which ever team 
has the ball on offence. They do not play Defense they simply keep switching according 
to whoever has the ball 
-
Rotate the free agent every few minutes
Play with no goals and have the 
offensive team aim to make 5 
successful pass 
This game can be used for any 
invasion minor game

area 
Add goals 
Increase or decrease the amount 
of players per team

ball?

attacker what can you do?
7

fiches.title: Bumper Cars
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Bumper Cars
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Cones
Balls
Bibs/Pinnies
4
Passing 
Defense
• Setup as shown. Have five 2m x 2m areas marked off as squares inside the playing area.  
• This game has 2 defenders (red) and 9 attackers (blue) 
• Defenders aim to steal and kick the balls out of the playing area. Once they have kicked all 
4-5 out of the playing area, the game stops. 
• Attackers can dribble in the playing area and pass to team mates inside the 2m x 2m ‘pods’ 
• Defenders cannot go in the pods or ‘hang’ by them.  
• If an attacker passes to another player in the pod, that person dribbles or passes out of the 
pod and replaced by the the passer.  
• Dribblers can switch with a person in a pod at any time if they need a rest
Shorten the playing area 
to make it harder, 
increase the paying area 
to make it easier 
Add more attackers and 
less balls so there is more 
of a focus on passing the 
ball 
How can you avoid losing 
your ball to a defender? 
Why is accurate passing 
important in this game?
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

Group Size
Complexity

4+
Cones
Balls

fiches.title: Dribble Knockout
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Dribble Knockout
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Set up the playing area as shown below. 
• Each player has a ball 
• The aim of the game is to keep control of your ball, whilst trying to knock other 
players balls out of the playing area 
• If your ball is knocked out you dribble one lap around the playing area and 
come back into the game 
• No slide tackles or wild kicks at the ball
Dribbling
8+
Cones
Balls
Change the activity needed in 
order to rejoin the game (ie. 10 
toe touches on the ball, 10 
pushups, 10 sit-ups etc.) 
Shorten or lengthen the area to 
suit
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Through the Gates
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Through the Gates
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Setup the playing area as shown below. Each player has a ball 
• The aim is to dribble through as many ‘gates’ as possible  
• Play each round for one minute 
• Partner up and every time you go through a ‘gate’ you must pass to your parter 
who is waiting at the previous gate.  
• If the pass is inaccurate the kicker must collect and dribble to their team mate
Dribbling, Passing
8+
Cones
Balls
Easier: Increase the width of the 
gates 
Harder: decrease the width of the 
gates 
Have students use only their left 
foot or right foot 
Vary the size of the gates and 
make the narrower ones worth 
extra points
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Flip Cone Dribble
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Flip Cone Dribble
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Set up playing area as shown below.  
• Start with 2-4 teams all lined up in a corner each. Each team has 1 ball. 
• Choose two teams to be ‘volcanoes’ and two teams to be tornadoes. Tornadoes 
= upside down Cone. Volcanoes = normal Cones 
• On teachers command, first player dribbles their ball into the playing area, traps 
the ball and flips a cone to their teams side with their hand. They then dribble 
back and the next person goes through. 
• Play for 1-2 mins. Win by flipping more to your side than the other team. 
Dribbling
8+
Cones
Balls
Easier: introduce the game by 
playing without the soccer 
dribbling element - just have 
students run out and flip the cones 
before returning 
Harder: students must perform 10 
toe-taps before flipping a cone
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

Modify it

the ball and flips a cone to their teams side with their hand. They then dribble

• Play for 1-2 mins. Win by flipping more to your side than the other team.

fiches.title: Shadow Dribbling
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Shadow Dribbling
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Setup playing area as shown. Each player has a ball and a partner 
• The aim is to ‘shadow’ your partner who is the lead dribbler around the playing 
area. 
• Players must mimic the leader dribblers patterns and whatever trick they perform 
• Keep a safe distance apart where possible 
• Switch over after one minute
Dribbling
8+
Cones
Balls
Introduce the activity by playing  
a round of Simon Says first 
Add an extra shadow by including 
a third student in each group
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Reaction Dribbling
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Reaction Dribbling
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Create approx a 20m x 20 grid with 1 large Cone, pole or flag in each corner  
• Players free dribble inside the playing area  
• The coach calls out a players name and that player quickly chooses to dribble 
around one of the corner Cones (tip: choose a person close to one of the Cones) 
• All the other players follow the chosen student. 
• The aim is to not be the last person back
Dribbling
4+
Cones
Balls
Call out multiple students in each 
round. Other players can choose 
which to follow 
Last student to arrive at the cone 
must do a lap around the playing 
area before rejoining the game
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Soccer Marbles
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Soccer Marbles
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Players form pairs and have a ball each 
• Players take turns to make contact with their partners ball 
• The player with the most amount of ‘ball hits’ in a row win 
• Play against the whole group and the pair with the highest amount of ‘ball hits’ 
win
Passing
2+
Cones
Balls
• Adjust the distance between 
partners 
• Have students pass with the 
left foot or right foot 
• Have students chip the ball 
over an obstacle
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Circle Chase
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Circle Chase
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Create a circle with approx 8-10 students. Make sure the circle is wide enough so 
students are approx 2-4 metres apart 
• Have two players on opposite sides of the circle start with the ball 
• Players pass the ball around the circle (without skipping a person) and try to 
overtake the ball in front  
• Teacher calls ‘change’ and players must change the direction of the pass
Passing, Receiving
8+
Cones
Balls
• Increase or decrease 
distance between each 
student 
• Easier: start with 1 ball 
• Harder: add in a 3rd ball
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Star Wars
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Star Wars
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Start with a group of 8-10 students inside a 20m x 20m playing area 
• Have 4 students on the outside of each side of the square as interchange players 
• Students each have a tall Cone (‘planet’) they have to defend 
• Students aim to knock over their opponents planet using a pass (ball along the 
floor) 
• Students may run to collect a ball that is in open space but must be aware that 
their cone can be knocked over with a pass if so 
• If a cone is knocked over that students change over with an interchange player
Dribbling
8+
Cones
Balls
• Adjust the number of balls in 
play at once 
• Use different size “planets” to 
make the targets harder or 
easier to hit
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: Piggy in the Middle
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Piggy in the Middle
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Set up the playing area as shown below. Split students in to groups of 4 
• Have 3 attacker and one defender (Piggy) who defends in the middle of the 
square 
• Piggy can only intercept, not steal from an attacker who has possession 
• Attacker start at a corner cone (with one left over spare) and can move between 
cones but only one attacker can stand at each cone. If two attackers are at a 
cone, one must move to a spare 
• Attackers aim to make 5-10 passes without the piggy intercepting the ball
Dribbling
5+
Cones
Balls
• Add extra Piggies 
• Increase the distance 
between cones to make it 
more difficult for the Piggy to 
intercept
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Split the Defender
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Setup 10 x 10m grid. 4 passer and one defender 
• Passers can move up and down their line 
• Defender cannot ‘steal’ the ball, intercept only  
• Attackers aim to complete as many passes across the zone and possible without 
the defender intercepting the ball 
• Attackers cannot pass back to the player who passed to them.
Dribbling
5+
Cones
Balls
• Add extra defenders inside 
the grid 
• Increase the size of the grid 
to make it more difficult for 
the Piggy to intercept 
• Players must either trap the 
ball first, or pass it 
immediately on the first touch

fiches.title: Circle Passes
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Circle Passes
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Set up the playing area as shown below. 
• Split students into groups of 6 
• Have one person in the middle who passes around the circle to each player 
• Focus should be on quick trap and accurate passes 
• Switch so everyone get a go in the middle 
• If you have multiple groups, race between the groups to see who can get around 
the circle first
Dribbling
4+
Cones
Balls
• Adjust the size of the circle to 
create longer or shorter 
passes 
• Players must either trap the 
ball first, or pass it 
immediately on the first touch

fiches.title: Soccer Tennis
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Soccer Tennis
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Set up the playing area as shown below. Students partner up and try and pass a 
soccer back and forth across the ‘net’ (use Cones) 
• Students should start close and try and ‘one time’ the ball back and forth 
• As students move further back encourage players to tap and pass back 
• If students can one time the ball back and forth that is also allowed 
• Play  a game between groups to see who can get the most passes in one minute
Dribbling
2+
Cones
Balls
• Play singles, doubles, or triples 
by adding more players to 
each side 
• Use cones as the net so 
students have to chip the ball 
over the “net” to the other 
side
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.body:

Through Ball Passing
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Set up the playing area as shown below. Split students in to groups of 4 - 6 
• Have two lines ‘Passers and ‘Receivers’ 
• Passers aim to pass the ball between two cones to the receiver who makes a well 
timed run 
• Receiver collects the ball and dribbles the ball around to the passers line 
• The passer who passed the ball heads Roberto the receivers line

4+

on goal after receiving the 
pass 
• Adjust the length of the pass 
to make it easier or harder 
• Add in a defender on the 
passer or receiver

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Zig Zag Pass and Follow
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
• Players pass the ball in a zig zag pattern down the line 
• Players follow their pass to the next cone after they kick the ball 
• When the last person in the line gets the ball they dribble to the starting Cone 
• Race: Make it competitive by having groups race each other to finish. When the 
leader gets back to the starting Cone, the group sit down. First team to finish is the 
winner

cones to make longer or 
shorter passes 
• Have students pass with their 
left or right foot

fiches.title: Give and Go
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Give and Go
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
- Setup as shown with one leader (designated by the red shirt player) 
- Every student has a ball 
- First in line passes to the ball to the leader  (1)

- Once collected the ball from the leader the player sprint dribbles between the 
end cones (3 to 4) and rejoins the line.  
- Change leader after everyone has had a go.

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Social Distancing Games!
Inside this resource, you’ll get 24 fun activities you can use with Kindergarten through 8th Grade. Each activity is explained 
with simple instructions, safety guidelines, diagrams and modifications. So you can get your students active and having 
fun, while maintaining your social distancing protocols.
Skill Focus
Each activity will have a skill focus, based on a fundamental movement or sports skill the students are learning.
Group Size
All of these activities are designed to work with smaller or larger groups. A group size is provided, but this is just the 
minimum number of students you will need for the game to work. For larger groups, you can increase the number of 
players in the game, or create multiple stations with smaller groups.
Complexity
Each activity is rated on a Complexity scale indicating how difficult the skills are to perform.
  Green - recommended for Kindergarten and up (or with modifications for older children)
  Yellow - recommended for 3rd grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children)
  Red - recommended for 5th grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children)
Modifications
We’ve also included modifications to make the games easier or harder, or to change up the skill focus for more variety.
Safety Considerations
All of these activities and games will keep your students at least 6 feet apart at all times. There are a range of options 
depending on how much shared equipment you are willing to allow. In some cases, there will be a shared ball (often 
using feet only). In others, you are encouraged to have each student retain their own ball if possible.

• Setup playing areas as shown. Remind students of your social distancing

• The aim of the game is to throw (underarm or overarm) a beanbag and land it 
completely inside a hula hoop (Island). If successful, the person/team owns this

beanbag or set of beanbags

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Space Invaders
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
safety
Throwing
2+
Students maintain 6 feet of

Each student should have their 
own frisbee or ball
Sanitize frisbees and balls 
before and after use
Move the distance of the 
Cones further or closer to the 
targets
Adjust the size or shape of the 
ball
Choose a different way to 
send the ball e.g kick. roll etc
Change throwing technique 
e.g opposite hand
• Setup playing areas as shown. Balance discs on top of cones to create targets. 
Remind students of your social distancing protocols
• The object is to knock apart as many “space invaders” as you can in 60 seconds by 
throwing the foam ball from behind the line and knocking the discs off the cones.  
• On the start signal, throw the foam ball at the cones and discs. After you throw the 
ball, run and get it, return to the throwing line, and then throw again. Continue for 1 
full minute. 
• If you knock all 8 discs off of the cones, you can reset the discs and then move 
back to the line to attempt more throws. Don’t forget to count the number of 
‘space invaders’ you destroy

Cones
Beanbags
• Setup playing areas as shown. Remind students of your social distancing protocols
• The object of the game is to be the first player to toss each of the 5 beanbags 
into each of the 5 buckets. 
• Students compete head-to-head with the player on the opposite side. Or, play as 
a team game where subsequent tossers can only begin once their teammates 
have scored.
• On the start signal, begin by tossing 1 of the beanbags into 1 of the buckets. If 
students miss, they retrieve the beanbag and try again. 
• Students cannot interfere with opponent’s toss.

fiches.title: Gotcha
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Gotcha
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
safety
Rolling / Throwing
6+
Dodgeballs
• Setup area as shown and split into two teams. Remind students of your social 
distancing protocols
• One team of students are the rollers and the other students are the runners
• Rollers start from predetermined spot as deiced by the teacher
• On “go” the rolling team counts to 5, while the runners run away in a straight line
• At “5” the runners must completely freeze and cannot move 
• The roller rolls a ball along the ground aiming to hit a runners feet
• If hit, the roller scores a point for their team
• Rotate so each students gets a roll and then switch team roles
Increase or decrease second 
before yelling freeze
Increase or decrease space 
of playing area
Running team start closer or 
further from rolling team
Have all rollers roll at the same 
time
Students maintain 6 feet of 
distance at all times
If possible, each student 
should retain their own ball
Sanitize balls before and after 
use

fiches.title: High Five
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
High Five
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Throwing
2+
Frisbee
Cones
Large ball
• Set up area as shown, placing five pylons one meter apart in a horizontal line. Remind students 
of your social distancing protocol.
• Students select a throwing line that is a challenging distance from the tall Cones
• On top of each tall Cone, place an object as a target, each worth a set number of points as 
decided by the group. For example, a beach ball may be worth 1 point, a basketball 2 points, 
a frisbee 3 points, a beanbag 4 points, and a tennis ball 5 points.
• Each student has 3 attempts using an underhand throw to send a ball from behind the 
throwing line at the targets.
• If a student knocks the target off the tall Cone, he or she counts the number of points that 
target is worth. Each student takes turns throwing and tries to accumulate as many points as 
possible during his/her turn.
• If a target is knocked down, a student resets the target before the next student throws.

• Set up area as shown, placing five pylons one meter apart in a horizontal line. Remind students
of your social distancing protocol.
• Students select a throwing line that is a challenging distance from the tall Cones
• On top of each tall Cone, place an object as a target, each worth a set number of points as
decided by the group. For example, a beach ball may be worth 1 point, a basketball 2 points,
a frisbee 3 points, a beanbag 4 points, and a tennis ball 5 points.
• Each student has 3 attempts using an underhand throw to send a ball from behind the
throwing line at the targets.
• If a student knocks the target off the tall Cone, he or she counts the number of points that
target is worth. Each student takes turns throwing and tries to accumulate as many points as
possible during his/her turn.
• If a target is knocked down, a student resets the target before the next student throws.
Beanbags
Basketball
Tennis ball

throw

tennis ball etc

the ball e.g kick, roll, slide etc

fiches.title: Traffic Lights
fiches.body:
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
SAFETY
Dribbling
4+
Cones
Balls
• Setup playing area as shown. Remind students of your social distancing protocols. 
• Each student receives a ball and dribbles in the playing area
• Students try and avoid bumping into other players and must stay 6+ feet apart at 
all times. If the students come within 6 feet or touch, it’s a “traffic accident” - 
students must report to “driving school” by dribbling one lap around the outside of 
the playing area before re-joining the game.
• Teacher chooses one of the following commands 
Red light: Student stops dribbling and traps ball
Orange light: Student dribbles while walking or jogging
Green light: Dribble as fast as they can while under control of the ball
If you don’t have enough 
soccer balls to go around, 
you can mix up the 
equipment as needed!

basketball, others bounce a 
tennis ball on a racket, 
others stickhandle with a 
hockey stick etc.
Students maintain 6 feet of 
distance at all times
Each student should have 
their own ball
Sanitize balls before and after

fiches.title: Zone Kickball
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
• Setup playing area as shown. Remind students of your social distancing protocols. 
• Split group into a kicking team and a fielding team. The fielding team are assigned “zones” 
using yellow cones. To maintain distance, fielders are not allowed to chase the ball outside 
of their zone.
• Play begins with a pitcher softly rolling the ball towards the kicker
• The kicker aims to kick the ball into the field of play and run around the orange cone and 
back. Each complete run back to home base is worth 1 point. 
• The fielders aim to either catch the ball in the air, or get the ball back to the pitcher while 
the batter is between the orange cone and home base. These both result in the kicker 
being called out. 
• Play until 3 outs, or until everyone on the kicking team has had a try
8+

Balls

fiches.title: Get Off My Lawn
fiches.body:
4+
Cones
Balls
Striking 
Kicking
• Setup playing area as shown. Remind students of your social distancing protocols. 
• Divide students into two equal teams. Within each team, each student has a 
square roughly 10 feet by 10 feet. This is their “lawn”
• Add one or more soccer balls to each student’s lawn
• On teachers command, students attempt to kick all the balls out of their lawn and 
into the opposing team’s lawn
• The greyed out area is the ‘neutral zone’ and only the coach can retrieve the ball. 
If a ball goes out of bounds, students can retrieve and bring back in
• Play for 2 mins. Winning team is the team that has the least amount of balls in their 
lawn.
Use whatever balls you have on 
hand to play this game! Add in 
tennis balls, beach balls, utility 
balls etc.

like hockey sticks or tennis 
rackets instead of kicking

• Switch defender every 2 minutes

• Students create their own scoring criteria for each hoop
• Students rotate after each throw
Hoops
Pool Noodles
Cones
sections.Safety:
Students maintain 6 feet of
distance at all times
Each student should have
their own pool noodle
Sanitize noodles before and
after use

fiches.title: Teacher  Teacher  Instructions Instructions
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Welcome to Superhero Fitness Stations!
This lesson kit includes 19 printable station cards that feature fun, original superhero and supervillain 
characters.
The great thing about station work is the freedom of students to explore various levels of skill with little 
anxiety. Kids who are new to a sport or skill do not feel pressure of having the entire class watch them 
perform. At the same time, the more experienced athletes can challenge themselves and polish their skills.
Equipment
This is an equipment-free activity. All you need is a group of kids and a space to play (inside or outside).
Setup guide
1.	
Print and laminate each of the station posters.
2.	 Decide which posters you will be using and how long you will have the kids stay at a station before 
rotating (we recommend 1-3 minutes).
3.	 Set up your stations by attaching each poster to the wall or to a tall cone. You can do these stations 
inside a gym, or outside on a field or blacktop. Spread your stations out so students will have enough 
space to perform the task safely.
4.	 Introduce your students to each station. Teach them how to do the exercise, explain how long they will 
spend at each station and where they will rotate next.
5.	 Place the students in even groups and divide them up between the stations.
6.	 Blow your whistle to have the students begin. Put on some fun, upbeat music. Walk around to answer 
questions and help students complete the activities.
7.	 After the set time has elapsed, blow the whistle again and have the students rotate to the next station. 
Continue for the duration of your session.
Teacher

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE Tag Games! 
In this resource, we’ve provided 16 fun tag-based games you can use for PE class, recess, field 
days, summer camps, or after school sports programs. 
Each game is explained with step by step instructions and rules for running the game. Along with 
a detailed diagram and equipment list showing you exactly how to set up the space. The 
activities are appropriate for Kindergarten through 8th grade students, and can be done with 
small groups or large groups, indoors or outdoors. 
Let’s get started!

fiches.title: Capture the Flag
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Capture the Flag
x 6
beanbags
Cones
1.
Teams start in their own half, the aim is to capture the flags/beanbags and bring them back to their side. 
2.
When travelling on the other teams side, if you are tagged you must go to jail.  
3.
Players can be released from jail by a team mate making it to them, if so you can walk safely back to 
your side. 
4.
If players make it to the other teams flag area (inside cones) you cannot be tagged, they will try and get 
the flag/beanbag back to their side. 
5.
If tagged on the way back, the player must return the flag/beanbag 
6.
if a team captures all the flags, the game is over and start a new game.  
7.
After 5 or so minutes, if the game is still going the team with the most flags/beanbags are the winners. 
Setup

fiches.title: Toilet Tag
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Toilet Tag
Pool Noodles
1.
Designate playing area, players must stay in boundaries 
2.
Choose 3-4 students to become ‘toilet makers’ and give them a pool noodle to tag students with. 
3.
The toilet makers try to tag students with a pool noodle. If so, they turn into a ‘toilet’. They become a toilet by 
kneeling down on one knee and raising the other to make a ‘seat’ 
4.
To be released another person who has not been tagged, must sit on their seat and pull the chain (arm raised) 
5.
Toilet makers cannot tag students sitting on a toilet. 
6.
The game can be played for a set time or until there are a few players left. 
Setup

fiches.title: Freeze and Melt
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Freeze and Melt
2 x Red and 4 x Blue Pool Noodles
1.
Arrange playing area as shown, players must stay in boundaries 
2.
Designate 4 ‘freezers’ (who have the blue pool noodles) and 2 ‘melters’ (who have the red pool 
noodles) 
3.
On teachers command the freezers try and tag other students (except melters). If a student is 
tagged the must ‘freeze’. They can only be unfrozen by the ‘melters’ 
4.
The melters run around and try to unfreeze as many students that are frozen as possible.  
5.
Play for a designated time period or until there are only a few students left unfrozen. 
Setup

fiches.title: Electricity Tag
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Electricity Tag
x 4
4 colored sets of ﬂagbelts
1.
Split group into 4 teams and give each student a pair of flagbelts, one color per team 
2.
The object is to steal the flagbelts of opposing teams hips and take them to your ‘power station’ (hoop). No body contact 
allowed.  
3.
 Players must not protect or hold their own flagbelts with their hands. 
4.
Each team has a ‘regenerator’. This player does not have flag belts and simply recollects their flags from other teams hoops 
and places them back in their power station.  
5.
Once both flags have been taken, players must collect new flags from their power station or wait until the regenerator has 
returned 2 flags.  
6.
Play for a designated time period, winning team is the team that has the most opposing flags in their power station
Setup

fiches.title: Pacman
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Pacman
T
T
T = Taggers
No Equipment needed except court with line markings
1.
Choose 4-5 taggers (ghosts), the rest are Pacmen 
2.
Ghosts and Pacmen must stay on lines whilst running with no cutting of corners 
3.
if tagged, a pacman is  not out nut must simply perform 5 star jumps (off of a line) and then return to the game.  
4.
If whistle is blown the roles reverse and pacmen can tag the ghosts (10 seconds only), if tagged a ghost must 
perform 10 star jumps 
5.
Rotate roles and locomotion frequently
Setup

fiches.title: Crows and Cranes
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Crows and Cranes
Cones
1.
Students partner up: One is a ‘crow’ and one is a ‘crane’ 
2.
The face each other, one behind the blue Cone, one behind the red Cone 
3.
Teacher call either ‘crows or ‘cranes’. That player must turn and run for their home Cone and 
reach it before being tagged by their opponent. Score a point for a tag or getting to your home 
Cone.  
4.
Mix up the locomotion and starting position frequently
HOME
HOME
Setup

fiches.title: Octopus
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Octopus
Setup
Tagger
Tagger
Cones to design playing area
Students (Fish) line up on one end. Choose 3-4 people to start as ‘taggers’ (Octopus). The aim is to 
reach the other side without being tagged by one of the ‘taggers’. if you are you turn into 
‘seaweed’ where you were tagged and go to your knees. Seaweeds can tag fish, but cannot run. 
Last student left is the winner.

fiches.title: Helicopters
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Helicopters
Setup
4 diﬀerent sets of colored Cones
pool noodles for taggers
In this game have 2- 4 taggers who have pool noodles. All other students are in a colored 
helicopter pod (Coneed areas). The teachers calls a color and those students have to run, skip 
etc (mix up the locomotor) to another helicopter pod without getting tagged. If they do get 
tagged they must complete 10 star jumps and then head to a color of their choice. Mix up the 
taggers and locomotor regularly.

pool noodles for taggers

fiches.title: Cops and Robbers
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Cops and Robbers
Setup
4 diﬀerent sets of colored Cones
pool noodles for taggers
In this game have 2 end zones marked out as jails. The aim is for the taggers to send dodgers 
into the jail by tagging them. If a dodger is tagged with a pool noodle they must immediately head 
to the closest jail. They can be released 3 ways. Either completing a designated exercise/s, having 
another dodger tag them, or by rolling a ‘six’ on a dice (large exercise dice work best). Have a dice 
at each end. 
JAIL
JAIL
Dice x 2

fiches.title: TAils
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
TAils
Setup large playing area. Each student attaches 2 flagbelts to their waist (or a bib/pinnie tucked into their 
waistband on either side). The aim is to steal other students flagbelts whilst keeping your own. Students cannot 
make body contact and cannot protect their flags with their hands. After a flagbelt is ‘stolen’ the ‘stealer’ 
throws it on the ground. If both or your tags have been stolen you do a lap of the playing area and come back in 
and pick up two flags off of the ground and play again. The game is continuous but you can add points for every 
flag that a students ‘steals’ to determine a winner. 
x 2 ﬂagbelts for each student (colors don’t matter)

fiches.title: Dead ants
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Dead ants
Setup a large playing area. Choose 4 taggers who start with pool noodles, the rest are ‘ants’. When an ant is 
tagged they become a ‘dead ant’ and lie down waving their arms and legs. 4 ants can carry a dead ant to the 
medical bay at either by holding an arm and a leg each, whilst carrying a dead ant, students cannot be tagged by 
the taggers. Once in the medical bay ants have been revived and may rejoin the game. Taggers cannot go into the 
‘medical bays’ Variations: add more or less taggers, medical bays and change locomotion. 
Pool Noodles
Dead ant
Hula Hoops

fiches.title: The Wall
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
The Wall
The Wall
T
T = Tagger
T
Make a large playing area. Choose 2-3 students to be taggers who are stationed inside ‘the wall’. The aim is for 
students to avoid getting tagged inside ‘the wall’. Taggers must only tag students inside the wall, any tag outside 
the wall does not count and the students continues to the end. If a student is tagged inside the wall they become 
taggers. Mix up the locomotor often and emphasise safety and awareness. To make it easier, have taggers that 
are inside the wall pivot only.

The Wall
T
T = Tagger
T

fiches.title: Spitfires and Bombers
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Spitfires and Bombers
Make a large playing area. Choose 2 students who are the spitfires, the rest of the class are the bombers and 
find a partners and hold hands. On Go, the spitfires try and tag the bombers, if tagged the bombers must form a 
‘hanger’ and join hands above their head creating an arch. To be released another pair of bombers must run 
through the arch. Spitfires cannot tag a pair of bombers inside an arch and cannot wait for them to exit. 
Variation: Change up the locomotor, have more spitfires or change the size of the playing area.  
x2 Spitﬁres
Bombers

fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Dice TAg
Assorted Colored hula hoops
Dice Key
1
2
3
4
5
Exercise of 
teacher choice
6
Teacher is it
Pool Noodles
Dice
Scatter 4 different colored hula hoops throughout the area, each student stands in one. Teacher rolls the dice, if 
the number lands on 2 (see they for associated numbers) then all students in the red hula hoops become taggers 
and collect a pool noodle. All other students leave the hula hoop and try and dodge the taggers. If a student is 
tagged they go ta hula hoop of their choice until the time is up (1 min) or everyone is tagged and wait for the next 
round.

fiches.title: Mat TAg
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Mat TAg
Setup large playing area. Each team is designated a mat. All teams start on their mat. One team is the 
‘tagging team’ and wears bibs/pinnies. On teachers command the students all leave their mats and try to 
dodge the taggers. If a student is tagged they must return to their mat. The last team to have a team 
member out still playing is the winner. After each round, change tagging team. Variations: Change locomotor, 
students have to perform an exercise once tagged on their mat and come back in to make it an endless or 
time limited game. 
4 x mats or marked oﬀ areas to designate teams

T

fiches.title: Frogger
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Setup
Frogger
Setup a large playing area. Choose 3-4 students who are the taggers, the rest of the students scatter 
throughout the area. On teachers command, the taggers try and tag the there other students, if tagged 
the student must crouch down like a ‘frog’. Frogs can be released by students leapfrogging them (using the 
shoulder or a clean jump). A student cannot be tagged if they are releasing a ‘frog’ and have their hands on 
the shoulders of the frog. Variations: Mixup the locomotor, have less or more taggers, increase or 
decrease the playing area.
= Taggers

fiches.title: Introduction
fiches.body:
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com
Introduction
Welcome to Powerhouse PE - Volleyball & Net Games! 
Inside this resource, you’ll get a selection of fun activities you can use with Kindergarten through 8th Grade. Many of the 
games are volleyball-based, but can be played on any type of court with a net separating competitors. Each activity is 
explained with simple instructions, equipment lists, diagrams and modifications. 
Skill Focus 
Each activity will have a skill focus, based on one of the fundamental skills of racket sports. 
Group Size 
All of these activities are designed to work with smaller or larger groups. A group size is provided, but this is just the 
minimum number of students you will need for the game to work. For larger groups, you can increase the number of 
players in the game, or create multiple stations with smaller groups. 
Complexity 
Each activity is rated on a Complexity scale indicating how difficult the skills are to perform. 
  Green - recommended for Kindergarten and up (or with modifications for older children) 
  Yellow - recommended for 3rd grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
  Red - recommended for 5th grade and up (with modifications for younger or older children) 
Modifications 
We’ve also included modifications to make the games easier or harder, for different age levels. 
Ask Students 
Some games also include review questions you can ask your students - allowing you to reinforce key coaching points, 
rules or skills.

Skill Focus
Group Size

Why is serving important in 
net and court games? 
How can you improve the 
accuracy of your serve/
throw? 
8
• Split students into teams of 4 
• Start the game with students from one team each having a volleyball (or similar) 
aiming to serve/throw over the net and land a ball in a hoop off of the first bounce 
• If successful, the students crosses to other side, picks up the hula hoop and removes 
it from the game 
• The opposition team then get a ball each and serve/throw over the trying to do the 
same, the team that just served head to the back of their side of the court 
• The first team to remove all the hoops or the team that has the least when the time 
expires are the winners

fiches.title: Rebound Ball
fiches.body:
Rebound Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
What strategy did you use 
to score points? 
Why is it important to move 
your opponent around in 
net and court games?
2
• Students form groups of 2 and play in area approx 5 metres x 5 metres 
• The aim of the to throw the ball against the rebound net and have the ball hit the 
ground before your opponents can catch it 
• Players can use either an underarm or overarm throw 
• If the ball lands out out bounds on the full a point is awarded to the non thrower 
• Players must throw and move out of the way, if the thrower is in the way of their 
opponent, players replay the point 
• If the throwers ball lands before the front line or misses the net, then a point is 
awarded to the catcher 
• Play to 11 points or a set time period 
The ball can bounce 
once or more before 
catching 
Students can use different 
balls e.g gator ball, 
dodgeball, tennis ball etc 
Increase or decrease the 
size of the paying area 
Play as doubles 
Markers
Balls
Rebound Nets

Defending Space
Creating an attack
Front line
Front line

fiches.body:
Dot Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Court Position
How can you make it more 
difficult for your opponent 
to receive the ball? 
How can you prevent your 
opponent from scoring a 
point?
2
• Students pair up and play in an area the size of a small volleyball 
court or badminton court

over the net higher than the opponents height landing inside the

• Points are won by: having the ball bounce twice in your opponent’s

opponents side, opponent throwing the ball out of bounds

serve

balls, shuttles

fiches.title: Stockpile
fiches.body:
Stockpile
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Court Position
How can you make it more 
difficult for your opponent 
to receive the ball? 
How can you prevent your 
opponent from catching a 
ball?
10
• Students form teams of approx 5 on either side of a volleyball/tennis/
badminton net 
• The aim is to throw a ball over the net and have it hit the floor inside the 
playing area 
• Any balls that are on the floor can be picked up and thrown back over 
• If a team catches a ball, they put it in their ‘pile’ at the back of the court 
• If the opposition throws out of bounds on the full, teams can add that ball to 
their pile 
• The team with the most balls in their pile at the end of a designated time is the 
winner
Increase or decrease the 
size of the court 
Increase or decrease the 
amount of players per 
team

e.g tennis balls, bouncy 
balls, shuttles

fiches.title: Newcombe Ball
fiches.body:
Newcombe Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
How can you avoid too 
many players in one area? 
Where might you throw/hit 
the ball to make it harder 
for your opponents?
8
• The game starts with one player tossing the ball from the back of the court 
across the net for an opponent to catch 
• Each player must stay within their area 
• A maximum of 3 passes can be made between payers before the ball is

fiches.title: Bound Ball
fiches.body:
Bound Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
How can you avoid too 
many players in one area? 
Where might you throw/hit 
the ball to make it harder 
for your opponents?
8
• Split students in to teams of 4 and play without a net (use markers or poly spots as 
the net) 
• The game starts with one player underhand serving the ball from the back of the 
court 
• Students must let the ball bounce once before attempting to dig, set or spike.  
• When passing to a team mate, the ball must also bounce once 
• Teams have a maximum of 3 hits before the ball must be hit over the dividing line 
• If the ball bounces twice, an opponent hits the ball on the full or the ball is played 
out of bounds a point is awarded to the other team 
• Play to 11 points or a set time limit

fiches.body:
1 vs 1 Volleyball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
Court Position
What are the key points of 
the dig/set? 
How can you work 
cooperatively with your 
partner to build a higher 
score?
3
• Students for groups of 3. Two players and one who holds a skipping rope 
above the head to act as a ‘net’ 
• Students work cooperatively to try and perform as many digs or sets over the 
‘net’ as possible, students can perform digs/sets to themselves before hitting 
the ball over. 
• Rotate every 2 minutes or when the ‘net holder’ needs a break.  
• No spiking is allowed 
The ball can bounce 
once or more before

fiches.title: Planet Ball
fiches.body:
Planet Ball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
How can you work 
cooperatively with your 
team to play effectively?
Whole Class
• Split class into four even groups 
• The aim of the game is to work with your team mates to push the beachball 
(meteorite) over the net, in to another teams zone (planet) 
• If the beachball hits the floor in your zone (planet) then the coach/teacher 
throws a marker (comet) into your area (frisbee style), if you catch it you put it 
back in the coach pile, if it hits the floor you place it in your teams hula hoop 
(space station) 
• The team with the least amount of markers (comets) in their space station is 
the winner
After a lost point, students 
collect a marker (comet) 
from the coach pile and 
place it in the teams 
space station 
Set a hit limit to get the 
ball over the net 
Add another beachball 
(meteorite) 
Beachball
Hoops
Court Position
Defending Space
Creating an attack
Markers
© AmericanCoachingAcademy.com

fiches.title: 4 Square Volleyball
fiches.body:
4 Square Volleyball
Skill Focus
Group Size
Complexity
Equipment
Modify it
Ask STudents
How do you hit the ball 
away from your opponent? 
Where can you put the ball 
so it is difficult fro your 
opponent to get it back?
8
• Students partner up to form a group of two as shown below 
• Split the court into 4 zones separated by either 2 nets or Cones 
• The game starts with the team in zone 1 performing a serve into any 
other zone 
• Teams can hit the ball into any zone 
• If the ball hits the ground in a teams zone, they move to zone 4.  
• Everybody else moves up a zone. 
Use different striking 
instruments e.g hand, 
different racket etc 
Use different shaped balls 
e.g tennis balls, bouncy 
balls, shuttles 
Teams score a point for 
every rally they win in 
Zone 1. The team with the 
most points wins 
Balls
Cones
Zone 2
Zone 1
Zone 3
Court Position
Defending Space
Creating an attack
Zone 4
